This will be received by international climate finance bodies as 'improved but insufficient,' which will sustain India's 'Highly Insufficient' CAT rating and create a persistent sovereign climate credibility discount that institutional ESG frameworks will price into Indian corporate ratings. Second, the EU's CBAM definitive phase beginning January 2026 will accelerate a reckoning for Indian heavy industry: companies in steel, cement, and fertilisers that have not invested in transition credibility — measured partly through NDC alignment and SBTi commitments — will face compounding disadvantage in EU export markets just as domestic BEE PAT Cycle IX tightens energy norms post-2026. The companies that are already running 1.5°C scenario analysis, have third-party assured BRSR Core data, and have initiated SBTi target-setting will be able to demonstrate transition readiness to both regulators and capital providers. Those that have not will face the twin pressure of higher cost of capital and shrinking export market access simultaneously. Third, and most importantly for boards: MCA's sustainability reporting standards, expected for consultation in 2025 and aligned with ISSB IFRS S1/S2, will require Indian listed companies to disclose transition plans — which are, by definition, anchored to government climate policy. A company whose transition plan references India's NDC as its policy context will need that NDC to be credible, quantified, and sector-specific to withstand audit scrutiny and investor challenge. The boards and CFOs who are building their transition plans now, using the 1.5°C-aligned sector benchmarks that Climate Action Tracker has quantified, are not just doing good ESG — they are building audit-defensible, investor-grade climate disclosures ahead of a regulatory wave that will arrive whether India's NDC is bold or timid. The gap between action and ambition is not just a climate problem. For Indian business in 2025, it is a capital markets problem.

posts.title: Fossil Fuel Export Emissions: The Accountability Gap That Threatens India's Climate Commitments and Supply Chain Credibility
posts.summary: Climate Action Tracker's latest analysis exposes a structural accountability gap in global climate governance: the top fossil fuel exporting nations — Russia, Australia, the US, Indonesia, Saudi Arabia, and Canada — account for 50% of total exported fossil fuel emissions, yet these emissions appear

sections.summary: Climate Action Tracker's latest analysis exposes a structural accountability gap in global climate governance: the top fossil fuel exporting nations — Russia, Australia, the US, Indonesia, Saudi Arabia, and Canada — account for 50% of total exported fossil fuel emissions, yet these emissions appear in no national inventory and no corporate Scope 3 accounting obligation. For India, this is not an abstract geopolitical debate. India is one of the world's largest importers of coal (from Indonesia and Australia) and oil (from Russia and Saudi Arabia), meaning the 'exported emissions' of these nations are India's Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions in waiting — embedded in the energy and industrial base of every BSE 500 company. The immediate consequence for Indian CFOs and CSOs is a compounding disclosure risk: as ISSB IFRS S2 adoption accelerates globally and SEBI's BRSR Core Scope 3 disclosures tighten, the combustion-side emissions of imported fossil fuels will become a mandatory reporting obligation — exposing Indian corporates to stranded asset risk, SBTi target misalignment, and investor scrutiny that their export-nation counterparts are currently escaping.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/highlighting-the-hypocrisy-fossil-fuel-export-emissions/), authored by Sofia Gonzales-Zuniga, Danial Riaz, and Mia Moisio, quantifies for the first time the scale of what CAT terms the 'fossil fuel exports iceberg effect': countries like Norway and Australia have exported emissions that exceed their entire domestic greenhouse gas inventory. Australia's exported emissions are more than double its domestic footprint. Canada has had exported emissions exceeding domestic emissions since 2015. The US, meanwhile, has significantly grown its exported emissions over the past decade and — per US EIA 2023 projections — plans to increase oil and gas exports to 2050. What this analysis overturns is the comfortable diplomatic fiction that COP28's 'transition away from fossil fuels' language represents a coherent global decarbonisation pathway. It does not. The GHG Protocol's territorial accounting boundary means that Scope 1 emissions from Australian coal combustion in an Indian steel plant appear in India's national inventory and in Indian steelmakers' Scope 1 disclosures — not in Australia's. This is not merely a moral asymmetry; it is a live regulatory and capital market risk for Indian industry. Under SEBI's BRSR Core framework, top 150 listed entities (by market capitalisation) are already required to disclose Scope 3 emissions on a reasonable assurance basis for FY2024-25 onward. For energy-intensive sectors — steel, cement, power, chemicals — Scope 3 Category 1 (purchased goods and services) and Category 3 (fuel and energy-related activities) will directly capture the embedded emissions of imported coal, LNG, and crude oil. The CAT analysis makes clear that the upstream supply of these fuels is not being decarbonised: Indian companies cannot rely on supplier-side action to reduce their Scope 3 numbers. The India-specific regulatory hook deepens further: India's Perform Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme under the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) sets specific energy consumption reduction targets for designated consumers, many of whom are large importers of thermal coal and fuel oil. If the PAT scheme evolves — as expected under India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) notified in June 2023 — into a carbon price-linked offset mechanism, the emissions intensity of imported fuels will directly affect the cost of compliance credits. Companies importing high-emission-intensity coal from Australia or Indonesia will face structurally higher compliance costs compared to peers transitioning to domestic renewable energy or cleaner fuel mixes.
who_is_affected: Indian steel manufacturers (primary steel via blast furnace route) importing coking and thermal coal from Australia and Indonesia — direct Scope 1 and Scope 3 Category 1 exposure

Indian power generation companies (thermal IPPs and state DISCOMs) relying on imported coal from Indonesia, South Africa, and Australia for capacity balancing — exposed to fuel cost volatility and future carbon border adjustments

Indian oil refiners and petrochemical companies importing Russian crude under current pricing arrangements — Scope 3 Category 3 exposure and potential EU CBAM indirect risk if refined products are exported to European markets

Top 150 SEBI-listed companies by market cap required to disclose Scope 3 under BRSR Core from FY2024-25 — audit committees face assurance risk if upstream emission factors for imported fossil fuels are not accurately sourced
CFOs and treasury heads at energy-intensive BSE 500 companies negotiating long-term fuel supply contracts — need to price in forward carbon cost of imported fossil fuel emissions

CSOs and sustainability heads at Indian conglomerates with SBTi near-term or net-zero commitments — Scope 3 Category 1 and 3 targets will be unachievable without a credible imported fuel decarbonisation pathway

ESG analysts and institutional investors at domestic mutual funds, foreign portfolio investors (FPIs), and development finance institutions (DFIs) with exposure to Indian energy and industrials — need to reassess Scope 3 transition risk embedded in portfolio companies

Export-oriented Indian manufacturers (textiles, auto components, chemicals) supplying EU buyers subject to CSRD — upstream Scope 3 disclosure requirements from EU customers will cascade to Indian suppliers' own fuel and energy emission disclosures
key_obligations: SEBI BRSR Core — Scope 3 disclosure (all 15 GHG Protocol categories) required with reasonable assurance for top 150 listed companies from FY2024-25 reporting (annual reports due by September 2025)
SEBI BRSR Core — Scope 3 disclosure extended to top 1,000 listed companies from FY2026-27; companies must begin baseline data collection and supply chain emission factor mapping now

India Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS), notified June 2023 under Energy Conservation Act 2001 — BEE to operationalise compliance market by FY2025-26; emission intensity of imported fossil fuels will affect PAT cycle 7 and CCTS compliance obligations

ISSB IFRS S2 — material Scope 3 emissions including Category 1 (purchased goods) and Category 3 (fuel and energy) required in climate disclosures; Indian subsidiaries of multinationals or Indian companies with international listings face ISSB-aligned reporting obligations from financial years beginning January 2024 onward in early-adopter jurisdictions

EU CBAM — full phase-in from January 2026 applies to cement, steel, aluminium, fertilisers, hydrogen, and electricity; Indian exporters in these sectors must verify and disclose embedded carbon content including from imported fossil fuel inputs to avoid CBAM surcharges at EU border

SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard — companies with validated near-term targets must reduce absolute Scope 3 emissions 42% by 2030 from base year; Indian companies with SBTi commitments that source significant energy from imported coal face a structural compliance gap if supply-side decarbonisation by exporting nations does not materialise

CDP Supply Chain Programme — Indian suppliers to global multinationals responding to CDP Supply Chain questionnaire must disclose Scope 3 Category 1 and 3 data; non-disclosure increasingly results in supplier delisting from procurement shortlists
sections.climate_angle: The CAT analysis fundamentally reframes the physical and transition risk calculus for Indian industry. India sits at the intersection of two colliding forces: it is simultaneously the world's third-largest coal consumer and one of the most climate-vulnerable major economies. The continued — and in some cases growing — fossil fuel export pipelines from Australia, Indonesia, Russia, and the US mean that the feedstock of India's existing industrial base will remain available and price-competitive for at least another decade. This creates a classic carbon lock-in dynamic: Indian steel and power companies can continue to access imported coal cheaply, reducing the near-term economic incentive to accelerate the transition, while simultaneously accumulating transition risk in their balance sheets as global carbon pricing and border adjustment mechanisms tighten. Under ISSB IFRS S2 — which India's MCA and SEBI are watching closely as the foundation for future mandatory climate disclosure standards — companies are required to disclose climate-related risks across the full value chain, including the transition risks associated with carbon-intensive purchased inputs. For Indian steelmakers using Australian metallurgical coal, this means disclosing the scenario analysis implications of a world where Australian coal exports face carbon pricing, export levies, or shipping emission costs under IMO's 2023 GHG Strategy (targeting net-zero shipping by or around 2050). The physical risk dimension is equally acute: climate impacts on Australian and Indonesian coal export infrastructure — cyclones, flooding of mine access roads, port disruptions — are live supply chain risks that BRSR Core's value chain risk disclosures now require Indian companies to assess. India's Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC), updated in 2022, targets 45% reduction in emissions intensity of GDP by 2030 and 500 GW of non-fossil electricity capacity. These targets are achievable in principle, but the CAT analysis illustrates that exporting nations are not pricing the transition cost into their export economics. India's policymakers at MoEFCC and NITI Aayog must now grapple with the reality that the 'transition away' language of COP28 will not be operationalised by exporting nations voluntarily — meaning India's own industrial transition timeline cannot be calibrated on the assumption of supply-side tightening of imported fossil fuels. This has direct implications for BEE's energy efficiency roadmaps and MoEFCC's Long-Term Low Emission Development Strategy (LT-LEDS) submitted to UNFCCC in 2022.
what_to_do: CFO / Head of Sustainability — within 60 days: commission a Scope 3 Category 1 and 3 materiality assessment using GHG Protocol Scope 3 Calculation Guidance; map all imported fossil fuel volumes (coal, LNG, crude oil, fuel oil) to country-of-origin emission factors to build BRSR Core-ready disclosure datasets ahead of FY2024-25 annual report filing

Procurement and supply chain teams — within 90 days: engage key fossil fuel commodity suppliers (coal traders, LNG off-takers, crude oil procurement desks) to obtain primary emission intensity data per GJ of fuel delivered; this is required for credible Scope 3 Category 1 supplier engagement under SBTi's Supplier Engagement Rating and CDP Supply Chain scoring

Head of Strategy / CSO — within 6 months: conduct a scenario analysis stress-test under IEA Net Zero by 2050 and IEA Announced Pledges Scenario for the price and availability of imported coal and LNG through 2035; present findings to board risk committee as a climate transition risk disclosure for BRSR Core and any TCFD-aligned investor reports

Company Secretary / Audit Committee — before FY2024-25 assurance engagement: brief statutory auditors and ESG assurance providers on the Scope 3 boundary, specifically the treatment of imported fossil fuel emission factors; confirm that the assurance methodology aligns with ISAE 3000 or equivalent and that country-of-origin emission intensity data sources are defensible under SEBI's BRSR Core reasonable assurance requirement

Board Risk Committee — at next quarterly meeting: table the CAT analysis alongside India's CCTS roadmap and EU CBAM phase-in timeline as connected transition risk triggers; request management to disclose what percentage of current Scope 1 emissions are dependent on imported fossil fuels and what the forward carbon cost exposure is under a ₹1,000/tonne CO2 and ₹3,000/tonne CO2 scenario

Investor Relations / ESG team — within 3 months: proactively update institutional investor and FPI engagement decks to address Scope 3 Category 1 and 3 exposure from imported fossil fuels; anticipate specific questions from UNPRI signatories and Climate Action 100+ members about alignment with SBTi and ISSB S2 disclosure on value chain emissions
sections.our_take: The CAT analysis is important not because it reveals something unknown — the accounting blind spot for exported fossil fuel emissions has been a feature of the UNFCCC architecture since Kyoto — but because it arrives at a moment when the capital market and regulatory infrastructure to act on this knowledge is finally maturing. ISSB IFRS S2's Scope 3 requirements, SEBI's BRSR Core reasonable assurance mandate, EU CBAM's embedded carbon methodology, and SBTi's Scope 3 absolute reduction targets are collectively creating a regulatory pincer movement that will, within five years, make the 'exported emissions are not my problem' position untenable for Indian listed companies. The exporting nations — Australia, Indonesia, the US, Russia, Saudi Arabia — are not going to resolve this through voluntary action. That means the accountability will migrate downstream, to the importers, combustors, and disclosers: Indian industry. What boards should watch over the next 24 to 36 months: first, expect IOSCO and IESBA to push for mandatory Scope 3 assurance standards that close the current gap between Scope 1/2 (limited assurance becoming reasonable assurance) and Scope 3 (largely unassured today) — this will change the cost and risk profile of Scope 3 non-disclosure overnight. Second, watch for India's CCTS to evolve from a sectoral intensity-based scheme to an absolute cap mechanism as India approaches its 2070 net-zero commitment year; at that point, the carbon cost of imported fossil fuels will be explicitly priced into Indian compliance markets. Third, anticipate that the EU CBAM scope — currently covering six sectors — will expand to cover chemicals, paper, and glass by 2030; Indian exporters in these sectors who have not built imported fossil fuel emission tracking into their cost accounting will face sudden and severe competitive disadvantage. The companies that treat the CAT analysis as a procurement, disclosure, and strategy problem today will be structurally advantaged when these regulatory shifts materialise. Those that wait for regulatory compulsion will be managing crisis, not transition.

posts.title: IMO MEPC83 Carbon Levy Negotiations: What India's Shipping Sector, Exporters, and ESG Investors Must Understand Now
posts.summary: The IMO's MEPC83 negotiations in London are finalising the architecture of a global carbon levy and fuel standard for international shipping — a regulatory moment with direct and underappreciated consequences for India's export economy, its shipping and port sectors, and the ESG disclosure obligatio

sections.summary: The IMO's MEPC83 negotiations in London are finalising the architecture of a global carbon levy and fuel standard for international shipping — a regulatory moment with direct and underappreciated consequences for India's export economy, its shipping and port sectors, and the ESG disclosure obligations of listed companies with material ocean freight exposure. Climate Action Tracker rates the IMO's current ambition as 'Highly Insufficient,' consistent with 4°C warming, but even the most ambitious MEPC83 outcome will not align with a 1.5°C pathway — meaning further tightening is structurally inevitable. For Indian CFOs and CSOs, the question is not whether a carbon cost on shipping arrives, but at what price, in what form, and how fast it will cascade into supply chain costs, Scope 3 disclosures, and export competitiveness.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/imo-at-a-crossroads-a-defining-juncture-for-climate-action-and-an-equitable-transition/) details that MEPC83, the 83rd session of IMO's Marine Environment Protection Committee, is currently negotiating two competing frameworks: a universal flat-rate carbon levy — championed by Small Island Developing States (SIDS) and Least Developed Countries (LDCs) — and the 'J9' proposal put forward by Singapore, which offers a two-tiered Global Fuel Standard paired with a credit trading mechanism rather than a direct levy. The EU, previously aligned with the SIDS bloc, has reportedly softened its position and is open to compromise, materially weakening the prospects of the more ambitious levy architecture. The significance of this divergence cannot be overstated for Indian business. International shipping emissions — covering approximately 3% of global GHG emissions, comparable to Germany's entire national footprint — are explicitly excluded from countries' Nationally Determined Contributions under the Paris Agreement. India's own NDC, updated in 2022 and targeting a 45% emissions intensity reduction by 2030 against 2005 levels, does not cover these shipping emissions. This means that whatever the IMO decides at MEPC83 represents the only binding multilateral lever on a segment of India's trade-linked emissions that currently sits entirely outside BRSR Scope 3 reporting norms and India's domestic climate architecture. A carbon levy at IMO level would impose direct cost pressure on Indian importers and exporters — sectors ranging from iron ore, steel, and textiles to pharmaceuticals and chemicals — regardless of India's domestic carbon pricing trajectory under the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) notified under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act, 2022. The J9 alternative introduces a two-tiered GHG intensity standard that, as CAT notes, could make LNG-fuelled vessels more commercially attractive — a serious lock-in risk given India's significant investments in LNG bunkering infrastructure at Paradip, Ennore, and Kochi. If LNG becomes the dominant transitional fuel under a weak IMO standard, Indian port infrastructure capex decisions made in 2025–2027 risk becoming stranded assets by the time the IMO tightens standards in its post-2030 review cycle.
who_is_affected: Indian exporters with high ocean freight dependency: textiles and apparel (Bangladesh-competitive), pharmaceuticals (US/EU export lanes), steel and iron ore (seaborne bulk), chemicals, and auto components — all face direct cost pass-through risk from a carbon levy on shipping

Indian Shipping Corporation (SCI), Shipping Corporation of India Land and Assets Ltd, and private fleet operators: compliance cost exposure, fleet retrofit decisions, and green fuel procurement strategies
Major Indian ports — JNPT, Mundra, Paradip, Vizag — with bunkering infrastructure investment pipelines that will be shaped by which fuel standard the IMO adopts

BSE 500 listed companies with material international shipping in their Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) or Category 9 (downstream transportation) under GHG Protocol — increasingly relevant under BRSR Core's value chain disclosure requirements for the top 250 listed entities
CFOs and treasury heads at export-oriented manufacturers who currently do not price carbon into freight cost modelling or long-term supply chain contracts
CSOs and ESG heads at companies with SBTi-validated or BRSR-disclosed net zero targets who have not yet addressed Scope 3 shipping emissions in their decarbonisation roadmaps
Indian institutional investors and ESG analysts covering shipping, logistics, and export-driven industrials within BSE 500 portfolios
DFIs and green bond issuers financing port infrastructure or green shipping corridors — including India's proposed Green Hydrogen corridors where shipping is a key offtake pathway
key_obligations: MEPC83 negotiations concluding April 2025: outcome will define whether a flat-rate carbon levy or the J9 credit-trading mechanism forms the basis of IMO's mid-term measure — Indian shipping companies and exporters must track the final text immediately

IMO 2023 GHG Reduction Strategy: indicative target of 20% emissions reduction (aspirational 30%) by 2030 from 2008 baseline — any measures agreed at MEPC83 are expected to operationalise this; Indian fleet operators must begin fleet efficiency audits against these benchmarks now

IMO Net Zero by or around 2050 goal: sets the long-term compliance horizon — Indian shipping companies with vessels currently on order or under long-term charter must stress-test against zero-emission fuel compatibility by 2045 at the latest

BRSR Core (applicable to top 1,000 listed entities from FY2024-25): while Scope 3 is not yet mandatorily assured under BRSR Core, SEBI's phased roadmap signals value chain emissions will be in scope — companies with material shipping Scope 3 should begin baselining now to avoid disclosure gaps when SEBI mandates it

EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) — steel, aluminium, fertilisers, cement, hydrogen: live from October 2023 for reporting, levy from 2026 — embedded carbon in sea freight is not yet in CBAM scope but EU's shipping ETS (effective January 2024) already adds carbon cost to vessels calling EU ports, directly impacting Indian exporters to Europe

EU Shipping ETS (EU ETS Phase 4, effective 1 January 2024): 100% of emissions from intra-EU voyages and 50% of emissions from voyages to/from non-EU ports now require ETS allowances — Indian exporters to Europe face freight rate increases of €50–€100/tonne CO2 equivalent depending on vessel type; this is operational today, not theoretical

India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) under Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022: currently covers energy-intensive domestic industries — international shipping is outside scope, but the Ministry of Power's roadmap for CCTS expansion could eventually intersect with IMO compliance mechanisms for Indian-flagged vessels
sections.climate_angle: India sits in a structurally complex position in IMO negotiations. As both a major maritime nation (ranking among the top 15 in flag state tonnage and a significant ship-owning nation through SCI and private operators) and a large developing economy with freight-intensive export growth aspirations, India's negotiating posture has historically leaned toward the J9-style flexibility architecture rather than the flat levy. This is commercially understandable but strategically shortsighted from a transition finance perspective. The CAT analysis makes clear that the flat levy — with revenues directed to climate-vulnerable nations — is both the most cost-effective long-term solution (per UNCTAD's own Comprehensive Impact Assessment) and the most equitable. India, as a country that repeatedly and rightly calls for climate finance flows from developed to developing nations, should recognise that a robust carbon levy on shipping is one of the only multilateral mechanisms currently on the table capable of generating dedicated, predictable climate finance outside the UNFCCC's perennially underfunded channels. From a transition pathway perspective, the J9's LNG-tolerance creates a direct contradiction with India's own Green Hydrogen Mission and its ambition to build green ammonia and green hydrogen export corridors — both of which require zero-emission shipping as the offtake pathway to be commercially viable by 2030. If IMO locks in an LNG-compatible standard at MEPC83, the commercial case for Indian green hydrogen exporters weakens materially, as their European and Japanese customers will face lower carbon cost penalties for using LNG carriers rather than switching to green hydrogen or ammonia-fuelled vessels. This is a $6–8 billion investment decision sitting inside India's National Green Hydrogen Mission that will be shaped, in part, by what happens in London this week. Indian companies with BRSR-disclosed net zero targets that include Scope 3 shipping emissions — or those pursuing SBTi FLAG or Corporate Net Zero Standard validation — will find their 2030 and 2035 Scope 3 trajectories increasingly difficult to defend to institutional investors if IMO's ambition remains at the 'Highly Insufficient' level that CAT currently rates it.
what_to_do: CFO / Treasury (immediate — within 30 days): commission a freight carbon cost scenario analysis modelling three IMO outcomes — no levy, J9 credit mechanism, flat carbon levy at $18–$150/tonne CO2 range — and stress-test against your top 10 export trade lanes; build this into FY2026 cost forecasting and supplier contract renegotiation calendars

CSO / ESG team (within 60 days): audit your BRSR Core value chain disclosures and SBTi target boundary to determine whether Scope 3 Category 4 and Category 9 shipping emissions are baselined; if not, initiate data collection through your freight forwarders and 3PL partners using GLEC Framework-aligned methodology — this is the methodology most likely to align with future BRSR mandatory Scope 3 reporting

Legal / Compliance (within 60 days): review exposure to EU Shipping ETS pass-through in existing freight contracts for Europe-bound shipments; confirm whether your logistics providers are absorbing or passing through ETS allowance costs, and ensure this is captured in your BRSR Core 'Scope 3 significant emission sources' disclosure

Shipping and Port teams / Infrastructure investors (within 90 days): pause or condition any LNG bunkering infrastructure commitments pending MEPC83 outcome; if the J9 is adopted, reassess LNG lock-in risk against IMO's post-2030 tightening cycle — build a 2035 regulatory scenario into your infrastructure IRR models

Board Risk Committee (next board meeting): table IMO MEPC83 outcome as a named climate transition risk under BRSR Principle 6 and ISSB IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures) — both require disclosure of policy and legal transition risks; the IMO carbon levy is now a board-level material risk for any listed company with >5% of COGS linked to international freight

Investor Relations / ESG communications: proactively brief institutional investors and ESG analysts on your shipping emissions exposure and transition strategy — foreign portfolio investors (FPIs) subject to EU SFDR or TCFD-aligned mandates will increasingly ask about Scope 3 shipping risks in engagement calls through 2025–2026
sections.our_take: MEPC83 is the most consequential IMO session since the 2018 Initial GHG Strategy, and the outcome will define the commercial and regulatory landscape for international shipping through 2035. The EU's reported backsliding from the SIDS-led levy position is a significant warning signal: it mirrors the pattern seen in early CBAM negotiations, where political compromises produced a weaker initial instrument that was subsequently tightened faster than industry expected. Indian boards and CFOs who dismiss the IMO carbon levy as a distant diplomatic problem are repeating the same miscalculation that left Indian steel and cement exporters underprepared for CBAM's October 2023 reporting phase. The structural direction is unambiguous — international shipping will be carbon-priced, the only variable is the mechanism, price point, and timeline. What concerns us most about the J9 outcome — which now looks more likely given EU softening — is not the immediate cost exposure but the signal it sends on transition finance. A credit-trading mechanism without predictable revenue generation will not fund the green corridor infrastructure, the methanol and ammonia bunkering networks, or the vessel retrofit financing that Indian shipping and port sectors need to remain competitive in a decarbonising global trade system. India should be using its G20 legacy and its voice at IMO not just to protect short-term freight competitiveness, but to secure a seat in the revenue redistribution architecture of a flat levy — ensuring that Indian climate-vulnerable coastal communities and Indian green shipping infrastructure both benefit from the transition finance flows a robust levy would generate. In the next 12–24 months, watch for: SEBI expanding BRSR Core Scope 3 assurance requirements to include transportation categories; RBI's climate risk disclosure framework (expected 2025–2026) beginning to ask banks about shipping-linked transition risk in export credit portfolios; and EU CSRD double materiality assessments by European customers of Indian exporters flagging upstream Scope 3 shipping emissions as financially material — triggering supplier-level data requests that Indian companies are currently not equipped to answer.

posts.title: CAT's Power Sector Decarbonisation Scorecard: What India's NDC Update and Renewable Energy Trajectory Mean for Capital Markets and BRSR Disclosure
posts.summary: The Climate Action Tracker's May 2025 virtual event — 'Plugging into Progress: Evaluating Power Sector Decarbonisation for 1.5°C' — has released a structured evaluation of whether countries' renewable energy rollouts are on track to meet the COP28 commitment to triple global renewables capacity by 2

sections.summary: The Climate Action Tracker's May 2025 virtual event — 'Plugging into Progress: Evaluating Power Sector Decarbonisation for 1.5°C' — has released a structured evaluation of whether countries' renewable energy rollouts are on track to meet the COP28 commitment to triple global renewables capacity by 2030. For India, this is not an abstract global scorecard: India's updated NDC — due in 2025 as part of the Paris Agreement cycle — will be benchmarked against exactly this framework, and its credibility gap or progress story will directly influence sovereign climate risk ratings, green bond demand, and the ESG narrative Indian listed companies can credibly tell institutional investors. The CAT analysis, sourced from climateactiontracker.org, makes clear that NDC ambition without sectoral power decarbonisation evidence is no longer sufficient for 1.5°C alignment. For Indian CFOs and CSOs at BSE 500 companies, this event signals an accelerating convergence: global investors and supply chain counterparties will increasingly triangulate India's national power grid trajectory against company-level Scope 2 disclosures under BRSR Core and ISSB IFRS S2, making grid decarbonisation both a country-level diplomatic issue and an enterprise-level financial materiality question.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/virtual-event-pulling-into-progress-power-decarbonisation/) documents a formal structured evaluation of national power sector decarbonisation trajectories against the 1.5°C pathway, with particular focus on whether the COP28 tripling-renewables pledge — signed by over 130 countries including India — is being translated into credible, measurable NDC commitments in 2025. CAT's framework goes beyond headline renewable capacity targets and evaluates the pace of rollout, policy sufficiency, and alignment with Paris Agreement carbon budgets. The key analytical shift: CAT is now treating power sector decarbonisation as a leading indicator of NDC credibility, not just a sub-component. For India, this matters acutely. India submitted its updated NDC in 2022, committing to 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030 and a 45% reduction in emissions intensity of GDP. The 2025 NDC revision cycle — which all Paris signatories are expected to complete ahead of COP30 in Belém, Brazil, in November 2025 — will require India to either deepen these commitments or explain why not. CAT's evaluation framework, now publicly available, will be used by investors, ratings agencies (including those informing MSCI ESG Ratings, S&P Global ESG Scores, and Sustainalytics), and EU counterparties assessing India under CBAM and CSRD value chain due diligence to judge whether India's power sector transition is real or rhetorical. Critically, India's current renewable addition rate — while impressive in absolute terms, with over 200 GW of installed non-fossil capacity as of early 2025 — still faces structural challenges: grid integration bottlenecks, land acquisition delays, state DISCOM financial distress, and a coal capacity pipeline that remains active. CAT's methodology distinguishes between installed capacity and actual clean energy share in generation mix, a distinction that will increasingly matter to Scope 2 emissions calculations that Indian companies must disclose under BRSR Core (mandatory for top 150 listed entities by market cap from FY2023-24) and that institutional investors will scrutinise under ISSB IFRS S2 climate-related disclosure frameworks.
who_is_affected: Power generation and utilities sector: NTPC, Adani Green, Tata Power, JSW Energy, ReNew Power — directly rated on generation mix trajectory and capex alignment with 1.5°C pathways

Energy-intensive industries with high Scope 2 exposure: Steel (Tata Steel, JSW Steel, SAIL), Aluminium (Hindalco, Vedanta), Cement (UltraTech, ACC, Ambuja), and Data Centres — whose decarbonisation credibility is structurally dependent on grid emission factor trajectory

Top 150 BSE-listed companies by market cap: mandatory BRSR Core disclosures require Scope 1 and 2 GHG reporting; grid emission factor assumptions used in Scope 2 calculations will face investor scrutiny against CAT's power sector ratings

Export-oriented manufacturers: companies supplying to EU buyers face CSRD value chain disclosure requirements and CBAM carbon pricing on steel, aluminium, cement, and fertilisers — all sectors where Scope 2 grid intensity is a direct cost and compliance variable

CFOs and Treasury teams at companies with green bonds or sustainability-linked loans: covenants tied to renewable energy procurement or Scope 2 reduction targets are exposed if India's grid decarbonisation pace slips
ESG analysts and portfolio managers at FIIs, domestic mutual funds with ESG mandates, and development finance institutions (IFC, ADB, AIIB) financing Indian energy transition assets
India's Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) and MoEFCC officials preparing India's 2025 NDC revision — CAT's evaluation will be a reference document at COP30 negotiations
key_obligations: India's updated NDC submission: Due ahead of COP30 (November 2025, Belém) — India must present revised 2035 targets; CAT's power sector evaluation will benchmark the credibility of any renewable energy commitment made

BRSR Core — Scope 2 GHG disclosure: Mandatory for top 150 listed companies by market cap from FY2023-24 reporting (published in FY2024-25 annual reports); companies must use a defensible grid emission factor — currently CEA's CO2 baseline database — and disclose methodology

BRSR Core — Energy intensity and renewable energy consumption: Leadership indicators require disclosure of percentage of renewable energy consumed; this directly links to grid mix trajectory and on-site/off-site renewable procurement strategies

COP28 Tripling Renewables Pledge: India is a signatory; 2025 NDC must demonstrate a credible pathway to contribution — failure to do so will be flagged by CAT and picked up by sovereign ESG rating agencies

EU CBAM Phase-In: Full CBAM obligations begin January 2026 for steel, cement, aluminium, fertilisers, hydrogen, and electricity; Indian exporters in these sectors must report embedded carbon including Scope 2 from grid electricity — grid decarbonisation pace directly affects CBAM liability

SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard: Companies with validated science-based targets must align Scope 2 reduction trajectories with a 1.5°C pathway; CAT's assessment of India's grid trajectory will inform SBTi's country-level grid decarbonisation assumptions used in target validation

ISSB IFRS S2 adoption: While not yet mandated in India, SEBI's stated intent to align BRSR with ISSB means companies should begin preparing climate-related financial disclosures including transition risk scenarios tied to power sector decarbonisation pace
sections.climate_angle: The CAT power sector evaluation is, at its core, a transition risk stress test applied at the country level — and for Indian businesses, it directly shapes the financial materiality landscape. India's NDC credibility gap, if identified by CAT, will flow into sovereign climate risk assessments used by Moody's, S&P, and Fitch, raising the cost of green sovereign bonds and infrastructure financing. At the corporate level, the most immediate transition risk is Scope 2 emissions intensity: if India's grid emission factor — currently approximately 0.71 tCO2/MWh on the CEA baseline — does not decline at a pace consistent with 1.5°C, then energy-intensive Indian manufacturers face a structural competitive disadvantage in EU export markets under CBAM, and a credibility deficit in meeting SBTi-validated Scope 2 reduction targets. The BRSR Core framework requires listed Indian companies to disclose energy consumption from grid versus renewable sources, and to report total Scope 2 emissions. As institutional investors — particularly those aligned with TCFD, ISSB IFRS S2, and the Net Zero Asset Managers Initiative — increase their scrutiny of Scope 2 trajectories, a stagnant or slow-decarbonising Indian grid becomes a portfolio-level risk flag. Indian companies that have not invested in captive renewable energy, Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) for RE100-quality certificates, or RECs will find their Scope 2 disclosures increasingly difficult to defend against 1.5°C alignment benchmarks that CAT's methodology now makes publicly accessible and comparable. The physical risk dimension is equally present: India's power sector is already experiencing grid stress from extreme heat events — a trend that the IPCC AR6 WG2 report identifies as accelerating under 1.5°C and beyond, creating both reliability risk for industrial consumers and stranded asset risk for coal-heavy generation portfolios.
what_to_do: Sustainability and Strategy teams (immediate, Q2-Q3 FY2025-26): Download CAT's power sector evaluation slides and methodology; map India's rating against your company's Scope 2 emission factor assumptions in BRSR Core disclosures — identify whether your current grid emission factor is defensible or needs updating to CEA's latest CO2 baseline

CFOs and Treasury (within 90 days): Review green bond frameworks, sustainability-linked loan covenants, and ESG-linked financing terms for any Scope 2 or renewable energy KPIs; assess exposure if India's grid decarbonisation pace causes covenant breach or target miss — engage lenders proactively with a grid trajectory narrative

Procurement and Energy teams (FY2025-26 planning cycle): Accelerate evaluation of long-term PPAs, open access renewable energy, and captive solar/wind projects to structurally decouple Scope 2 intensity from grid trajectory — this is now both a compliance necessity (BRSR Core, SBTi) and a CBAM cost-management strategy for exporters

Board Risk Committee (next board meeting): Add 'India NDC revision and power sector decarbonisation trajectory' as a standing agenda item under climate-related financial risk; commission a scenario analysis under ISSB IFRS S2 Appendix B climate scenarios showing the impact of slow vs. fast grid decarbonisation on Scope 2 costs and export competitiveness

Investor Relations (ahead of AGM season FY2025-26): Prepare a briefing note for institutional investors on your company's Scope 2 strategy relative to India's grid trajectory, referencing CAT's framework; proactively address how you are managing the gap between current grid emission factor and your net-zero or SBTi pathway — investors aligned with NZAM and PRI will ask this question

Legal and Compliance (Q3 FY2025-26): Map CBAM embedded carbon reporting requirements for any steel, aluminium, cement, or fertiliser exports to the EU against current Scope 2 grid emission factor; engage with EU CBAM authorised declarant process and ensure carbon data chain of custody is audit-ready by January 2026
sections.our_take: CAT's structured power sector evaluation is a signal of a much larger shift: the era of NDC-level ambiguity is ending. By 2026-2027, sovereign climate performance on power decarbonisation will be a hard input into capital market pricing — not a soft ESG narrative. India is at a pivotal juncture. Its renewable energy capacity addition story is genuinely impressive, but CAT's methodology will expose the gap between installed capacity headlines and actual grid carbon intensity, and that gap has direct financial consequences for Indian listed companies across three vectors: Scope 2 disclosure credibility under BRSR Core and ISSB IFRS S2, CBAM cost exposure for industrial exporters, and SBTi target validation integrity. What boards and investors should watch over the next 12-36 months: First, India's 2025 NDC revision will be a defining moment — if it fails to include a credible 2035 power sector decarbonisation trajectory with quantified coal phase-down milestones, expect CAT and allied research bodies to publish critical assessments that will be cited in sovereign ESG ratings downgrades. Second, SEBI is moving toward mandatory ISSB IFRS S2 alignment — the BRSR evolution roadmap telegraphed in SEBI's 2023 consultation suggests that Scope 2 market-based disclosures and transition plan requirements are coming within the next 2-3 years, which will make RE100-quality procurement and grid factor methodology a board-level governance question, not just a sustainability team exercise. Third, the EU's CSRD value chain rules will force large Indian suppliers to multinational companies to provide power sector carbon data that is externally assured — companies that have not invested in renewable energy procurement infrastructure by 2026 will face supply chain disqualification risk, not just reputational exposure. India's power sector transition is no longer a background condition for corporate ESG strategy — it is the single most important structural variable determining whether Indian companies can credibly claim 1.5°C alignment, and the capital market consequences of getting this wrong are now measurable, proximate, and accelerating.

posts.title: India's Fossil Fuel Import Trap: Why the Middle East Energy Shock Is a Capital Allocation Wake-Up Call for Indian Boards
posts.summary: India's structural dependence on imported fossil fuels — 85–90% of crude oil, 45–50% of gas, and over 240 million tonnes of imported coal annually — is no longer a geopolitical footnote but a balance sheet risk that Indian CFOs and board risk committees must price into long-term capital planning. Th

sections.summary: India's structural dependence on imported fossil fuels — 85–90% of crude oil, 45–50% of gas, and over 240 million tonnes of imported coal annually — is no longer a geopolitical footnote but a balance sheet risk that Indian CFOs and board risk committees must price into long-term capital planning. The Middle East crisis, as documented by Climate Action Tracker (CAT) in its analysis 'Breaking the cycle of energy shocks: India's renewable opportunity' (https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/breaking-the-cycle-of-energy-shocks-indias-renewable-opportunity/), exposes a systemic contradiction: India is simultaneously accelerating renewable energy deployment and doubling down on imported coal and gas infrastructure, creating a stranded asset liability that will surface in BRSR Core disclosures and ISSB S2-aligned climate risk reporting within the next 24 months. For listed Indian companies — particularly energy-intensive sectors under BEE's PAT scheme, exporters facing EU CBAM scrutiny, and companies with international institutional investors applying ISSB or TCFD lenses — this is not an abstract energy policy debate. It is a live, quantifiable financial risk that is heading directly toward mandatory disclosure.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by Dr. Nandini Das, CAT's India country lead, published at https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/breaking-the-cycle-of-energy-shocks-indias-renewable-opportunity/, documents what three decades of energy shocks have repeatedly confirmed: India's energy system vulnerability is structural, not cyclical. The current Middle East disruption — affecting approximately 20% of global energy supply — has triggered two simultaneous and contradictory Indian policy responses: emergency clearances for wind and battery storage acceleration, and the invocation of emergency clauses to run imported-coal plants at full capacity. This is not a temporary tactical tension; it represents a fundamental strategic incoherence in India's energy transition pathway. What has materially changed is the speed at which these contradictions are becoming financially visible. India's coal import bill and crude oil import dependency create a persistent current account vulnerability — each oil price spike of USD 10/barrel adds approximately USD 12–15 billion to India's annual import bill, directly affecting the INR, inflation, and fiscal headroom. For Indian corporates, this translates into input cost volatility, working capital stress, and — critically — scope 2 and scope 3 emissions exposure that is now subject to mandatory disclosure under SEBI's BRSR Core framework for the top 150 listed companies (FY2023-24) and will extend to the top 1,000 listed companies by FY2026-27. The CAT analysis makes explicit what Indian boards have been slow to acknowledge: expanding gas infrastructure — LNG terminals, city gas distribution networks, cross-country pipelines — risks locking India into a second fossil fuel dependency trap at precisely the moment when solar and wind are cost-competitive and storage technology is scaling rapidly. The India-specific regulatory consequence is sharp. Under SEBI's BRSR Core (mandated for top 150 listed entities from FY2023-24), companies must now disclose Scope 1, 2, and 3 GHG emissions with reasonable assurance, and energy intensity metrics tied to their business activity. For companies in sectors such as steel, cement, aluminium, petrochemicals, and power — all of which sit within BEE's PAT Scheme Cycle III — exposure to imported fossil fuel price volatility is not just an operational risk; it is a disclosed, auditable financial risk. Simultaneously, Indian exporters to the EU face the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), which is now in its transitional reporting phase (October 2023–December 2025) and enters full financial liability from January 2026, with embedded carbon costs in steel, aluminium, cement, fertilisers, and hydrogen exports directly affecting trade competitiveness.
who_is_affected: Energy-intensive industrial sectors under BEE PAT Scheme Cycle III: steel, cement, aluminium, chlor-alkali, pulp and paper, textiles, fertilisers — all face rising input cost volatility from imported coal and gas price shocks
Top 150 and progressively top 1,000 BSE/NSE-listed companies subject to SEBI BRSR Core mandatory GHG and energy disclosures — fossil fuel exposure is now an auditable line item

Indian exporters to the EU in CBAM-covered sectors (steel, aluminium, cement, fertilisers, hydrogen) — embedded carbon from coal-based power and process heat is a direct financial liability from January 2026
CFOs and Treasury heads at energy-intensive corporates: fuel import cost volatility is a working capital and hedging strategy issue requiring board-level risk quantification
Chief Sustainability Officers and ESG heads at listed companies: must now map fossil fuel dependency into BRSR Core KPIs, and anticipate ISSB IFRS S2 scenario analysis disclosure requirements

Board Risk Committees: stranded asset risk from long-lived coal and gas infrastructure investments (20–30 year asset life vs. accelerating energy transition) needs explicit treatment in risk registers
Institutional investors — domestic (LIC, SBI MF, HDFC AMC) and foreign (GIC, Norges, BlackRock) — applying TCFD or ISSB lenses to Indian energy and industrial sector holdings
Project finance lenders and infrastructure funds exposed to Indian coal-based power or gas distribution capex — stranded asset timelines are compressing as renewables + storage cost curves fall
key_obligations: SEBI BRSR Core — Scope 1, 2, and 3 GHG emissions with reasonable assurance: mandatory for top 150 listed entities from FY2023-24 (reporting due in annual reports filed by September 2024); extends to top 1,000 listed entities by FY2026-27

BEE PAT Scheme Cycle III — energy consumption and intensity reduction targets for designated consumers (DCs) across 13 industrial sectors: compliance and trading period active; DCs must meet specific energy consumption targets or purchase Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts)

EU CBAM transitional phase — Indian exporters in covered sectors must submit quarterly embedded carbon reports to EU importers: reporting obligation active from October 2023 through December 2025; full financial carbon cost liability from January 1, 2026

ISSB IFRS S2 Climate Disclosures — scenario analysis and climate-related financial risk disclosure: adopted by IOSCO; SEBI is expected to align BRSR evolution with ISSB S1/S2 architecture — Indian listed companies should treat scenario analysis as a near-term disclosure expectation, likely mandatory within 2–3 years

MoEFCC Environment Impact Assessment (EIA) Notification — any new coal or gas infrastructure requiring environmental clearance must demonstrate alignment with India's NDC pathways; boards approving such capex face increasing regulatory and investor scrutiny

India's Updated NDC (2022) — 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030, 50% electricity from non-fossil sources by 2030: all corporate energy procurement strategies and PPAs must be stress-tested against this policy trajectory to assess stranded asset risk in existing fossil fuel offtake contracts
sections.climate_angle: The CAT analysis crystallises a transition risk that Indian boards have been underpricing: the physical and transition risk vectors are now converging simultaneously. On the physical side, Middle East supply disruptions — which CAT estimates are affecting ~20% of global energy supply — directly raise India's energy import costs and inflation, a macroeconomic transmission mechanism that ultimately affects corporate earnings, consumer demand, and sovereign fiscal capacity. On the transition risk side, India's accelerating renewable buildout (solar module prices have fallen over 90% in a decade; utility-scale solar is now the cheapest form of new electricity generation in India at INR 2–3/kWh) is rapidly making new coal and gas assets economically non-competitive, even before carbon pricing mechanisms fully activate. For Indian companies, the BRSR Core's Scope 3 disclosure requirement is particularly consequential in this context. Supply chain emissions — Category 1 (purchased goods and services) and Category 4 (upstream transportation) — are directly exposed to fossil fuel import price volatility and embedded carbon costs. A steel company using imported coking coal, or a cement company running captive coal-fired power, is carrying both a financial risk (fuel cost volatility) and a disclosure risk (rising Scope 1 and 2 emissions intensity) that will increasingly affect access to green finance, export market eligibility, and ESG ratings. The SBTi's requirement that corporate net-zero targets cover Scope 1, 2, and material Scope 3 emissions means that companies with heavy fossil fuel dependency in their value chains face a structural barrier to credible target-setting — which in turn affects their eligibility for green bonds, sustainability-linked loans, and institutional ESG mandates. India's carbon market, under development via the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) notified by MoEFCC in June 2023, will over time put an explicit price on this embedded fossil fuel dependency, further compressing the economics of coal and gas-intensive operations. The renewable energy acceleration pathway — if executed with storage integration, grid flexibility, and demand-side management — represents India's most credible hedge against both physical energy security risk and transition-related capital market risk. For corporate energy buyers, this translates into a clear strategic imperative: accelerate long-term renewable PPAs, invest in captive solar and wind, and where feasible, pursue green hydrogen for hard-to-abate process heat. These are not just ESG commitments — they are balance sheet risk management decisions that CFOs should be driving alongside CSOs.
what_to_do: CFO / Treasury: Within Q3 FY2025, commission a quantitative stress test mapping the company's direct and indirect fossil fuel import cost exposure against three oil/gas price scenarios (base, +30%, +60%) — this feeds directly into BRSR Core's financial risk disclosure and ISSB S2 scenario analysis expectations

CSO / Sustainability team: Map all energy inputs — grid electricity, captive generation, process fuels — against BRSR Core Scope 1/2/3 boundaries by December 2024; identify which Scope 3 categories carry fossil fuel import price risk and disclose accordingly in the FY2025 annual report

Board Risk Committee: Add 'fossil fuel import dependency and stranded asset risk' as a named line item in the enterprise risk register, with quantified financial exposure and a transition timeline; link this to the company's capital allocation review for any planned coal or gas infrastructure investments over a 5-year horizon

Strategy / Capex Committee: Any new capex proposals involving coal-based power, gas distribution, or LNG offtake contracts must include a stranded asset assessment against India's 2030 NDC targets and IEA's Net Zero 2050 scenario — board approval should require explicit sign-off on transition risk

Procurement / Supply Chain: For EU-facing exporters, initiate CBAM embedded carbon calculation for all exported products in covered categories immediately — the transitional reporting period is live, and errors or gaps will create compliance liability from January 2026

Investor Relations: Proactively brief top 10–15 institutional investors (both domestic and FII) on the company's fossil fuel transition strategy, renewable energy procurement roadmap, and BRSR Core GHG disclosure trajectory — investors applying ISSB or TCFD frameworks are asking these questions now, and silence is being read as unpreparedness

Legal / Compliance: Review all long-term fossil fuel offtake contracts and PPAs for force majeure, price re-opener, and termination provisions in light of accelerating energy transition policy; escalate any contracts with tenors beyond 2030 to board risk committee for stress-testing
sections.our_take: The CAT analysis by Dr. Nandini Das is a rigorous, data-grounded articulation of what India's most sophisticated energy economists have been saying privately for years: India's energy transition is not a climate policy choice — it is an economic and national security imperative. What makes the current moment strategically different from previous energy shock cycles is the simultaneous maturation of three forces that did not exist together before: renewable energy is now cheaper than new fossil fuel capacity in India; mandatory ESG disclosure frameworks (BRSR Core, EU CBAM, incoming ISSB alignment) are making fossil fuel dependency financially legible to capital markets; and India's carbon market architecture (CCTS + PAT scheme convergence) is beginning to put a price signal on carbon intensity for the first time. For boards and institutional investors, the 12–36 month outlook is unambiguous: the cost of inaction on fossil fuel transition is rising faster than the cost of transition itself. We expect SEBI to progressively align BRSR with ISSB S1 and S2 over the next two annual reporting cycles, which will introduce mandatory climate scenario analysis for listed companies — a disclosure that will make stranded asset risk in coal and gas infrastructure directly visible to markets. We also expect MoEFCC's CCTS to begin its first compliance cycle for obligated entities by FY2026-27, creating an explicit carbon cost that will further erode the economics of coal-intensive operations. Companies that treat this as a compliance exercise will be perpetually behind; companies that treat it as a capital reallocation signal — accelerating renewable procurement, retiring or repurposing fossil fuel assets, and embedding carbon pricing into investment hurdle rates — will build a structural competitive advantage in export markets, green finance access, and institutional investor engagement. The renewable opportunity that CAT identifies is real. The question for Indian boards is whether they will capture it proactively or be forced into it reactively, at higher cost and with greater reputational and regulatory exposure.

posts.title: IMO's Net Zero Framework Postponement to April 2026: What India's Shipping-Dependent Exporters, Port Operators, and ESG Investors Must Prepare For Now
posts.summary: In October 2025, the International Maritime Organization failed to adopt its Net Zero Framework — a global fuel emissions standard paired with a market-based pricing mechanism — following blocking pressure from the United States and Saudi Arabia, deferring the decision to MEPC 84 in April 2026. The

sections.summary: In October 2025, the International Maritime Organization failed to adopt its Net Zero Framework — a global fuel emissions standard paired with a market-based pricing mechanism — following blocking pressure from the United States and Saudi Arabia, deferring the decision to MEPC 84 in April 2026. The Climate Action Tracker (CAT) warns this delay risks locking shipping onto a 4°C emissions trajectory rather than the 2°C pathway the NZF would have enabled (https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/rescuing-shippings-net-zero-framework/). For India, the consequences are immediate and commercially material: Indian exporters moving roughly 95% of trade by volume through international shipping, major port operators at JNPA, Mundra, and Vizag, and Indian shipbuilders competing for next-generation vessel contracts all face accelerating compliance cost uncertainty — and their European buyers are already pricing Scope 3 shipping emissions into CSRD-aligned supply chain disclosures. The April 2026 IMO vote is not a distant geopolitical event; it is a board-level capital allocation decision for every Indian company with material ocean freight exposure.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (climateactiontracker.org), authored by Michael Petroni and Finn Hossfeld, confirms that the IMO's Marine Environment Protection Committee failed at its October 2025 session to adopt the Net Zero Framework measures that had been agreed in principle earlier in the year. The postponement to MEPC 84 (April 2026) was driven by opposition from the United States — which remains categorically opposed to the framework — and Saudi Arabia, whose economy is structurally exposed to declining bunker fuel demand embedded in any credible shipping decarbonisation regime. The NZF, in its current form, would introduce two interlocking mechanisms: a Global Fuel Standard (GFS) that mandates a progressive reduction in the greenhouse gas intensity of marine fuels, and a market-based mechanism (MBM) — effectively a carbon levy or credit trading system — that prices excess emissions and channels revenues into an IMO Fund to support zero-emission fuel infrastructure and a just transition for developing maritime nations. CAT's modelling projects the NZF would reduce shipping emissions 14–22% below 2008 levels by 2030, improving the sector's current 'Highly Insufficient' rating to merely 'Insufficient' — still consistent with 3–4°C of warming — but critically establishing the regulatory architecture for steeper reductions post-2035 that could make the framework 1.5°C-compatible by 2050. What this overturns is the assumption, held widely in Indian shipping and port circles, that IMO decarbonisation rules would arrive on a predictable schedule tied to the 2023 GHG Strategy milestones. That strategy committed to 'at least 20%' emissions reduction by 2030 and net zero 'by or around 2050.' The NZF was the operational instrument to enforce those targets through MARPOL Annex VI amendments. Its postponement creates a 6-month regulatory vacuum — but more critically, it signals that the US under its current administration will use its IMO membership to actively obstruct binding global shipping emissions rules, a posture that may persist beyond April 2026. For Indian trade policy and port investment planning, this is a structural uncertainty, not a procedural delay. The India-specific implication is layered. First, India is among the world's top 10 flag states and a significant ship recycling nation under the Hong Kong Convention; any IMO framework that reshapes vessel economics directly affects Indian yards at Alang. Second, India's major exporters — textiles from Tirupur, pharmaceuticals from Hyderabad and Ahmedabad, auto components from Pune and Chennai, chemicals from Surat — ship predominantly on foreign-flagged vessels whose owners will pass through compliance costs via freight rates the moment the NZF is adopted. Third, the EU's own unilateral shipping inclusion under the EU ETS, which has been operational since January 2024 and covers 50% of emissions on voyages to/from EU ports, is already creating a two-tier freight market. Indian exporters sending containerised cargo to Rotterdam, Hamburg, or Antwerp are already absorbing EU ETS carbon costs embedded in freight invoices — whether or not their CFOs have mapped this as a Scope 3 cost or a procurement risk.
who_is_affected: Indian merchandise exporters with >15% revenue from EU, UK, or US ocean freight routes — particularly textiles, pharma, chemicals, auto components, and agri-commodities

Indian port operators and port trusts: JNPA (Nhava Sheva), Adani Ports (Mundra, Hazira, Kattupalli), DP World India, Vizag Port — all facing infrastructure investment decisions tied to green fuel bunkering (methanol, ammonia, green hydrogen) whose economic case shifts materially with NZF adoption

Indian shipbuilders and ship recyclers: Cochin Shipyard, Hindustan Shipyard, Alang yards — competing for new vessel orders where green propulsion specifications are being set by European and Japanese owners anticipating NZF-aligned fuel standards

CFOs and Chief Procurement Officers at listed Indian companies with material ocean freight in their Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) inventory — relevant to BRSR Core disclosures and ISSB IFRS S2 alignment

ESG and fixed income investors in Indian port infrastructure, logistics REITs, and shipping-adjacent companies — particularly those benchmarked against ESG indices that are beginning to price IMO transition risk

Indian banks and DFIs (SBI, EXIM Bank, IIFCL) with project finance exposure to port terminals and vessel financing, where stranded asset risk rises if NZF adoption forces accelerated retrofitting or early vessel retirement
key_obligations: MEPC 84, April 2026: The next IMO vote on NZF adoption — Indian maritime and trade ministries (MoPSW, DGSHIP, MEA) must submit India's formal position before this session; India's vote matters given its status as a major flag state and developing economy beneficiary of the IMO Fund

EU ETS for shipping — already in force since January 2024: Vessels of 5,000 GT+ on EU port calls must surrender EU Allowances (EUAs) for 50% of emissions in 2024, rising to 100% by 2026; Indian exporters on EU routes are absorbing this cost now through freight surcharges — CFOs must quantify and disclose this in FY25 annual reports

EU FuelEU Maritime Regulation — effective January 2025: Sets GHG intensity limits for energy used by ships on EU routes; escalates 2% below 2020 baseline by 2025, 6% by 2030, 80% by 2050 — directly sets the fuel specification standard for vessels carrying Indian goods to Europe regardless of IMO NZF outcome

BRSR Core (mandatory for top 150 listed entities, FY2023-24; top 250 from FY2024-25): Scope 3 emissions disclosure under Principle 6 — CFOs must now decide whether ocean freight emissions (Category 4) are material enough to require quantification; NZF uncertainty makes this a forward-looking risk, not just a historical accounting question

ISSB IFRS S2 — India's roadmap to adoption: ICAI and SEBI are monitoring ISSB adoption timelines; IFRS S2 requires disclosure of climate-related risks including transition risks from sector decarbonisation regulations — IMO NZF trajectory is a named transition risk for shipping-dependent Indian companies

Ship Recycling: India's Recycling of Ships Act 2019 (Hong Kong Convention aligned) — as older, non-NZF-compliant vessels face accelerated retirement under any post-2026 framework, Alang yards must be positioned to absorb the resulting scrapping volumes; DGSHIP must assess capacity and compliance readiness
sections.climate_angle: The CAT's core finding — that NZF adoption determines whether shipping settles on a 2°C or 4°C emissions pathway — carries direct implications for India's own Paris Agreement commitments and its Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) update cycle. India's updated NDC targets 45% emissions intensity reduction of GDP by 2030 and 50% of cumulative electric power from non-fossil sources. Shipping is not currently disaggregated within India's NDC, but international shipping emissions — by IMO convention — sit outside national inventories. This accounting gap means Indian companies can simultaneously claim NDC alignment domestically while their ocean freight supply chains generate uncounted, unpriced Scope 3 emissions that are material to global temperature outcomes. The transition angle sharpens when you consider India's emerging green hydrogen and green ammonia ambitions. The National Green Hydrogen Mission, with its ₹19,744 crore outlay, explicitly targets maritime fuel exports as a demand driver. India's NTPC, Adani Green, and Greenko are all developing green hydrogen production capacity partly premised on shipping sector offtake as marine fuel. If the NZF is adopted — even in its current 'Insufficient' form — it creates binding demand pull for zero-emission fuels on global shipping routes from 2030, directly validating the investment thesis for Indian green hydrogen producers targeting maritime export markets. A delayed or weakened NZF is, paradoxically, a demand risk for India's green hydrogen ambitions, not just a global climate policy failure. On physical climate risk: shipping route disruption from climate impacts — particularly cyclone intensification in the Bay of Bengal and Arabian Sea affecting Indian coastal port operations — is already documented in the physical risk literature. The TNFD's LEAP framework and ISSB IFRS S2 both require assessment of physical climate risk to operations. Indian port operators have not yet systematically integrated IMO transition risk with physical climate risk in their board-level risk registers — this is a governance gap that institutional investors should flag in their stewardship engagement with port companies listed on BSE/NSE.
what_to_do: CFO / Treasury — within 60 days: Quantify EU ETS freight surcharge exposure for FY25 and FY26 across all EU-bound ocean freight contracts; treat this as a known, escalating cost line (rising to 100% EUA coverage in 2026), not a contingent liability — budget accordingly and disclose in BRSR Core Scope 3 commentary

Chief Sustainability Officer / ESG team — within 90 days: Map Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) and Category 9 (downstream transportation) for ocean freight lanes; use GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Standard methodology; flag IMO NZF as a named regulatory transition risk in the company's climate risk register under ISSB IFRS S2 / BRSR Core alignment

Procurement / Supply Chain Head — immediately: Engage top 3–5 shipping line counterparties (Maersk, MSC, CMA CGM, Evergreen) to obtain carbon intensity ratings of allocated vessel capacity; require green freight data feeds compatible with CDP supply chain reporting — this protects against Scope 3 audit exposure as BRSR assurance requirements tighten post-FY2025

Board Risk Committee — before Q4 FY26 board meeting: Commission a scenario analysis under two pathways — (a) NZF adopted at MEPC 84 April 2026, and (b) NZF further delayed or weakened — assessing freight cost impact, export competitiveness risk, and green hydrogen demand implications; disclose material findings in FY26 Annual Report under BRSR Principle 6

Government affairs / policy team — before February 2026: Engage Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways (MoPSW) and the Ministry of External Affairs on India's MEPC 84 submission; India should align with SIDS and LDCs in supporting NZF adoption to protect the IMO Fund mechanism that benefits developing nations — and to preserve the demand signal for India's green hydrogen export strategy

Port operators and infrastructure investors — within 6 months: Commission a green bunkering infrastructure feasibility study for methanol and ammonia at JNPA, Mundra, and Paradip; NZF adoption or EU FuelEU Maritime alone will create near-term commercial demand — first-mover advantage in South Asian green bunkering is a real competitive position
sections.our_take: The IMO NZF postponement is a preview of the fracturing multilateral climate governance architecture that Indian boards and investors must now price into their strategic planning. The US opposition to binding shipping emissions rules is not an aberration — it is consistent with a broader posture of withdrawing from multilateral climate frameworks, from Paris Agreement re-exit to IRA rollbacks. But here is what sophisticated Indian companies should understand: unilateral regulatory action by the EU does not wait for IMO consensus. The EU ETS for shipping is live. FuelEU Maritime is live. The EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), while currently covering steel, cement, aluminium, and fertilisers, has an explicit review clause for potential expansion to shipping. India's exporters are already operating under a de facto European shipping emissions regime — the IMO NZF, if and when adopted, would merely globalise what the EU has already unilaterally imposed. The deeper strategic signal is this: the 'by or around 2050' ambiguity in the NZF's net zero formulation — flagged explicitly by CAT — is a red flag for long-dated infrastructure and vessel financing. Indian banks and DFIs financing port terminals with 20–30 year asset lives, or vessel acquisition loans with 15-year tenors, are implicitly underwriting a transition risk they have not yet quantified. The stranded asset risk from premature vessel retirement or bunkering infrastructure obsolescence (LNG being the canonical example, given CAT's concern about NZF locking in LNG) should be stress-tested against MEPC 84 outcomes in every credit committee presentation from Q1 2026 onwards. Looking 12–36 months out: if MEPC 84 adopts the NZF in April 2026 — even in its current insufficient form — it will trigger an immediate renegotiation of long-term shipping contracts, accelerate retrofitting decisions for Indian-flagged vessels, create a commercial bunkering investment race in major South Asian ports, and force every BRSR Core-reporting company with material ocean freight to upgrade its Scope 3 Category 4 methodology from estimate-based to activity-data-based calculation. India's SEBI will face pressure from global institutional investors — already aligned with ISSB S2 — to require shipping transition risk as a named disclosure item in BRSR. The companies that have done the work by April 2026 will be differentiated in capital market conversations. Those that have not will face the same audit scrutiny that drove the first wave of BRSR Core restatements. The window to act is now, not after the IMO vote.

posts.title: Why Nature Matters: What Indian Business Leaders Need to Know About TNFD and the Coming Wave of Nature-Related Disclosure
posts.summary: The Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) has established a global framework that compels companies and financial institutions to assess, disclose, and act on their dependencies and impacts on nature — including biodiversity, freshwater, land, and ocean systems. For Indian busines

sections.summary: The Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) has established a global framework that compels companies and financial institutions to assess, disclose, and act on their dependencies and impacts on nature — including biodiversity, freshwater, land, and ocean systems. For Indian businesses, which are deeply embedded in nature-dependent sectors such as agriculture, textiles, pharmaceuticals, and infrastructure, this is not a distant voluntary exercise — it is the next frontier of mandatory ESG accountability. As global investors and trading partners increasingly align with TNFD, Indian companies that fail to understand their nature footprint risk losing capital access, supply chain contracts, and social licence to operate.
sections.what_changed: In September 2023, the TNFD released its final recommendations — a comprehensive, science-based framework for organisations to report on how their business activities affect and depend upon nature. Built on the architecture of the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD), the TNFD framework introduces the concept of 'nature-related risks and opportunities' across four pillars: Governance, Strategy, Risk & Impact Management, and Metrics & Targets. Unlike climate disclosures, which focus primarily on greenhouse gas emissions and temperature pathways, nature-related disclosures require companies to map their interactions with ecosystems — think water consumption from stressed river basins, deforestation embedded in commodity supply chains, soil degradation from intensive farming, or habitat destruction from real estate and mining operations. The TNFD also introduces the LEAP approach (Locate, Evaluate, Assess, Prepare) — a practical methodology for identifying where a company's operations and supply chains intersect with sensitive or degraded ecosystems. Globally, over 400 organisations have already signed up as early adopters, committing to begin TNFD-aligned reporting by financial year 2024–25. Regulators in the EU, UK, and Singapore are actively exploring how TNFD recommendations will be incorporated into mandatory disclosure regimes. India's own SEBI Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework is increasingly converging with global standards, making TNFD alignment a near-term inevitability for listed Indian companies.
who_is_affected: Large listed Indian companies (NSE/BSE) already subject to SEBI's BRSR Core disclosures, particularly those in high nature-impact sectors
Agribusiness, food & beverage, and FMCG companies with commodity supply chains touching forests, freshwater, or marine ecosystems
Textile and apparel manufacturers dependent on water-intensive cotton and synthetic fibre production
Pharmaceutical and chemical companies sourcing botanical or marine-derived inputs
Infrastructure developers, real estate firms, and mining companies operating near biodiversity-sensitive zones or protected areas
Financial institutions — banks, asset managers, and insurers — with portfolio exposure to nature-dependent sectors
Indian subsidiaries of multinational corporations whose parent companies are TNFD early adopters or subject to EU CSRD requirements
Export-oriented businesses supplying to EU or UK markets, where nature-related due diligence obligations (e.g., EU Deforestation Regulation) are already in force
key_obligations: TNFD Early Adopter Commitment (Voluntary — FY2024-25 onwards): Companies that have signed up as TNFD early adopters are expected to begin producing TNFD-aligned disclosures in their annual or sustainability reports for financial years starting in 2024

EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) — Enforcement from December 2025 (delayed from 2024): Indian exporters of soy, palm oil, cattle, wood, cocoa, coffee, rubber, and derived products must demonstrate deforestation-free supply chains with geolocation data

EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) — Phased from 2025: Indian companies that are subsidiaries or significant suppliers of EU-regulated entities will face nature-related disclosure expectations flowing down their value chains

SEBI BRSR Core — Mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24, expanding to top 1,000 by FY2026-27: While not yet explicitly TNFD-aligned, BRSR's biodiversity, water, and land-use disclosures are foundational building blocks
Immediate action (now): Conduct an initial screening of operations and supply chains using the TNFD LEAP methodology to identify high-priority nature-related interfaces
Near-term (6–12 months): Establish internal governance ownership of nature risks at board or senior management level, mirroring existing climate risk governance structures
sections.climate_angle: Nature and climate are inseparable. Approximately 55% of global GDP is moderately or highly dependent on nature, and the degradation of ecosystems directly amplifies physical climate risks.
