Importers and brand owners of foreign-manufactured solar PV equipment and medical devices who are the deemed 'producers' under India's E-Waste Rules
Environmental compliance officers and legal teams at conglomerates with diversified portfolios spanning energy, electronics, and healthcare
key_obligations: Effective Date — 1 April 2023: All obligations under the Amendment Rules came into force. Companies in the solar PV and medical device sectors should treat this date as the baseline for compliance assessment.

Substance Restriction Compliance: Manufacturers and importers of solar panels, PV modules, and medical devices (non-implanted) must ensure that cadmium and lead content in covered products complies with the threshold limits specified in Schedule II of the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022. Review existing product specifications against permitted concentration values.

EPR Registration and Target-Setting: Producers newly brought under Schedule II coverage (solar PV and medical devices) must register on the CPCB EPR portal if not already registered, and set EPR targets for collection and recycling of end-of-life products containing the restricted substances.

CPCB Disclosure Readiness: Under the amended Rule 16(5), companies must maintain up-to-date, accessible records of hazardous substance content across their product lines — ready to be submitted to CPCB upon request. This replaces the earlier static documentation requirement but demands a more rigorous internal data management system.

Supply Chain Due Diligence: Engage tier-1 and tier-2 suppliers to obtain material declarations (preferably aligned with IEC 62474 or equivalent standards) confirming the presence or absence of cadmium and lead in components used in covered product categories.

Existing Inventory Review: Audit stock and products manufactured before 1 April 2023 to determine whether they contain restricted substances above threshold limits and assess how these will be managed, disclosed, or phased out.

Legal and Documentation Update: Update product compliance declarations, technical files, and import documentation to reflect the new Schedule II entries. Ensure that EPR annual returns filed with CPCB account for the newly regulated product categories.
sections.climate_angle: At first glance, this amendment may appear to be a narrow regulatory housekeeping exercise. In reality, it sits at the intersection of two of the most important climate transition trends affecting Indian industry: the explosive growth of renewable energy infrastructure and the rapid expansion of the domestic medical technology sector. India's solar capacity ambitions are staggering — the government targets 500 GW of renewable energy by 2030, with solar PV forming the backbone of that expansion. However, the lifecycle environmental cost of solar panels, particularly the cadmium in thin-film technologies and the lead in solders and junction boxes, has historically been under-regulated in India. By formally bringing these substances under the E-Waste Rules, the government is signalling that the clean energy transition must itself be clean — that the environmental liability of end-of-life solar panels cannot be externalised onto communities or landfills. For companies, this means that EPR obligations for solar PV will become increasingly material as the installed base ages and the first wave of large-scale decommissioning begins within the next decade. From a global standards perspective, this move aligns India more closely with the European Union's RoHS (Restriction of Hazardous Substances) Directive, which has long restricted cadmium and lead in electrical and electronic equipment, and with the EU's emerging Solar PV End-of-Life framework under the WEEE Directive. For Indian solar manufacturers exporting to Europe or seeking European investment, this domestic regulatory alignment reduces the compliance gap and strengthens the case for ESG-grade supply chain credentials. The ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standard and the EU's CSRD both require companies to assess and disclose material climate-related risks, including physical risks (such as extreme weather damage to solar assets) and transition risks (such as regulatory changes affecting product design and end-of-life costs). The inclusion of solar PV in India's E-Waste Rules is precisely the kind of transition risk that sustainability officers must now factor into product lifecycle cost models, capital expenditure planning, and investor disclosures. Companies that have not begun integrating e-waste EPR costs into their solar project financial models are materially underestimating their long-term liability exposure.
what_to_do: Conduct an immediate product and substance audit: Map all solar PV and medical device product lines against Schedule II entries to determine which products contain cadmium or lead above permissible thresholds. Engage your materials engineering or EHS team to produce a substance inventory aligned with IEC 62474 or RoHS declaration formats.

Register or update your CPCB EPR profile: If your company manufactures, imports, or brands solar PV panels or relevant medical devices and is not yet registered under the EPR framework, initiate registration on the CPCB centralised portal immediately. Existing registrants should update their product category listings to include the newly covered items.

Build a CPCB-ready disclosure system: Since Rule 16(5) now requires hazardous substance information 'as and when required by CPCB,' establish an internal data management process — ideally a Product Compliance Management System — that can generate substance disclosure reports on demand, with version control and audit trails.

Engage your supply chain proactively: Issue supplier questionnaires or formal Material Declaration Requests to all tier-1 component suppliers for solar and medtech product lines. Specify that declarations must cover cadmium and lead content and be updated whenever materials or processes change. Build contractual obligations for substance compliance into new supplier agreements.

Integrate EPR costs into financial and ESG planning: Work with your CFO and sustainability team to model the long-term cost of EPR targets for solar panels — including collection, dismantling, and recycling — and incorporate these into project IRR calculations, product pricing strategies, and BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting) disclosures to investors.
sections.our_take: The E-Waste First Amendment Rules, 2023 are deceptively significant. On the surface, two new Schedule II entries and a tweak to a disclosure clause may seem like minor administrative updates. But read in the context of India's climate ambitions and the global regulatory direction of travel, they represent a clear and irreversible policy signal: the solar energy sector will be held accountable for the full environmental lifecycle of its products, not just the clean kilowatt-hours they generate. The modification to Rule 16(5) also deserves attention — by making disclosure CPCB-triggered rather than document-embedded, the regulator has given itself a powerful supervisory tool while simultaneously raising the stakes for companies that lack robust internal substance data systems. We expect the CPCB to use this authority actively as enforcement capacity improves. Looking ahead, companies should anticipate further expansions of Schedule II to cover additional solar PV materials (such as per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances used in panel coatings) and a potential tightening of EPR collection targets for solar as India's decommissioning cycle accelerates post-2030. Those who build compliance infrastructure now — rather than scrambling when enforcement begins — will be best positioned to compete for institutional capital and long-term project contracts in a market where ESG credentials are increasingly non-negotiable.

posts.title: Draft Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2022: What India's Businesses Must Know About the Next Phase of EPR
posts.summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) released draft amendments to the Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 in January 2022, significantly tightening the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs). The amendments

sections.summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) released draft amendments to the Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 in January 2022, significantly tightening the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs). The amendments introduce sharper definitions for plastic packaging waste categories, expand the scope of plastic waste processors, and reinforce that EPR obligations will be governed by periodically updated regulations — making compliance a moving target rather than a one-time exercise. Companies that use, produce, or import plastic packaging must treat this not as a routine regulatory update, but as a structural shift in how plastic accountability is governed in India.
sections.what_changed: The 2022 draft amends the foundational Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 and builds on the 2021 Amendment Rules (notified August 12, 2021) that first introduced the EPR framework for plastic packaging. Here is what is new in plain language: First, the draft introduces a set of new and revised definitions that significantly broaden the regulatory perimeter. 'Brand owner' is now clearly defined as any individual or company selling goods under a registered brand label or trademark — closing a loophole that previously allowed some brand operators to sidestep liability. 'Plastic packaging' is redefined comprehensively to cover all plastic used for protection, preservation, storage, and transportation of products. Second, the rules introduce two new and important waste categories: 'pre-consumer plastic packaging waste' (waste generated during the manufacturing and packaging process, before the product reaches the end consumer) and 'post-consumer plastic packaging waste' (waste generated after the consumer has used and discarded the packaging). This distinction is critical because it means EPR obligations now extend upstream into manufacturing processes, not just downstream to post-sale collection. Third, the definition of 'plastic waste processors' is expanded to include recyclers, waste-to-energy facilities (including co-processing in cement kilns), waste-to-oil converters, and industrial composters — broadening the ecosystem of compliant end-of-life pathways that PIBOs can use to meet their EPR targets. Fourth, 'end-of-life disposal' is formally defined to include energy recovery (co-processing in cement kilns), waste-to-oil production, and use in road construction per Indian Roads Congress guidelines — legitimising these pathways under the rules. Fifth, and perhaps most consequentially for compliance planning, Rule 9(1) is replaced with language stating that producers, importers, and brand owners shall fulfil their EPR obligations 'in accordance with regulations issued from time to time under these rules.' This means EPR targets, timelines, and mechanisms are no longer static — they will be updated by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) through regulations, requiring companies to continuously monitor for changes. Finally, registration requirements are clarified: entities operating in more than two states or union territories must register with the CPCB (not state boards), creating a clearer compliance pathway for national and multinational brands.
who_is_affected: Producers and manufacturers of plastic packaging materials — from flexible films and rigid containers to multilayer packaging and carry bags
Brand owners — any company selling products in plastic packaging under a registered trademark, regardless of whether they manufacture the packaging themselves
Importers of plastic packaging products or products packaged in plastic, including importers of multilayer and single-use packaging
Plastic waste recyclers, waste-to-energy operators, and industrial composters who are newly classified as 'plastic waste processors' with defined roles in the EPR chain
Fast-Moving Consumer Goods (FMCG) companies, e-commerce firms, food and beverage brands, pharmaceutical companies, and consumer durables manufacturers — all heavy users of plastic packaging
Small and medium enterprises (SMEs) operating as brand owners or manufacturers, who now have no exemption from EPR obligations
Exporters of plastic packaging or plastic-packaged goods, who face expanded definitional scope under the revised 'importer/exporter' clause
key_obligations: EPR Compliance (Ongoing): Producers, importers, and brand owners must fulfil EPR obligations for plastic packaging as per CPCB regulations issued under these rules — companies must register on the CPCB's centralised EPR portal and report collection and recycling targets on time

Registration Jurisdiction Clarity (Immediate): Entities operating across more than two states or union territories must register with the CPCB centrally; those operating within a single state register with the respective State Pollution Control Board (SPCB) or Pollution Control Committee (PCC) under Rule 9(5)

Pre-Consumer Waste Accounting (Upon Finalization): Once the rules are finalized, companies must track and report pre-consumer plastic packaging waste (waste generated during in-house manufacturing and packaging operations) as a distinct waste stream — update internal waste accounting systems accordingly

Post-Consumer Waste Collection Targets (Per CPCB Regulations): PIBOs must meet annually escalating collection and recycling targets for post-consumer plastic packaging waste as notified by CPCB — FY2024-25 onward targets have been set; verify current targets on the CPCB EPR portal

End-of-Life Pathway Validation (Ongoing): Only CPCB-approved plastic waste processors (recyclers, co-processors, waste-to-oil units) qualify for EPR credit — ensure all third-party processors in your waste management chain are registered and certified

Thickness and Specification Compliance (Ongoing): Rule 4(1)(d) amendments specify that thickness exemptions are only as notified by the government — review all plastic product specifications against current MoEFCC notifications

Draft Comment Window: The draft was open for public comments to the Secretary, MoEFCC via post or email (satyendra.kumar07@nic.in / amit.love@nic.in) within 60 days of gazette publication — industry associations should verify if final rules have been notified and check for any modifications from the draft stage
sections.climate_angle: India's plastic waste reforms are not happening in isolation — they are part of a sweeping global realignment of how plastic is governed as both a pollution crisis and a climate risk. The two are deeply connected. Plastic production is almost entirely fossil-fuel dependent; globally, plastic manufacturing accounts for approximately 3.4% of greenhouse gas emissions, and that figure is projected to grow significantly if no structural intervention occurs. The expansion of EPR to cover pre-consumer waste is directly aligned with the logic of Scope 3 emissions accounting. Just as companies are now expected under frameworks like the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) IFRS S2 and the EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) to account for emissions across their entire value chain — including raw material extraction and processing — the expanded plastic waste rules now require companies to account for packaging waste generated upstream in their supply chain, not just what the consumer discards. The recognition of waste-to-energy and co-processing as compliant end-of-life pathways also has a climate dimension that requires careful scrutiny. While these pathways recover energy value from plastic, they are not zero-carbon solutions — co-processing in cement kilns, for example, releases CO2. Companies that rely heavily on waste-to-energy to meet EPR targets should be aware that international sustainability standards (particularly the EU Taxonomy and CSRD) treat such pathways with caution and may not recognise them as genuinely circular or low-carbon solutions for disclosure purposes. Supply chain risk is the other critical lens. Companies that source plastic packaging from suppliers who are not EPR-compliant face increasing regulatory and reputational exposure. As the CPCB tightens enforcement and EPR certificate trading becomes more formalised, non-compliant supply chains will become a material liability — both in terms of regulatory penalties and in terms of ESG ratings and investor scrutiny. The physical risk dimension is equally real: plastic pollution degrades watersheds, agricultural soil, and coastal ecosystems on which India's food and textile supply chains depend. Finally, India's plastic EPR framework is converging — albeit imperfectly — with the trajectory of the UN Global Plastics Treaty negotiations, which aim to create binding international obligations on plastic production, design, and waste management. Indian companies with export exposure, particularly to the EU (where the Single-Use Plastics Directive and Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation are already in force), must treat domestic EPR compliance as a floor, not a ceiling.
what_to_do: Conduct a Plastic Packaging Audit: Map all plastic packaging used across your product portfolio — distinguish between pre-consumer waste (generated in your facilities) and post-consumer waste (generated by end consumers). Quantify volumes by category (rigid, flexible, multilayer, single-use) to establish your EPR baseline and ensure your data is ready for CPCB reporting cycles.

Verify and Update CPCB EPR Portal Registration: Confirm your registration status on the CPCB centralised EPR portal. If your operations span more than two states, ensure registration is with CPCB directly, not just state boards. Update your registration to reflect any new product lines or packaging types covered under the revised definitions.

Audit Your Plastic Waste Processor Network: Review all recyclers, co-processors, and waste processors in your EPR fulfilment chain. Confirm they are registered with CPCB or the relevant SPCB as 'plastic waste processors' under the updated definition. Processors without valid registration will not generate recognised EPR credits, leaving you non-compliant regardless of volumes collected.

Align Internal ESG Reporting with the New Waste Categories: Update your sustainability reporting templates — including BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report) disclosures for listed companies — to separately account for pre-consumer and post-consumer plastic packaging waste. This granularity is increasingly expected by ESG rating agencies and institutional investors, and will be required for alignment with ISSB IFRS S2 value chain disclosures.

Engage Legal and Government Affairs Teams to Track Final Notification: The January 2022 gazette notification was a draft open for public comment. Assign responsibility to track whether and when the final rules have been notified, and identify any changes from the draft. Given that Rule 9(1) now ties EPR obligations to periodically updated CPCB regulations, establish a standing process to monitor CPCB circulars and gazette notifications on plastic waste — this is no longer a set-and-forget compliance exercise.
sections.our_take: The Draft Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2022 represent a meaningful maturation of India's plastic governance architecture — but the most important signal for business leaders is not in any single definition or deadline. It is in the structural shift embedded in the revised Rule 9(1): EPR obligations are now explicitly dynamic, governed by regulations that will be updated 'from time to time.' This is a deliberate design choice by MoEFCC and CPCB, giving regulators the flexibility to ratchet up targets without going through the full rule-amendment process each time. For companies, this means that plastic compliance is now a live risk — one that requires continuous monitoring, not just an annual checkbox. The convergence of India's EPR framework with global standards like CSRD and the emerging UN Plastics Treaty also means that companies building robust domestic compliance systems today are, in effect, future-proofing themselves for international market access requirements. Those that treat EPR as a cost to be minimised rather than a capability to be built will find themselves structurally disadvantaged as enforcement tightens and as investors increasingly price in plastic-related liability. Our strong advice: invest now in the data infrastructure, supplier engagement, and internal governance needed to make plastic accountability a core competency — the regulatory trajectory makes this a question of when, not if.

posts.title: CPCB's Interim EPR Credit Transfer Arrangement for E-Waste Recyclers: What Producers Must Know
posts.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice establishing an interim arrangement for the generation and transfer of EPR end-product credits (certificates) by e-waste recyclers, signalling a critical transitional phase in India's e-waste compliance ecosystem. Producers, impo

sections.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice establishing an interim arrangement for the generation and transfer of EPR end-product credits (certificates) by e-waste recyclers, signalling a critical transitional phase in India's e-waste compliance ecosystem. Producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) registered under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 must pay close attention, as the validity and traceability of their EPR certificates — the backbone of their compliance proof — are directly implicated. Companies that rely on recycler-issued credits to meet their annual EPR targets face immediate operational and documentation risks if they do not adapt to the interim protocol.
sections.what_changed: Under India's E-Waste Management Rules, 2022, producers of electrical and electronic equipment are legally obligated to meet Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) targets — essentially, ensuring that a prescribed quantity of end-of-life e-waste is collected and channelled to authorised recyclers. The mechanism for proving compliance is the EPR certificate, or 'end-product credit,' which authorised recyclers generate on the CPCB's centralised EPR portal and transfer to the respective producer or importer. The CPCB's notice indicates that there is a transitional or technical gap in the existing portal-based system for credit generation and transfer — likely related to system upgrades, reconciliation of recycler registrations, or data integrity concerns on the EPR portal. To prevent a compliance vacuum during this period, CPCB has introduced an interim arrangement that prescribes a specific, time-bound process for recyclers to generate and transfer these credits outside of or alongside the standard digital workflow. In plain terms: the rules of how e-waste recyclers issue proof-of-recycling certificates to producers are temporarily modified. Both recyclers and producers must follow the interim process precisely — failing to do so could result in credits that are not recognised by CPCB for annual EPR target fulfilment, exposing producers to non-compliance penalties.
who_is_affected: Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) of electrical and electronic equipment listed under Schedule I of the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 — including consumer electronics, IT and telecom equipment, and large/small household appliances
Authorised E-Waste Recyclers registered on the CPCB EPR portal who generate and transfer end-product credits to PIBOs
E-Waste Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) that aggregate EPR obligations and credits on behalf of multiple producers
Refurbishers and dismantlers in the e-waste value chain whose outputs feed into the recycler credit system
Compliance and sustainability teams at large manufacturing conglomerates, electronics multinationals with Indian operations, and fast-moving consumer electronics brands
Environmental auditors and consultants who verify EPR compliance documentation on behalf of producers
key_obligations: E-waste recyclers must follow the CPCB-prescribed interim process for generating end-product credits — do not rely solely on the standard portal workflow until CPCB confirms the permanent arrangement is restored or updated

All credit transfers conducted during the interim period must be documented with supporting records (weight slips, invoices, recycling certificates) that can be presented to CPCB during audits or target verification

Producers and PROs must confirm with their recycling partners that credits issued during this interim phase are being generated in accordance with CPCB's notice — verbal assurances are insufficient; obtain written confirmation and copies of interim certificates

PIBOs should cross-verify that interim credits received are reflected or will be reconciled in the official CPCB EPR portal against their annual targets — proactively seek CPCB clarification if reconciliation timelines are unclear

Do not defer e-waste recycling activity or credit generation pending resolution of the interim arrangement — EPR annual targets and their associated deadlines remain unchanged under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022
Monitor the CPCB website (cpcb.nic.in) and EPR portal notifications actively for the withdrawal of the interim arrangement and restoration of standard credit generation procedures

Deadline for annual EPR target compliance: as per the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022, producers must fulfil targets for each financial year — ensure interim credits are secured well before the close of the relevant compliance year
sections.climate_angle: E-waste EPR is not merely a domestic regulatory checkbox — it sits at the intersection of circular economy policy, climate risk, and global sustainability reporting standards. Electronic equipment contains critical minerals such as cobalt, lithium, copper, and rare earth elements whose extraction is both carbon-intensive and geopolitically sensitive. A robust, credible EPR credit system ensures that these materials are recovered and re-enter the supply chain, directly reducing Scope 3 emissions associated with virgin material extraction for producers. From a climate transition perspective, any disruption or opacity in the EPR credit system undermines the integrity of circular economy claims that Indian companies are increasingly making in their sustainability reports. Under the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) framework — specifically IFRS S2 — and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), companies with global investors or European customers will face scrutiny over the traceability and verifiability of their waste management and circularity data. An interim credit arrangement that lacks clear audit trails could compromise the quality of data feeding into these disclosures. Additionally, physical climate risks — such as flooding of recycling facilities or heat-stress-driven operational disruptions — can further strain the recycler network. Companies should view this interim notice as a prompt to assess the resilience of their entire EPR supply chain, not just its current regulatory adequacy. The transition to a low-carbon, circular economy in India's electronics sector depends on a credit infrastructure that is both technically robust and climate-resilient.
what_to_do: Immediately contact your authorised e-waste recycling partners and PROs to confirm their awareness of the CPCB interim notice and verify that they are issuing credits in full compliance with the interim arrangement — request copies of all interim certificates issued on your account

Assign your compliance or legal team to download and review the original CPCB notice from the official source (cpcb.nic.in) and prepare an internal FAQ or briefing note for your operations, finance, and sustainability teams on how EPR credit documentation will be affected during this period

Conduct an immediate audit of your EPR credit position for the current financial year — calculate the gap between credits already secured and your annual target, and accelerate recycling activity if necessary to avoid a year-end shortfall exacerbated by interim processing delays

Engage your sustainability reporting team to flag this interim arrangement as a data quality risk in your ESG disclosures — if you report against GRI, BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting), or are preparing for ISSB-aligned disclosure, ensure that EPR credit traceability documentation is audit-ready

Establish a direct communication channel with CPCB's EPR cell for timely updates on when the standard credit generation and transfer process will resume, and document all correspondence for your compliance records
sections.our_take: CPCB's interim arrangement notice is a symptom of a larger growing pain: India's EPR infrastructure for e-waste is scaling rapidly, but the underlying digital systems and verification mechanisms are still catching up with the ambition of the 2022 Rules. This is not a reason for alarm, but it is a reason for vigilance. The companies that will be caught off-guard are those treating EPR compliance as a once-a-year reconciliation exercise rather than an active, year-round supply chain management discipline. We expect CPCB to continue tightening EPR enforcement — including stricter audits of recycler capacity claims and credit validity — as India aligns its circular economy framework with global standards ahead of international trade and investment scrutiny. The interim notice is, in effect, a stress test of your EPR supply chain's robustness. Use it as such.

posts.title: CPCB Puts Recyclers on Notice: What the E-Waste Management Rules 2022 Compliance Crackdown Means for Your Business
posts.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice to registered e-waste recyclers across India, signalling heightened regulatory scrutiny over compliance with the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022. Recyclers who have not met their Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations

sections.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice to registered e-waste recyclers across India, signalling heightened regulatory scrutiny over compliance with the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022. Recyclers who have not met their Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations — including collection targets, channelisation records, and certificate issuance — now face potential suspension or cancellation of registration. This notice is a clear signal that India's e-waste enforcement regime is tightening, and the entire EPR value chain — from producers to dismantlers to recyclers — must treat compliance as a board-level priority.
sections.what_changed: The E-Waste Management Rules, 2022, which came into force replacing the earlier 2016 rules, introduced a significantly more structured EPR framework. Under this framework, Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) are required to meet annual e-waste collection and recycling targets, and they must do so by sourcing EPR certificates from CPCB-registered recyclers and dismantlers. The entire system is operationalised through a centralised EPR portal managed by CPCB. The CPCB's latest urgent notice targets registered recyclers who appear to be falling short of their obligations under these rules. Non-compliances likely include failure to upload accurate processing data on the EPR portal, inability to issue valid EPR certificates to producers, inadequate record-keeping of material channelisation, and operating below permitted capacities. By issuing this notice, CPCB is formally putting recyclers on record — non-response or continued non-compliance could trigger enforcement action including show-cause notices, financial penalties, and deregistration. This is not a routine advisory. An 'Urgent Notice' classification from CPCB indicates that the regulator has already identified specific gaps and is demanding corrective action within a defined timeframe. Recyclers receiving this notice must treat it as a legal document requiring an immediate, documented response.
who_is_affected: CPCB-registered e-waste recyclers and dismantlers across all states, particularly those with pending or incomplete EPR portal submissions

Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) — including consumer electronics, IT equipment, white goods, and telecom hardware — who rely on recyclers to fulfil their own EPR targets
E-waste aggregators and collection point operators whose downstream recycling partners are affected
Companies in the electronics manufacturing sector — including contract manufacturers and component suppliers — with EPR obligations under Schedule I of the 2022 Rules
Investors and lenders with exposure to e-waste recycling businesses, where regulatory non-compliance creates material financial and reputational risk
Sustainability and ESG teams at large corporates whose Scope 3 or product stewardship disclosures depend on verified e-waste recycling data
key_obligations: Registered recyclers must ensure all e-waste processing data — quantity received, processed, and channelised — is accurately uploaded and up to date on the CPCB EPR portal (cpcbepr.parivesh.nic.in)
EPR certificates must be generated and issued to PIBOs in a timely manner upon verified processing; backlog in certificate issuance must be cleared immediately

Recyclers must maintain and be prepared to submit physical records of inward and outward material movement, including weight slips, invoices, and Form 3/Form 4 records as applicable under the 2022 Rules
Any discrepancy between declared capacity and actual throughput must be explained in a written response to CPCB, with supporting documentation
PIBOs should immediately audit their recycler partners' compliance status on the EPR portal — reliance on non-compliant recyclers puts the producer's own EPR target fulfilment at risk
Recyclers who have received the notice must respond within the timeframe specified in the notice (typically 15–30 days); failure to respond is treated as admission of non-compliance
Annual EPR targets for FY 2023–24 and FY 2024–25 must be reconciled; shortfalls may require additional collection or payment into the EPR fund as per CPCB guidelines
All recyclers should verify their registration validity and renewal status — lapsed registrations must be renewed before resuming operations or issuing certificates
sections.climate_angle: E-waste is not merely a compliance issue — it sits at the intersection of circular economy imperatives, critical mineral supply chains, and India's broader climate transition goals. Electronic devices contain recoverable quantities of gold, silver, copper, cobalt, lithium, and rare earth elements. Informal or unverified recycling not only creates toxic pollution but also means these materials are lost from the formal circular economy, increasing demand for virgin mining — a carbon-intensive process with significant biodiversity and water risks. From a global standards perspective, the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosures and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) both require companies to assess and disclose Scope 3 emissions, which include end-of-life treatment of sold products. Indian companies exporting to Europe or listed on global exchanges must be able to demonstrate that their e-waste is processed by verified, compliant recyclers — otherwise, their sustainability disclosures lack credibility and their supply chains face audit risk. India's National Action Plan on Climate Change and the country's commitment to net zero by 2070 both implicitly depend on building robust secondary materials markets. A well-functioning EPR system for e-waste is foundational infrastructure for this transition. CPCB's enforcement action, therefore, is not just about rule compliance — it is about building the institutional trust that makes India's circular economy investable and credible on the global stage. Physical climate risks are also relevant here: rising temperatures increase the rate at which electronic equipment fails and is discarded, and extreme weather events (floods, cyclones) can destroy unprocessed e-waste stockpiles, creating acute pollution incidents. Recyclers operating in climate-vulnerable geographies must factor these physical risks into their storage and operational continuity planning.
what_to_do: RECYCLERS — Conduct an immediate internal audit: Log into the CPCB EPR portal today, verify all submitted data for accuracy, identify any gaps in processing records or certificate issuance, and prepare a compliance rectification plan with clear owner accountability and a 2-week completion target

PRODUCERS & BRAND OWNERS (PIBOs) — Run a partner compliance check: Pull the list of all recyclers you have sourced EPR certificates from in FY 2023–24 and FY 2024–25 and cross-verify their active registration and portal submission status; if your recycler partners are flagged by CPCB, your own EPR fulfilment is at legal risk and you need contingency sourcing arrangements immediately

LEGAL & COMPLIANCE TEAMS — Review your notice response protocol: If your organisation has received the CPCB urgent notice, engage environmental law counsel within 48 hours, prepare a structured written response addressing each point of alleged non-compliance, and document all corrective actions taken — this response becomes part of your regulatory record

SUSTAINABILITY TEAMS — Align EPR data with ESG disclosures: Ensure that e-waste collection and recycling volumes reported in your Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR), GRI disclosures, or CDP submissions are consistent with data filed on the CPCB EPR portal; any discrepancy is a red flag in third-party audits

CFOs & RISK OFFICERS — Quantify EPR liability: Calculate the financial exposure from any shortfall in EPR targets, including potential penalties under the Environment Protection Act and the cost of purchasing additional EPR certificates in the secondary market; provision for this in your Q4 risk register if not already done
sections.our_take: This notice marks a meaningful inflection point in India's e-waste governance. For years, the EPR framework existed largely on paper — registration was obtained, targets were notionally assigned, but enforcement was inconsistent and data quality on the portal was poor. CPCB's decision to issue an urgent, public-facing compliance notice to recyclers signals that the regulator is moving from a registration-and-report model to an active enforcement posture. We expect this to be followed by physical inspections, show-cause notices to persistent non-compliers, and potentially a public blacklist of deregistered recyclers — similar to enforcement patterns we have seen in the plastic EPR space. For producers, the message is equally clear: you cannot outsource your EPR responsibility to a recycler and walk away. Due diligence on your recycling partners is now a compliance obligation, not just a best practice. Companies that build robust, auditable EPR supply chains now will be better positioned as India's environmental disclosure requirements — including SEBI's BRSR Core — become more stringent, and as global customers and investors demand verified circular economy credentials.

posts.title: ESG & Climate Compliance Digest — 22 May 2026: EPR Enforcement Intensifies Across E-Waste, Plastics & Batteries
posts.summary: Today's digest captures a significant regulatory surge across India's Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) landscape — with urgent enforcement notices targeting e-waste recyclers and producers, a second amendment to Plastic Waste Management Rules, updated EPR guidelines for plastic packaging, two

sections.summary: Today's digest captures a significant regulatory surge across India's Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) landscape — with urgent enforcement notices targeting e-waste recyclers and producers, a second amendment to Plastic Waste Management Rules, updated EPR guidelines for plastic packaging, two rounds of e-waste rule amendments, and back-to-back Battery Waste Management Rules in 2024 and 2025. The cumulative signal is unmistakable: India's Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) and the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) are closing compliance gaps and dramatically raising the cost of non-conformance. Producers, importers, brand owners, and waste processors operating in electronics, FMCG, consumer goods, EV/energy storage, and packaging-intensive sectors must treat EPR obligations as board-level risk — not a back-office checkbox.
sections.what_changed: TODAY'S HEADLINE — EPR ENFORCEMENT NOTICES (E-WASTE): Two urgent notices issued by CPCB mark the sharpest escalation in e-waste enforcement to date. The first targets registered recyclers and refurbishers under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, flagging non-submission or under-reporting of processing data and threatening suspension or cancellation of EPR certificates. The second is directed at producers — including manufacturers and importers of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) — for failure to meet annual EPR targets, file returns, or maintain valid EPR authorisations. Both notices signal a shift from advisory to punitive enforcement. PLASTIC WASTE — 2ND AMENDMENT & EPR GUIDELINES: The 2nd Amendment to the Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2022 tightens definitions around single-use plastics, revises the categorisation of plastic packaging for EPR purposes, and introduces stricter producer accountability for multilayer plastics. Simultaneously, CPCB has released revised Guidelines on EPR for Plastic Packaging, which update the EPR certificate framework, clarify the role of PROs (Producer Responsibility Organisations), and set revised recycling/end-of-life targets by plastic category. Together, these updates close several loopholes that companies had used to reduce reported plastic footprints. E-WASTE RULE AMENDMENTS — 2023 & 2024: The E-Waste Second Amendment Rules (2023) introduced revised collection targets phased by producer turnover category and tightened the dismantling-to-recycling handoff documentation requirements. The Third Amendment Rules (2024) went further — introducing mandatory deposit-refund scheme pilots for select EEE categories, stricter penalties for non-compliant recyclers, and a new requirement for producers to publish annual EPR performance reports on their websites. Read together, these amendments represent a near-complete overhaul of the 2022 framework within just two years. BATTERY WASTE MANAGEMENT RULES — 2024 & 2025: The Battery Waste Management Rules, 2024 established India's first comprehensive EPR framework specifically for batteries — covering portable, automotive, industrial, and electric vehicle batteries. The 2025 update accelerates collection and recycling targets, introduces extended producer liability for battery-embedded products (including EVs and consumer electronics), and mandates traceability through a centralised Battery Waste Management Portal. This is particularly consequential for EV OEMs, energy storage system providers, and consumer electronics brands, all of whom now carry full lifecycle responsibility for battery waste.
who_is_affected: Electronics & IT Hardware Manufacturers (laptops, phones, white goods, industrial EEE) — directly under e-waste producer EPR obligations
Importers & Brand Owners of EEE — including global brands selling through Indian subsidiaries or e-commerce channels
E-Waste Recyclers & Refurbishers — facing direct CPCB scrutiny on processing records and certificate validity
FMCG, Retail & Consumer Goods companies — under plastic packaging EPR rules, especially those using multilayer or non-recyclable packaging
EV Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) and Battery Manufacturers/Importers — battery waste EPR obligations now fully operational
Energy Storage System (ESS) Providers — industrial battery EPR targets now applicable
Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) — compliance and reporting obligations tightened across all three waste streams
E-commerce platforms acting as marketplace operators for EEE or packaged goods — increasingly in regulatory scope as deemed producers
SMEs and MSMEs in manufacturing with annual turnover above threshold limits — phased targets now apply
key_obligations: IMMEDIATE (within 30 days of notice): E-waste producers and recyclers served with CPCB urgent notices must respond with compliance documentation, EPR certificate status, and corrective action plans — failure risks certificate suspension
Q1 FY2026-27 (by 30 June 2026): Annual EPR return filing deadline for e-waste producers for FY2025-26 on the CPCB EPR Portal — non-filing triggers automatic penalty proceedings
30 June 2026: Plastic packaging EPR targets for FY2025-26 must be met and reported; revised guidelines require updated PRO agreements aligned to new recycling categories
31 July 2026: Battery waste EPR targets (2025 Rules) — annual collection and recycling obligations for FY2025-26 must be submitted via the Battery Waste Management Portal
Ongoing — FY2026-27 Targets: E-waste Third Amendment (2024) requires producers to publish EPR performance reports on corporate websites by 30 September 2026 for the previous financial year
Ongoing: Recyclers must maintain real-time digital records of inward and outward material flows; CPCB has indicated surprise audits will increase in H2 2026

Ongoing: Multilayer plastic producers must demonstrate transition roadmaps to mono-material or recyclable alternatives under the revised PWM Amendment — no hard deadline yet, but regulatory guidance expected by Q3 2026

EV OEMs: Battery traceability registration on the Battery Waste Management Portal is now mandatory for all new vehicle models; retrospective registration for existing models required by 31 December 2026
sections.climate_angle: India's escalating EPR enforcement is not occurring in isolation — it is structurally aligned with the global shift toward circular economy principles embedded in major international frameworks. The ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standard requires companies to assess physical and transition risks across their full value chain, which now unambiguously includes end-of-life waste liabilities. Companies with unresolved EPR non-compliance are, in effect, carrying unquantified transition risk on their balance sheets. The EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and its accompanying European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) require disclosure on circular economy practices, waste generation, and resource use — meaning Indian subsidiaries or supply chain partners of EU-headquartered companies will face double-barrelled pressure: Indian regulatory compliance AND international disclosure obligations. The battery waste framework, in particular, connects directly to physical climate risk. As India's EV penetration grows — driven partly by climate policy and partly by energy security — the volume of lithium-ion battery waste will grow exponentially. Poorly managed battery waste creates both toxic contamination risk (a physical risk to communities and supply chains) and a critical minerals recovery opportunity. Companies that build closed-loop battery recovery systems now will have a strategic advantage in securing recycled lithium, cobalt, and nickel as virgin material costs rise. Finally, the tightening of plastic waste rules signals regulatory alignment with India's commitments under the UN Global Plastics Treaty negotiations. Companies that treat EPR as a minimum compliance floor rather than a strategic input risk being caught flat-footed when treaty obligations translate into even stricter domestic law — likely within the next 24–36 months.
what_to_do: AUDIT EPR REGISTRATION & CERTIFICATE STATUS TODAY: Cross-check all EPR certificates (e-waste, plastic, battery) for validity, target achievement status, and filing deadlines on the CPCB portal. Identify any gaps before CPCB does — proactive disclosure is treated more favourably than reactive compliance.

ISSUE AN INTERNAL COMPLIANCE ALERT TO ALL BUSINESS UNITS: Given the multi-stream nature of today's updates (e-waste + plastics + batteries), sustainability and legal teams should immediately brief product, procurement, and operations heads on applicable obligations. Many EPR failures originate in product design and procurement decisions made without compliance input.

REVIEW PRO AND RECYCLER CONTRACTS: Validate that your registered PROs and recycling partners have valid CPCB authorisations and are meeting their processing obligations on your behalf. An invalid or suspended recycler certificate directly exposes the producer — conduct due diligence now, not at year-end.

MAP YOUR BATTERY AND PLASTIC FOOTPRINT TO THE NEW RULES: For companies in EV, consumer electronics, or FMCG sectors, conduct a rapid materiality assessment against the updated Battery Waste Rules (2025) and revised plastic EPR guidelines. Quantify your FY2026-27 EPR targets and identify whether current collection infrastructure is sufficient.

PREPARE FOR DISCLOSURE — ALIGN EPR DATA WITH ISSB/BRSR REPORTING: Begin structuring EPR performance data (collection volumes, recycling rates, EPR certificate spend) in a format compatible with Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) Core disclosures and ISSB S2 value chain metrics. Investors and lenders are increasingly requesting this data in ESG due diligence.
sections.our_take: Today's cluster of regulatory updates tells a single, coherent story: India is done with the 'awareness and registration' phase of EPR and has entered the 'enforce and penalise' phase with intent. The CPCB urgent notices — issued simultaneously against both recyclers and producers — are a deliberate signal that the regulator is tightening the full value chain at once, preventing companies from offloading liability onto downstream processors. The back-to-back amendments to e-waste and battery rules, layered over a revised plastic EPR framework, reflect a regulatory architecture that is becoming more sophisticated and more difficult to navigate through partial compliance. What's coming next? Expect CPCB to operationalise the deposit-refund scheme for select EEE categories by late FY2027, mandatory third-party EPR audits for Tier 1 producers, and the integration of EPR performance data into the BRSR Core framework for listed companies. For sustainability officers, the message is clear: EPR is no longer a peripheral compliance function — it is a material ESG risk that requires the same rigour, resourcing, and board visibility as carbon reporting.

posts.title: Battery Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2024: What India's Battery Industry Must Know Now
posts.summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change has amended the Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022 with effect from March 14, 2024, tightening the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for batteries. The amendments introduce price floors and ceilings on EPR certificates, mand

sections.summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change has amended the Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022 with effect from March 14, 2024, tightening the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for batteries. The amendments introduce price floors and ceilings on EPR certificates, mandate structured Environmental Compensation guidelines, and significantly liberalise carry-forward provisions for unmet collection targets — replacing volume-averaged calculations with a simpler 'remaining quantity' basis, uniformly capped at 60%. Producers, importers, refurbishers, and recyclers of all battery types must immediately reassess their EPR compliance strategies and portal registrations to align with the revised rules.
sections.what_changed: The Battery Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2024 make four substantive changes to the parent Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022. First, Rule 10 is amended to establish a price band for EPR certificates traded on the CPCB portal: the highest permissible price is set at 100% of the Environmental Compensation (EC) payable for non-fulfilment of EPR obligations, and the lowest permissible price is set at 30% of that EC value. This creates a regulated market corridor — EPR certificates can no longer be traded below 30% of the penalty equivalent, preventing a race to the bottom in certificate pricing. Second, a new sub-rule (18) is inserted to clarify that all certificate exchange prices between registered entities must fall within this regulated band, ensuring market integrity on the CPCB portal. Third, Rule 13(3) is rewritten to require the CPCB to formally prepare, consult on, and recommend to MoEF&CC a comprehensive guideline for the imposition and collection of Environmental Compensation from producers, refurbishers, and recyclers who fail to comply. This replaces a more open-ended provision and introduces a consultative process via the Rule 15 Implementation Committee before guidelines are finalised. Fourth, and perhaps most practically impactful, Schedule II carry-forward provisions are uniformly revised across multiple battery categories (including automotive, industrial, electric vehicle, and portable batteries under clauses vi through xii). The old language calculated carry-forward as a percentage of the average annual quantity placed in the market over multi-year cycles (which varied — 60% for some, 20% for others). The new, uniform language allows carry-forward of up to 60% of the remaining unmet quantity from the applicable compliance cycle. This shift from an average-based to a residual-based calculation is more equitable for companies whose market volumes fluctuate year to year, and it standardises the carry-forward cap at 60% across all major battery categories.
who_is_affected: Battery Producers and Manufacturers: All entities that manufacture batteries in India — including EV batteries (lithium-ion), automotive lead-acid batteries, industrial batteries, and portable consumer batteries — and are registered on the CPCB EPR portal.
Battery Importers: Companies importing batteries or battery-containing products (EVs, electronics, UPS systems, power tools) that have EPR obligations under the 2022 Rules.
Electric Vehicle (EV) OEMs: Automobile manufacturers selling EVs in India, who bear producer responsibility for the batteries placed in the market through their vehicles.

Battery Recyclers and Refurbishers: Entities engaged in collection, refurbishment, or recycling of waste batteries that participate in the EPR certificate ecosystem or are subject to Environmental Compensation for non-compliance.
EPR Certificate Traders and Intermediaries: Registered entities buying and selling EPR certificates on the CPCB portal, who must now operate within the newly defined price band.
Consumer Electronics and IT Hardware Companies: Brands placing portable battery-containing devices in the Indian market with battery EPR obligations.
Telecom Companies and Data Centre Operators: Large users of industrial and stationary batteries with take-back and EPR responsibilities.
key_obligations: Immediate (effective March 14, 2024): All EPR certificate trades on the CPCB portal must fall within the newly defined price band — minimum 30% and maximum 100% of the Environmental Compensation value applicable to the obligated entity. Any trades structured outside this band are non-compliant from the date of notification.

Ongoing — EPR Target Fulfilment: Producers must continue meeting annual collection and recycling targets as specified in Schedule II. However, for the current and future compliance cycles, the carry-forward entitlement is now calculated as 60% of the remaining (unmet) battery quantity from the applicable cycle — not as a percentage of the annual average placed in the market. Companies must recalculate their carry-forward positions using the new methodology.

Ongoing — Environmental Compensation Readiness: Await CPCB's new guidelines on Environmental Compensation imposition and collection, which the Board is now mandated to prepare in consultation with the Rule 15 Implementation Committee and submit to MoEF&CC. Once approved, these guidelines will govern penalties for non-compliance by producers, refurbishers, and recyclers.

Portal Compliance: Ensure all EPR certificate transactions are executed exclusively through registered entities on the CPCB portal at prices within the regulated band. Review existing contractual arrangements with recyclers and aggregators to ensure price terms remain compliant.

Registration Verification: Confirm that all entities in your battery waste value chain — recyclers, collection agents, refurbishers — are registered on the CPCB portal, as the price band provisions apply specifically to portal-registered entity transactions.

Documentation and Audit Trail: Update internal EPR compliance records to reflect the new carry-forward calculation methodology. Ensure your compliance management system is recalibrated before the next annual EPR reporting cycle.
sections.climate_angle: Batteries are the material backbone of India's energy transition. The country's stated target of 500 GW of renewable energy by 2030, its rapidly scaling EV ecosystem, and the electrification of industrial and telecom infrastructure all depend on a massive and growing battery supply chain. The waste that this transition generates — lithium, cobalt, nickel, lead, and other critical minerals — represents both an environmental liability and a significant circular economy opportunity. The Battery Waste Management Rules are India's primary regulatory instrument for ensuring that the climate solution does not become a waste crisis. The amendments signal regulatory maturation. By pricing EPR certificates within a defined band, the government is building a functional secondary market for battery recycling compliance — analogous to Renewable Energy Certificates (RECs) in the power sector. A credible price floor (30% of the penalty equivalent) ensures recyclers and collectors receive economically viable compensation; a ceiling (100% of penalty) prevents producers from simply buying their way out of physical collection obligations at any cost. From a global standards perspective, this framework is increasingly relevant to companies reporting under the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) IFRS S2 framework or preparing for alignment with the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). Both frameworks require disclosure of material resource and waste risks. For companies with significant battery supply chains or battery-intensive products, the cost of EPR compliance, exposure to Environmental Compensation, and the quality of take-back infrastructure are all financially material disclosures. Additionally, the EU Battery Regulation (2023), which mandates recycled content thresholds and carbon footprint declarations for batteries sold in Europe, means that Indian battery manufacturers and EV exporters face convergent regulatory pressure from both domestic and international directions. A robust domestic EPR compliance posture directly supports the due diligence documentation these exporters will need. On physical climate risk: battery recycling facilities and collection infrastructure in India face growing exposure to extreme heat, flooding, and water stress — all of which can disrupt waste battery logistics and processing. Companies building EPR compliance programmes should embed climate resilience into their collection network design, particularly in climate-vulnerable geographies.
what_to_do: Recalculate Your Carry-Forward Position Today: If you are in an active compliance cycle with unmet EPR targets, urgently recalculate your permissible carry-forward under the new 'remaining quantity' methodology (up to 60% of unmet quantity, not 60% of annual average). This will change your liability exposure and your EPR certificate procurement strategy for the remainder of the cycle. Engage your EPR compliance consultant or legal team to model both old and new calculations.

Audit All EPR Certificate Contracts and Portal Transactions: Review all existing and pipeline agreements for purchase or sale of EPR certificates. Any price terms that fall below 30% or above 100% of the applicable Environmental Compensation value are now non-compliant. Renegotiate contracts as necessary and brief your procurement and finance teams on the new price band before the next transaction.

Engage Your Recycler and Refurbisher Network: The new Rule 13(3) will bring recyclers and refurbishers under the Environmental Compensation regime, not just producers. Conduct a compliance health check with your downstream partners — are they portal-registered? Are they meeting processing standards? Their non-compliance creates reputational and supply chain risk for your EPR programme.

Monitor CPCB for Environmental Compensation Guidelines: The CPCB is now mandated to draft and recommend EC guidelines to MoEF&CC. Track CPCB notifications closely, as these guidelines will define the financial penalty structure for non-compliance. Once released, model the financial exposure under various EPR shortfall scenarios and factor this into your sustainability budget and risk register.

Integrate EPR Compliance into ESG Reporting and Transition Planning: If your company reports under BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting), GRI, or is preparing for ISSB/CSRD alignment, formally incorporate battery EPR compliance status, EC risk exposure, and recycled material recovery rates into your material topics assessment. This positions your organisation ahead of mandatory ESG disclosure requirements and supports investor-grade climate transition disclosures.
sections.our_take: The Battery Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2024 represent a quiet but consequential tightening of India's circular economy architecture for batteries. The introduction of a price band for EPR certificates is particularly significant — it transforms what was a compliance mechanism into an embryonic environmental commodity market with real price discovery, which is exactly what India needs to fund a scalable battery recycling industry. The uniform carry-forward cap of 60% on residual quantities is a pragmatic concession to industry volatility, but make no mistake: the direction of travel is toward stricter targets and higher accountability as India's EV and renewable energy markets scale exponentially. Companies that treat EPR as a box-ticking exercise — buying the cheapest certificates available and minimising engagement with actual waste streams — will find themselves exposed as the CPCB's Environmental Compensation guidelines sharpen and as international customers and investors demand verifiable circular economy credentials. The smart move is to use this regulatory moment to build genuine take-back infrastructure, lock in credible recycling partnerships, and embed battery lifecycle management into your broader climate transition strategy. Those who do will find compliance costs manageable and competitive advantage real; those who do not will face escalating penalties and reputational risk in an increasingly scrutinised sector.

posts.title: Battery Waste Management Amendment Rules, 2025: What Producers Must Do Now on EPR Labelling and Hazardous Metal Marking
posts.summary: The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the Battery Waste Management Amendment Rules, 2025 on 24 February 2025, introducing three targeted but operationally significant changes to Schedule I of the 2022 Rules. Producers of batteries and battery-integrated equipment m

sections.summary: The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the Battery Waste Management Amendment Rules, 2025 on 24 February 2025, introducing three targeted but operationally significant changes to Schedule I of the 2022 Rules. Producers of batteries and battery-integrated equipment must now print their Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) registration number as a barcode or QR code on products, packaging, or accompanying brochures — and must notify the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) in writing before doing so. Simultaneously, the amendment clarifies that mandatory hazardous-metal labelling for Cadmium ('Cd') and Lead ('Pb') does not apply where concentrations fall below defined trace thresholds, reducing compliance burden for manufacturers of modern low-hazard battery chemistries.
sections.what_changed: The Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022 established India's Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for batteries — requiring producers, importers, and brand owners to register with CPCB, meet collection and recycling targets, and label their batteries with specific information. The 2025 Amendment, the fifth modification to the 2022 Rules, makes three precise changes to Schedule I, Paragraph 2. First, an exemption has been carved out for labelling obligations under Clause (ia): packaging that is already regulated under Rule 26 of the Legal Metrology (Packaged Commodities) Rules, 2011 — which governs mandatory declarations on pre-packaged goods — will not need to duplicate those requirements under the Battery Waste Rules. This eliminates regulatory overlap for consumer-facing packaged battery products. Second, a new Clause (ib) has been inserted, offering producers a flexible pathway to fulfil EPR labelling requirements. Instead of a single mandated format, producers may now embed their EPR registration number into a barcode or QR code and place it on any one of five specified locations: the battery or battery pack itself, the equipment containing the battery, the battery's packaging, the equipment's packaging, or bulk packaging for wholesale (non-retail) batteries. Alternatively, the EPR registration number may be printed on the product information brochure. The critical procedural requirement: producers must inform CPCB in writing before opting for this alternative route. CPCB will in turn publish and quarterly-update a consolidated public list of producers who have done so on its centralised online portal. Third, the amendment brings welcome clarity on hazardous metal marking. Previously, all batteries containing Cadmium or Lead were required to display the chemical symbols 'Cd' or 'Pb'. The new proviso under Clause (v) specifies that this marking is not required when Cadmium concentration is at or below 0.002% by weight (20 parts per million) or Lead concentration is at or below 0.004% by weight (40 parts per million). This threshold-based exemption aligns India's approach more closely with international practice and reflects the reality that trace amounts of these metals in modern battery chemistries pose a categorically different risk profile than batteries where these metals are a primary component.
who_is_affected: Battery producers and importers: All entities registered or required to register under the EPR framework for batteries — including manufacturers of portable, automotive, industrial, EV traction, and stationary storage batteries.
Brand owners and OEMs: Companies that sell equipment with built-in batteries (consumer electronics, power tools, electric vehicles, UPS systems) under their own brand name and hold EPR obligations.

Bulk/wholesale battery suppliers: Entities supplying batteries or battery packs in bulk for industrial or commercial use (not for direct retail sale) must comply with QR/barcode labelling on bulk packaging.

Battery recyclers and extended supply chain: While the direct obligations are on producers, recyclers and downstream handlers benefit from improved traceability enabled by mandatory EPR registration codes on products.

Compliance and legal teams in the EV sector: Given India's rapidly scaling electric vehicle ecosystem, EV OEMs and their battery suppliers face the most material operational impact given high production volumes and complex battery pack configurations.

Consumer electronics manufacturers: Companies producing smartphones, laptops, wearables, and other devices with integrated or replaceable batteries must assess labelling on both the battery and the device/packaging.

Legal Metrology-regulated packagers: Companies whose packaging already complies with Legal Metrology (Packaged Commodities) Rules, 2011, Rule 26 gain direct relief from duplicative battery labelling requirements.
key_obligations: IMMEDIATE (effective 24 February 2025): The amendment came into force on the date of publication in the Official Gazette. All new obligations and exemptions are legally operative from this date.

EPR QR/Barcode Labelling — Prior Written Notice to CPCB: Before a producer adopts the barcode/QR code route under new Clause (ib), they must submit written intimation to the Central Pollution Control Board. This is a precondition, not a post-facto filing. Producers must not implement this labelling approach without completing this step.

EPR Registration Number Placement: Producers must print a barcode or QR code containing their CPCB EPR registration number on at least one of the following: (a) the battery or battery pack; (b) the equipment containing the battery; (c) packaging of the battery/battery pack; (d) packaging of the equipment; or (e) bulk packaging of batteries not for retail sale. Alternatively, the EPR registration number must appear on the product information brochure.

Quarterly Portal Updates: Producers who opt for the QR/barcode route must keep their details current on CPCB's centralised online portal, as CPCB will publish a consolidated and quarterly-updated list of such producers. Non-updation may result in removal from the published list, creating compliance visibility risk.

Cadmium and Lead Marking Exemption — Internal Chemical Testing: Producers seeking to avail the 'Cd'/'Pb' marking exemption must verify and document that their battery's Cadmium concentration is ≤ 0.002% by weight (20 ppm) and/or Lead concentration is ≤ 0.004% by weight (40 ppm). Internal test records must be maintained as evidence for potential regulatory scrutiny.

Legal Metrology Overlap Check: Producers whose packaging falls under Rule 26 of the Legal Metrology (Packaged Commodities) Rules, 2011 should formally document that determination to substantiate their exemption from the overlapping Battery Waste Rules labelling requirement under Clause (ia).

Review Existing EPR Registrations: Ensure your CPCB EPR registration number is current, valid, and accurately reflected in all labelling systems and product information materials before deploying QR/barcode-based compliance.
sections.climate_angle: At first glance, labelling and marking amendments may appear to be administrative housekeeping. In the context of India's climate transition, however, they carry deeper strategic significance. Batteries are the foundational technology enabling the shift from fossil fuels to clean energy — in electric vehicles, in grid-scale storage balancing intermittent solar and wind generation, and in distributed energy systems for homes and businesses. The integrity of the battery value chain — from raw material sourcing through manufacturing, use, collection, and recycling — is a critical climate infrastructure question. The QR/barcode EPR registration requirement is a traceability mechanism. When every battery in the market carries a machine-readable link to its producer's EPR registration, it becomes far easier to enforce collection and recycling targets, close leakages in the reverse logistics chain, and generate auditable data on battery material flows. This kind of product-level digital traceability is precisely what global frameworks are beginning to mandate: the European Union's Battery Regulation (EU) 2023/1542, for instance, requires a 'battery passport' — a digital record of a battery's composition, carbon footprint, supply chain provenance, and end-of-life data — for EV, industrial, and LMT batteries by 2027. India's EPR QR code requirement is an embryonic version of this concept. Companies that treat it as a checkbox exercise will find themselves unprepared when global buyers, institutional investors, and export market regulators ask for richer data. Companies that build robust digital product identity systems now — linked to bill-of-materials data, carbon footprint per cell, and recycled content claims — will be positioned to meet both domestic EPR obligations and emerging international disclosure requirements simultaneously. On physical risk: the amendment's trace-threshold exemptions for Cadmium and Lead reflect the shift in dominant battery chemistries — Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP), Nickel Manganese Cobalt (NMC), and solid-state batteries — away from historically hazardous chemistries. This chemistry transition is itself climate-driven, as energy density, cycle life, and thermal safety improvements accelerate electrification. Compliance teams should track chemical composition as a live variable, not a static product specification, given how rapidly battery technology is evolving. From a supply chain disclosure perspective, SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) Core framework — mandatory for the top 150 listed companies from FY 2023-24 — requires value chain disclosures on environmental metrics. Battery producers and their upstream mineral suppliers (Lithium, Cobalt, Manganese) face growing pressure to disclose Scope 3 emissions and hazardous waste management practices. ISSB's IFRS S2 Climate Disclosures and the EU's CSRD (applicable to Indian subsidiaries of European companies and Indian companies accessing EU capital markets) both require disclosure of climate-related physical and transition risks that are directly relevant to battery manufacturers operating in a rapidly decarbonising economy.
what_to_do: Audit your current EPR registration status and labelling: Immediately verify that your CPCB EPR registration number is valid and active. Map all current product labels, packaging designs, and brochures to identify where the EPR number currently appears — and whether a barcode/QR code integration is feasible in your next production cycle.

Submit written intimation to CPCB before switching to QR/barcode labelling: If your organisation chooses to adopt the new QR/barcode pathway under Clause (ib), draft and dispatch formal written notice to CPCB before any product bearing the new label format reaches the market. Work with your legal and regulatory affairs team to ensure this notification is filed correctly and acknowledged.

Conduct battery chemistry testing and documentation for Cd/Pb exemption: Commission or review existing test reports for Cadmium and Lead concentration in your battery products. If concentrations are at or below the defined thresholds (Cd ≤ 20 ppm, Pb ≤ 40 ppm), formally document this finding and instruct your labelling team to apply the exemption — while retaining test evidence for at least the duration of your regulatory record-keeping obligations.

Establish a quarterly CPCB portal update process: Assign ownership within your compliance or regulatory function to ensure your producer details on CPCB's centralised portal are reviewed and updated every quarter. Treat this as a recurring calendar obligation, not a one-time registration activity, to remain on CPCB's published consolidated producer list.

Begin building a battery digital traceability roadmap: Use this amendment as the trigger to initiate a broader conversation internally about digital product identity for batteries. Evaluate whether your QR code can be enriched over time to carry carbon footprint data, recycled content claims, and end-of-life instructions — positioning your organisation ahead of the EU Battery Regulation passport requirements and future BRSR value chain disclosure expectations.
sections.our_take: The Battery Waste Management Amendment Rules, 2025 are incremental in legal scope but directionally significant. India's battery EPR framework is still relatively young, and each amendment is systematically closing gaps — reducing regulatory duplication with Legal Metrology rules, providing operational flexibility on labelling formats, and calibrating hazardous-substance marking to reflect the chemistry realities of modern batteries. What stands out is the emphasis on digital traceability: mandating EPR registration numbers in machine-readable QR/barcode format is a meaningful step toward a data-driven circular economy for batteries. For compliance teams, the immediate priorities are clear — CPCB notification before label changes, chemistry testing for metal thresholds, and portal hygiene. But the strategic message is larger: the global battery regulatory environment is tightening rapidly, driven by the EU Battery Regulation, ISSB climate disclosure standards, and the supply chain scrutiny of institutional investors. Indian battery producers and OEMs that invest now in robust product identity, traceability infrastructure, and chemistry transparency will not merely be compliant — they will be competitive in the markets and supply chains that matter most over the next decade.

posts.title: E-Waste Second Amendment Rules, 2023: What India's Electronics and Refrigeration Industries Must Do Now
posts.summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the E-Waste (Management) Second Amendment Rules, 2023 on July 25, 2023, amending the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022. The amendment introduces three critical changes: mandatory safe management of refrigerants generated dur

sections.summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the E-Waste (Management) Second Amendment Rules, 2023 on July 25, 2023, amending the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022. The amendment introduces three critical changes: mandatory safe management of refrigerants generated during the manufacture and dismantling of air-conditioning and cooling equipment; revised timelines for phasing out hazardous substances (RoHS compliance) across newly classified equipment schedules; and new conversion factors for Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) certificates covering multiple recycling end-products. Companies across electronics manufacturing, HVAC, healthcare equipment, and IT hardware sectors must immediately assess their compliance posture against these updated obligations.
sections.what_changed: The E-Waste (Management) Second Amendment Rules, 2023 amend the parent E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 in four substantive areas, effective from the date of gazette notification (July 25, 2023). First, refrigerant management obligations have been inserted into three separate rules — Rule 5 (manufacturers), Rule 7 (dealers/refurbishers), and Rule 9 (dismantlers/recyclers) — requiring that refrigerants generated during the manufacturing process as well as during the dismantling of old air-conditioning and cooling equipment be managed in a safe, leak-proof, and sustainable manner, using only CPCB-approved destruction technologies and following CPCB-issued guidelines.
