Second, the hazardous substance (RoHS) phase-out timeline has been restructured. Equipment placed on the market before May 1, 2014 (listed under the new Schedule II-B) remains exempt from RoHS restrictions where compliant spare parts are unavailable. Critically, a new Schedule II-C has been created for a broader category of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) — including advanced telecom, IT, and consumer electronics — placed on the market on or before April 1, 2025, with a further exemption for components and spare parts required for this equipment extending until April 1, 2028. Third, for recyclers generating multiple end-products from a single waste stream, conversion factors will now be determined by CPCB (with Steering Committee approval) to enable proportionate EPR certificate generation across all or some end-products — removing a previous ambiguity that disadvantaged recyclers handling complex material streams. Fourth, Schedule II-A has been introduced to carve out specific exemptions for medical and laboratory monitoring and control equipment, listing 24 categories of applications where lead, cadmium, and mercury are permitted in tightly defined technical uses — such as X-ray tubes, MRI superconductors, pH electrodes, and ultrasonic transducers — aligning India's approach with international RoHS exemption frameworks.
who_is_affected: HVAC and Refrigeration Equipment Manufacturers: Companies manufacturing air conditioners, chillers, cold-storage units, and refrigeration systems — now obligated to manage refrigerants generated during production using CPCB-approved destruction technologies.

E-Waste Dismantlers and Recyclers: Facilities dismantling end-of-life air-conditioning and cooling equipment must ensure refrigerant recovery and safe disposal as per CPCB guidelines; recyclers generating multiple end-products gain clarity on EPR certificate conversion factors.

Consumer Electronics and IT Hardware Manufacturers and Importers: Companies placing desktops, laptops, notebooks, notepads, printers, copiers, telephones, and televisions on the Indian market — particularly those in the transition window to Schedule II-C — must track their RoHS compliance timelines.

Telecom Equipment Manufacturers and Importers: Products transmitting voice, image, or data via telecommunications (newly classified under Schedule II-C, Code ITEW17) face the April 1, 2025 market-placement threshold for RoHS exemption.

Medical Device and Laboratory Equipment Manufacturers: Companies producing X-ray systems, MRI machines, ultrasonic diagnostic equipment, pH meters, and portable defibrillators benefit from the new Schedule II-A exemptions but must document and justify their use of restricted substances against the listed categories.

Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) Portal Registrants: All producers registered under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 need to understand how the new conversion factor provisions affect their EPR certificate calculations and annual compliance filings.

Large Format Retailers and E-Commerce Platforms: As facilitators of EEE sales, they must ensure producer partners are compliant with the revised schedules applicable to products listed on their platforms.
key_obligations: Effective Immediately (July 25, 2023): Manufacturers of refrigeration and air-conditioning equipment must ensure that refrigerants generated during the manufacturing process are managed safely, in a leak-proof and sustainable manner, using CPCB-approved destruction technologies — as per new Rule 5(4).

Effective Immediately (July 25, 2023): Dismantlers and recyclers of old refrigeration and air-conditioning equipment must comply with new Rule 7(5) and Rule 9(11), mandating safe, leak-proof, and sustainable management of refrigerants from end-of-life equipment using CPCB-approved processes.

Effective Immediately (July 25, 2023): Recyclers handling e-waste that generates multiple end-products must engage with CPCB to understand the new conversion factor framework under Rule 14(1)(ii)(c) and adjust their EPR certificate generation methodology accordingly.

By April 1, 2025: Manufacturers and importers of Schedule II-C EEE (including advanced telecom and IT equipment) must note that products placed on the market after this date will no longer benefit from the RoHS exemption applicable to Schedule II-C equipment — full RoHS compliance will be required.

By April 1, 2028: Even for Schedule II-C equipment placed on market on or before April 1, 2025, all components, consumables, and spare parts must meet RoHS hazardous substance restrictions by this date, unless compliant alternatives remain unavailable.

Ongoing: Medical and laboratory equipment manufacturers must maintain documentation demonstrating that use of restricted substances (lead, cadmium, mercury) falls within the specific Schedule II-A exemption categories — this is not a blanket exemption and each application must be individually justified.

Ongoing: All regulated entities must monitor CPCB for issuance of specific guidelines on approved refrigerant destruction technologies, as the rules reference CPCB guidelines that are yet to be formally published in their final form.
sections.climate_angle: The refrigerant management provisions in this amendment are far more consequential for India's climate commitments than they might first appear. Refrigerants used in air-conditioning and cooling equipment — particularly hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs) — are among the most potent greenhouse gases, with global warming potentials (GWPs) hundreds to thousands of times higher than CO2. India is a signatory to the Kigali Amendment to the Montreal Protocol, which mandates a phased reduction of HFC consumption. The amendment's requirement for safe, leak-proof, and sustainable refrigerant management directly supports India's Kigali compliance obligations and its Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) under the Paris Agreement. From a physical risk perspective, India's rapidly expanding cooling demand — driven by rising temperatures, urbanisation, and income growth — means that refrigerant leakage during manufacturing and end-of-life dismantling represents a growing emissions source. The International Energy Agency estimates that the cooling sector could account for 45% of India's electricity demand by 2050. Poorly managed refrigerant recovery today creates a significant and measurable Scope 1 emissions liability for manufacturers, and a Scope 3 liability for companies throughout the cooling equipment value chain. On the disclosure front, global reporting frameworks are converging on refrigerant management as a material climate issue. The ISSB's IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures) requires companies to disclose material Scope 1 emissions, which include direct refrigerant releases. The EU's CSRD, which Indian exporters and subsidiaries of European multinationals must increasingly navigate, requires detailed value-chain emissions reporting under ESRS E1. India's own SEBI BRSR Core framework for top-listed companies is evolving in the same direction. Companies that treat this amendment merely as a waste management compliance issue — rather than as a climate and ESG disclosure issue — will find themselves underprepared for the next wave of mandatory climate reporting. The RoHS schedule restructuring also has supply chain implications. As India integrates more deeply into global electronics supply chains — particularly under the Production Linked Incentive (PLI) schemes — adherence to hazardous substance restrictions is a baseline market access requirement for exports to the EU, UK, and increasingly Southeast Asian markets. The new Schedule II-C timelines give Indian manufacturers a transition window, but global buyers and EPR scheme administrators in export markets will not extend the same flexibility.
what_to_do: Conduct a Refrigerant Management Audit: HVAC and refrigeration manufacturers and dismantlers should immediately audit their current refrigerant handling procedures during both production and end-of-life processing. Map all points of refrigerant release, quantify volumes, and assess whether current recovery and destruction methods align with CPCB's forthcoming guidelines. Engage a certified environmental consultant to identify gaps and implement a corrective action plan.

Review and Reclassify Your Product Portfolio Against Updated Schedules: Legal, compliance, and product management teams should jointly review all EEE product lines against the revised Schedule II-B and the new Schedule II-C. Identify which products fall under which schedule, note the applicable RoHS exemption windows (April 1, 2025 and April 1, 2028), and create a product-level compliance roadmap to eliminate restricted substances from components and spare parts before deadlines lapse.

Update EPR Registrations and Certificate Calculations: Recyclers and producers registered on the CPCB EPR portal should review the implications of the new conversion factor provisions under Rule 14(1)(ii)(c). Engage with CPCB to clarify how conversion factors will be determined for your specific waste streams and end-products, and update your EPR compliance model accordingly to avoid certificate shortfalls or over-reporting.

Build Refrigerant Management into Climate and ESG Disclosures: Sustainability and CFO teams should begin quantifying refrigerant-related GHG emissions (particularly HFC releases) as part of their Scope 1 inventory, in preparation for BRSR Core disclosures and any voluntary ISSB/TCFD-aligned reporting. This positions the company ahead of mandatory climate disclosure requirements rather than scrambling to comply retrospectively.

Monitor CPCB for Implementing Guidelines: The amendment makes several provisions conditional on CPCB issuing specific guidelines — particularly on approved refrigerant destruction technologies. Assign a regulatory affairs team member to actively track CPCB notifications and circulars, and ensure that once guidelines are published, internal SOPs are updated within 60 days.
sections.our_take: The E-Waste Second Amendment Rules, 2023 are deceptively significant. On the surface, they look like a technical amendment — tweaking schedules, inserting sub-rules, and adjusting deadlines. But taken together, these changes signal that India's regulatory architecture around electronic and electrical waste is maturing rapidly, and is beginning to align with both the Kigali Protocol's refrigerant phase-down agenda and the global convergence on RoHS-equivalent hazardous substance restrictions. The refrigerant management clauses in particular deserve board-level attention in the HVAC and cooling sector — not because they impose immediate penalties, but because they establish the legal foundation for what will almost certainly become a more stringent, monitored, and reported regime as India's climate commitments deepen. The companies that treat these provisions as an opportunity to build best-in-class refrigerant recovery infrastructure today will gain a durable competitive advantage in both domestic and export markets. Those that wait for CPCB enforcement action will find themselves managing a compliance crisis and a reputational risk simultaneously. The window to get ahead of this is open — but it is narrowing.

posts.title: E-Waste Third Amendment Rules, 2024: What India's Electronics Industry Must Know About EPR Certificate Trading and New Compliance Flexibilities
posts.summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change notified the E-Waste (Management) Amendment Rules, 2024 on March 8, 2024, introducing three significant changes to the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022: a clarified definition of 'dismantler,' a formal government power to extend filing deadl

sections.summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change notified the E-Waste (Management) Amendment Rules, 2024 on March 8, 2024, introducing three significant changes to the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022: a clarified definition of 'dismantler,' a formal government power to extend filing deadlines by up to nine months, and — most consequentially — a structured marketplace for trading Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) certificates, complete with price floors and ceilings set by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB). For manufacturers, producers, importers, refurbishers, and recyclers of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE), these amendments create both new compliance pathways and new strategic opportunities. Companies that move quickly to understand the EPR certificate trading mechanism will be better positioned to manage their obligations cost-effectively while staying ahead of tightening enforcement.
sections.what_changed: The E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 established India's current Extended Producer Responsibility framework for electronics, requiring producers and manufacturers to ensure that a defined percentage of the e-waste generated by their products is collected and recycled each year. The Third Amendment, effective from March 8, 2024, modifies three specific provisions of those rules. First, the definition of 'dismantler' (Rule 3, clause f) has been tightened. A dismantler is now explicitly defined as any person or entity engaged in dismantling used electrical and electronic equipment and components in accordance with CPCB guidelines. This alignment with CPCB standards raises the bar for who qualifies as a legitimate dismantler in the EPR ecosystem — a critical point for producers who source dismantling services to fulfil their obligations. Second, a new Rule 9A grants the Central Government the power to relax, by order, any return or report filing deadline under the rules for manufacturers, producers, refurbishers, or recyclers — for a further period not exceeding nine months. This is a practical relief mechanism, acknowledging that the EPR portal and compliance infrastructure sometimes face operational challenges, and providing a formal legal basis for deadline extensions rather than ad hoc administrative orders. Third, and most strategically significant, sub-rules (7) through (10) have been inserted into Rule 15, creating the legal architecture for a formal EPR Certificate Exchange Platform. The Central Government may establish one or more platforms for the exchange or transfer of EPR certificates, operated under CPCB-recommended guidelines. Critically, the CPCB will set price bands for certificate trading: the ceiling price will equal 100% of the environmental compensation (penalty) for non-fulfilment of EPR obligations under Rule 22, and the floor price will equal 30% of that same environmental compensation. All trades between registered entities must occur through the portal and within these price bands.
who_is_affected: Producers and manufacturers of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) covered under Schedule I of the E-Waste Rules — including consumer electronics, IT and telecom equipment, large and small household appliances, lighting equipment, and medical devices
Importers of EEE who are classified as 'producers' under the rules and carry EPR obligations for imported products placed on the Indian market
Refurbishers of used EEE who are required to file annual returns and meet EPR targets
Recyclers and dismantlers authorised under the CPCB framework, whose eligibility to generate EPR certificates depends on meeting the revised CPCB guideline-aligned definition of 'dismantler'

Extended Producer Responsibility Authorisation (EPRA) holders — both large multinational electronics brands and Indian manufacturers — who buy or sell EPR certificates to meet annual collection and recycling targets

Compliance and sustainability teams at fast-moving consumer electronics companies, white goods manufacturers, laptop and mobile device brands, and EV component producers (where applicable under EEE schedules)
EPR aggregators and intermediaries who facilitate certificate transactions on behalf of smaller producers
key_obligations: Effective immediately (March 8, 2024): The amended definition of 'dismantler' is in force — producers must verify that their dismantling partners operate strictly in accordance with CPCB guidelines; contracts or empanelment agreements with dismantlers should be reviewed and updated accordingly

Effective immediately (March 8, 2024): The Rule 9A relaxation mechanism is available — however, deadline extensions are not automatic; they require a Central Government order, so companies should not assume extensions will be granted and must continue filing returns on time unless a specific order is issued

Upcoming (timeline to be notified): Watch for Central Government orders establishing the EPR Certificate Exchange Platform under Rule 15(7) — the legal basis now exists, but the platform's operational launch depends on CPCB issuing guidelines and the government notifying the platform; companies should monitor the CPCB portal and MoEFCC notifications closely

Upcoming (to be fixed by CPCB): The CPCB will publish the highest and lowest price bands for EPR certificate trading — the ceiling is pegged at 100% of the Rule 22 environmental compensation and the floor at 30%; once notified, these prices will govern all certificate transactions

Ongoing: Producers must continue to meet annual EPR targets for e-waste collection and recycling as per their EPRA; the new trading platform, once live, will provide an additional compliance route but does not replace the obligation to achieve targets

Ongoing: All EPR certificate exchanges must occur through the designated government portal between registered entities only — off-portal or informal certificate transactions will not be recognised for compliance purposes
sections.climate_angle: The introduction of a regulated EPR certificate trading market for e-waste is far more than a procedural compliance update — it is a structural step toward embedding circular economy principles into India's electronics value chain, with direct relevance to climate transition planning. E-waste is one of the fastest-growing waste streams globally, and its improper management releases hazardous substances — including persistent organic pollutants and heavy metals — that degrade ecosystems and contribute to soil and water contamination, compounding physical climate risks already facing vulnerable communities. By formalising a price-signalling mechanism through certificate trading, the amendment creates a financial incentive for producers to invest in legitimate, high-quality recycling infrastructure rather than relying on the informal sector. From a global standards perspective, this development sits squarely within the scope of what the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) — through IFRS S1 and S2 — and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) require companies to disclose: namely, how they manage their waste obligations, circular economy commitments, and transition risks in their supply chains. For Indian subsidiaries of global electronics companies already reporting under CSRD or preparing for ISSB-aligned disclosures, the EPR certificate trading mechanism will become a material data point — demonstrating that India's regulatory framework is converging with global circular economy expectations. Supply chain implications are equally significant. The tightened dismantler definition aligns with the principle that only verified, standard-compliant facilities should handle hazardous e-waste — a concern directly connected to Scope 3 value chain emissions and responsible sourcing commitments. As buyers of EPR certificates, producers are effectively co-financing India's formal recycling ecosystem, which supports recovery of critical minerals (lithium, cobalt, rare earths) essential for the energy transition. Building robust e-waste recycling infrastructure today is an investment in domestic critical mineral security tomorrow.
what_to_do: Audit your dismantling partners immediately: Cross-check all empanelled dismantlers against current CPCB guidelines to confirm they meet the revised Rule 3(f) definition; any dismantler not operating in strict accordance with CPCB standards could invalidate the EPR credits generated through their activities — update vendor agreements and conduct physical or documentation-based audits as needed

Model your EPR certificate position and price exposure: Using the Rule 22 environmental compensation as the benchmark, calculate the implied ceiling and floor prices for EPR certificates that the CPCB is likely to set; stress-test your annual EPR budget against a scenario where you need to purchase certificates at the ceiling price — this will inform procurement strategy once the exchange platform launches

Engage with the CPCB portal and industry associations proactively: The EPR Certificate Exchange Platform is coming; register your interest, ensure your EPRA and portal credentials are current, and participate in any CPCB consultation processes on platform guidelines — early registered entities will have an advantage in navigating the new market

Integrate EPR compliance data into your sustainability reporting framework: If your organisation reports under BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report), GRI, or is preparing for ISSB/CSRD-aligned disclosures, begin capturing EPR certificate generation, purchase, and surrender data as a formal metric — this positions your company ahead of mandatory ESG disclosure requirements and demonstrates circular economy accountability to investors

Brief your legal and compliance teams on Rule 9A — but do not rely on it: The nine-month extension power is a safety valve, not a routine tool; treat filing deadlines as firm, document your compliance calendar, and only engage the Rule 9A mechanism through proper channels if a genuine operational barrier arises — proactive compliance is always preferable to seeking ex-post relief
sections.our_take: The E-Waste Third Amendment Rules, 2024 may appear modest in length — just four clauses — but they signal a maturing of India's EPR governance architecture that deserves serious strategic attention. The introduction of a price-banded EPR certificate exchange is India's most explicit move yet toward a market-based environmental compliance mechanism in the waste sector, drawing conceptually from models seen in carbon markets and plastic credit systems. The design — with a ceiling anchored to the penalty for non-compliance and a floor set at 30% of that penalty — is pragmatic: it prevents both market cornering at artificially high prices and a race to the bottom that would undermine the incentive to actually collect and recycle e-waste. What comes next will be decisive: the quality of CPCB's operational guidelines for the platform, the robustness of the portal's verification infrastructure, and the government's willingness to enforce against off-portal trades will determine whether this becomes a genuine driver of formal recycling capacity or another compliance checkbox. For forward-thinking companies, the message is clear — build your EPR strategy now, invest in verified recycling partnerships, and position yourself as a net seller of certificates rather than a distressed buyer. The companies that treat EPR as a supply chain and circular economy opportunity, rather than a regulatory cost, will be best placed as India's environmental compliance landscape continues its rapid convergence with global standards.

posts.title: India's EPR Framework for Plastic Packaging: What Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners Must Do Now
posts.summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) has embedded legally binding Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) guidelines for plastic packaging directly into the Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2022, effective February 16, 2022. Producers, importers, and brand ow

sections.summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) has embedded legally binding Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) guidelines for plastic packaging directly into the Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2022, effective February 16, 2022. Producers, importers, and brand owners are now required to collect, channel, and ensure the recycling or end-of-life processing of both pre-consumer and post-consumer plastic packaging waste — with specific roles, targets, and accountability mechanisms enforced by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB). Non-compliance carries significant regulatory, reputational, and supply-chain risk, particularly as global ESG disclosure standards increasingly demand transparency on plastic footprints.
sections.what_changed: Prior to this amendment, EPR obligations for plastic packaging under the Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 were governed by guidelines issued on an ad hoc basis — meaning the rules could shift without formal gazette notification, creating ambiguity for businesses. The 2022 amendment changes this fundamentally: the EPR guidelines are now codified as Schedule II of the PWM Rules, giving them the full force of law. In plain terms, this means that any company that manufactures, imports, or sells products using plastic packaging — from carry bags to multi-layer packaging to plastic sheets — can no longer treat EPR as a voluntary or loosely monitored obligation. The guidelines define precisely who is responsible (producers, importers, brand owners), what they must do (collect and channel pre- and post-consumer plastic packaging waste for recycling or end-of-life processing), and which authority oversees compliance (CPCB at the national level, State Pollution Control Boards and Pollution Control Committees at the state level). The amendment also introduces critical definitions that determine who falls within scope. 'Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)' is defined as a producer's responsibility for environmentally sound management of a product until its end of life. 'Brand Owner' covers any individual or company selling commodities under a registered brand, label, or trademark. 'Importer' includes anyone importing plastic packaging products, carry bags, multilayer packaging, or plastic sheets. Together, these definitions cast a wide net across India's manufacturing and consumer goods ecosystem.
who_is_affected: Producers: Manufacturers of carry bags, multilayer packaging, plastic sheets, and similar products — including industries that use plastic sheets or multilayer packaging to wrap or package goods for sale.
Importers: Any entity importing plastic packaging products, carry bags, multilayer packaging, plastic sheets, or similar materials into India.

Brand Owners: Any individual or company that sells commodities under a registered brand, label, or trademark — this includes FMCG, food & beverage, pharmaceuticals, personal care, e-commerce, and retail companies whose products reach consumers in plastic packaging.

Recyclers and Plastic Waste Processors: Entities engaged in recycling plastic waste or converting it into energy (waste-to-energy), oil (waste-to-oil), or industrial composting — they are assigned specific roles in the EPR ecosystem.

Sectors with highest exposure: Fast-Moving Consumer Goods (FMCG), food processing, beverages, pharmaceuticals, personal care and cosmetics, e-commerce and logistics, agriculture (plastic mulch films), and retail.

Company size: The rules apply regardless of company size — small manufacturers and importers are not exempt, though compliance pathways (such as Producer Responsibility Organisations, or PROs) can ease the burden for smaller entities.
key_obligations: Immediate effect (February 16, 2022): The EPR guidelines under Schedule II came into force on the date of gazette notification. All ongoing EPR processes must be aligned with these guidelines without delay.

EPR Registration: Producers, importers, and brand owners must register on the CPCB's centralised EPR portal to obtain EPR certificates. Operating without registration exposes companies to enforcement action.

Collection and Channelisation Targets: Entities must meet prescribed annual targets for collection and channelisation of plastic packaging waste — both pre-consumer waste (manufacturing rejects and trimmings) and post-consumer waste (packaging discarded by end consumers). Targets are category-specific and increase progressively year on year.

Plastic Packaging Categories: Companies must classify their plastic packaging under the prescribed categories (e.g., Category I: rigid plastic packaging; Category II: flexible plastic packaging including carry bags and multilayer packaging; Category III: multilayer plastic; Category IV: plastic sheets and like products) as targets and obligations vary by category.

Use of Recycled Plastic Content: Companies are required to progressively incorporate recycled plastic content into their packaging as mandated — this is a supply-side obligation that requires early engagement with certified recyclers.

EPR Certificate Procurement: Where companies cannot meet collection targets through their own systems, they must procure EPR certificates from registered recyclers or plastic waste processors through the CPCB portal — this is the compliance offset mechanism.
Annual Reporting: Entities must file annual returns with CPCB/SPCBs detailing plastic packaging placed on the market, waste collected, recycled, and processed, along with EPR certificates held.

End-of-Life Processing: Plastic waste that cannot be recycled must be directed to approved end-of-life processing routes — waste-to-energy, co-processing in cement kilns, waste-to-oil, or road construction as per Indian Roads Congress guidelines. Open burning and landfilling are not compliant disposal routes.

Non-compliance: Failure to meet EPR targets or register attracts penalties under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986, including fines, production stoppages, and public disclosure of non-compliance by CPCB.
sections.climate_angle: India's EPR framework for plastic packaging is not just a waste management regulation — it sits at the intersection of the circular economy, supply chain decarbonisation, and global climate disclosure requirements. From a physical risk perspective, plastic pollution degrades ecosystems, contaminates water bodies, and contributes to microplastic accumulation in soil and water — risks that directly impair agricultural productivity and water security, both of which are material climate adaptation concerns for India. By mandating collection and recycling, the EPR rules reduce the volume of plastic entering natural systems. On the transition risk and opportunity side, the shift to recycled plastic content in packaging aligns directly with the global push for circular economy principles embedded in frameworks like the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standard. Multinational brand owners sourcing from or selling into India are already subject to supply chain due diligence requirements that include plastic footprint disclosure. India's EPR rules create the domestic infrastructure — registered recyclers, certified EPR certificates, traceable collection chains — that makes this disclosure credible and auditable. For investors, EPR compliance is increasingly a proxy for broader ESG governance quality. Companies that build robust EPR systems now are better positioned to respond to Scope 3 value chain disclosures, ESG ratings scrutiny, and export market sustainability requirements — particularly as the EU's Green Deal tightens import standards on packaging sustainability. The SEBI Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework already requires India's top listed companies to disclose plastic waste management practices, making EPR compliance a direct input to mandatory ESG reporting.
what_to_do: Conduct a plastic packaging audit immediately: Map every SKU and product line to identify the type, category, and volume of plastic packaging placed on the market annually — this baseline is the foundation of your EPR target calculation and BRSR disclosure.

Register on the CPCB EPR portal without delay: If your company has not already registered as a producer, importer, or brand owner, initiate registration now. Retroactive compliance gaps increase regulatory exposure, especially as CPCB ramps up enforcement.

Engage certified recyclers and PROs: Build or formalise relationships with CPCB-registered recyclers or Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) who can help meet collection targets and supply EPR certificates. Do not wait until year-end — recycler capacity is finite and early movers secure better terms.

Embed recycled content targets into procurement and packaging design: Work with packaging R&D and procurement teams to incorporate recycled plastic content into future packaging specifications. This is both a regulatory requirement and a competitive differentiator for export markets and sustainability-conscious buyers.

Integrate EPR obligations into BRSR and ESG disclosures: Align your EPR data — tonnes of plastic placed on market, collected, recycled, EPR certificates held — with your SEBI BRSR reporting and any voluntary ISSB/GRI disclosures. Investors and ESG raters are increasingly scrutinising this data for accuracy and completeness.
sections.our_take: The codification of EPR guidelines into Schedule II of the Plastic Waste Management Rules is a watershed moment for India's plastic governance landscape. By elevating what were previously administrative guidelines to statutory rules, the government has removed ambiguity and significantly raised the compliance bar. What this signals clearly is that India is moving from a framework of stated intent to one of measurable accountability — with CPCB's centralised digital portal creating a real-time audit trail that makes enforcement far more credible than it was under the pre-2022 regime. For business leaders, the message is unambiguous: EPR is no longer a checkbox exercise managed by the environment team in isolation. It demands cross-functional coordination across procurement, packaging design, supply chain, finance, and ESG reporting. Companies that invest now in building traceable, certified EPR systems will not only stay ahead of domestic enforcement but will also be better prepared for the next wave of obligations — which is likely to include category-specific recycled content mandates, EPR for categories beyond packaging, and integration with India's broader green taxonomy and sustainable finance frameworks. The question is not whether to comply, but how quickly and how strategically you build the capability to do so.

posts.title: India's 2nd Amendment to Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2022: What Producers, Importers & Brand Owners Must Know
posts.summary: The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the Plastic Waste Management (Second Amendment) Rules, 2022 on July 7, 2022, introducing critical definitional clarity and strengthening the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for plastic packaging. The amendment

sections.summary: The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the Plastic Waste Management (Second Amendment) Rules, 2022 on July 7, 2022, introducing critical definitional clarity and strengthening the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for plastic packaging. The amendment formally defines key concepts — including biodegradable plastics, plastic packaging, post-consumer and pre-consumer plastic packaging waste, waste-to-energy, and recycled plastic use — bringing India's regulatory vocabulary closer to global standards. Producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) must take immediate note: EPR obligations under Schedule II are now explicitly mandatory, and registration requirements with the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) have been tightened for entities operating across multiple states.
sections.what_changed: The Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 have been amended for the second time in 2022 (the first amendment came in January 2022). This amendment, effective from the date of its publication in the Gazette — July 7, 2022 — makes targeted but consequential changes across Rules 3, 4, and 9 of the parent rules. At its core, the amendment does three things. First, it dramatically expands the definitional framework in Rule 3 by adding or refining over a dozen terms. These include 'biodegradable plastic' (distinct from compostable plastic, certified by CPCB under BIS standards, and required to degrade without leaving microplastics or toxic residues), 'plastic packaging,' 'post-consumer plastic packaging waste,' 'pre-consumer plastic packaging waste,' 'plastic waste processor,' 'recycler,' 'reuse,' 'use of recycled plastic,' 'waste-to-energy,' and 'end-of-life disposal.' This definitional expansion is not merely academic — it creates clear legal categories that determine which obligations apply to which actors. Second, the amendment updates Rule 4 on prohibitions and restrictions: references to biodegradable plastics are now included alongside compostable plastics in provisions relating to carry bags and packaging, and the applicable Indian Standard is updated from IS 17088:2008 to IS/ISO 17088:2021, aligning with the latest international compostability standard. Third, and most critically for business compliance, Rule 9 on EPR is substantially revised. Sub-rule (1) is replaced to make explicit that producers, importers, and brand owners shall fulfil EPR obligations for plastic packaging as specified in Schedule II. References to collection plans and a two-year implementation window have been removed, signalling that the grace period is over — EPR is now a present, enforceable obligation. The amendment also clarifies that entities operating in more than two states or union territories must register with the CPCB (not just state pollution control boards), centralising oversight for larger national players.
who_is_affected: Producers: Manufacturers of plastic packaging materials, plastic carry bags, multilayer packaging, and plastic sheets — regardless of sector
Importers: Any entity importing plastic packaging, products packaged in plastic, carry bags, multilayer packaging, or plastic sheets into India
Brand Owners (including those with registered trademarks): Companies that sell products under their own brand in plastic packaging — this now explicitly includes trademark holders
Plastic Waste Processors: Units engaged in recycling, co-processing (e.g., in cement or steel plants), waste-to-oil conversion, or waste-to-energy operations
Recyclers: Units involved in the recycling of plastic waste, including industrial composting
Fast-Moving Consumer Goods (FMCG) sector: High exposure due to large volumes of plastic packaging across product categories
E-commerce companies: Significant users of plastic packaging for logistics and delivery — now more clearly captured under the brand owner definition
Retail and organised trade: Entities using carry bags or packaging under their own brand or trademark
Multi-state businesses: Companies operating across more than two states or union territories must now register with CPCB centrally, not just with state boards
key_obligations: Effective immediately (July 7, 2022): All provisions of the Second Amendment Rules came into force on the date of Gazette notification — there is no phased implementation grace period

EPR compliance — mandatory now: Producers, importers, and brand owners must fulfil EPR targets for plastic packaging as per Schedule II of the PWM Rules; the earlier reference to a collection plan and two-year window has been deleted, confirming EPR is a live obligation

CPCB registration — multi-state operators: Entities operating in more than two states or union territories must register with the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB); single-state operators continue to register with the relevant State Pollution Control Board (SPCB) or Pollution Control Committee (PCC) under Rule 13(2)

Biodegradable plastic compliance: Producers or sellers of biodegradable plastics (as newly defined) must ensure CPCB certification and conformity with IS/ISO 17088:2021 (updated from IS 17088:2008); products must not generate microplastics or toxic residues under ambient environmental conditions

Trademark holders: Companies whose products are sold under registered trademarks are now explicitly included in the definition of 'brand owner' — such entities must assess their EPR exposure and register accordingly if not already done

Post-consumer and pre-consumer waste tracking: With formal definitions now in place, businesses should establish systems to separately account for and report pre-consumer (manufacturing stage) and post-consumer (end-user stage) plastic packaging waste — this data will be critical for EPR target calculations and CPCB audits

End-of-life disposal pathways: Companies channelling plastic waste to co-processors, waste-to-energy plants, or waste-to-oil units must ensure these facilities fall within the newly defined categories and comply with applicable guidelines; feedstock chemical production for plastic remanufacturing is separately classified as recycling
sections.climate_angle: India's tightening of plastic waste regulation is not happening in isolation — it is part of a global regulatory convergence on plastic pollution that carries direct climate implications. Plastics are derived predominantly from fossil fuels; the production, incineration, and landfilling of plastic waste contributes to greenhouse gas emissions across the entire value chain. The amendment's formal recognition of waste-to-energy, co-processing, and waste-to-oil pathways — while practical transition mechanisms — must be read alongside India's net-zero commitments and the need to eventually phase down fossil-fuel-derived materials. For businesses reporting under frameworks such as the ISSB's IFRS S1/S2 standards or preparing for alignment with the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) — increasingly relevant for Indian exporters to European markets — plastic waste management is a material topic under both physical risk (pollution, ecosystem degradation) and transition risk (policy, regulatory, and market shifts toward circular economy models). The new definitions around recycled plastic use and the distinction between pre- and post-consumer waste directly mirror the language used in CSRD-aligned value chain disclosures and the EU Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (PPWR). The push for higher recycled content in plastic packaging, implicit in these rules, also connects to supply chain resilience: companies that develop domestic recycled plastic supply chains reduce their exposure to virgin polymer price volatility — itself linked to global oil markets and carbon pricing trends. Early movers who build robust EPR compliance systems will be better positioned as India inevitably tightens recycled content mandates and aligns with the global plastics treaty currently being negotiated under UNEA.
what_to_do: Audit your EPR registration status immediately: Confirm whether your organisation is registered under the PWM Rules as a producer, importer, or brand owner — and check whether you qualify as a 'multi-state operator' requiring CPCB-level (rather than SPCB-level) registration. If not registered, initiate the process without delay as non-compliance carries enforcement risk

Map your plastic packaging footprint using the new definitions: Conduct an internal classification exercise using the newly defined categories — plastic packaging, post-consumer waste, pre-consumer waste, and recyclate use — to accurately quantify your EPR targets under Schedule II and prepare for CPCB reporting cycles

Review product claims on biodegradable and compostable plastics: If your packaging carries biodegradable or compostable claims, verify that these materials are CPCB-certified and conform to IS/ISO 17088:2021 (not the older IS 17088:2008 standard); non-conforming claims now carry heightened legal exposure

Engage your plastic waste processing partners: Verify that your recyclers, co-processors, and waste-to-energy partners meet the definitions and regulatory requirements under the amended rules; update contracts and due diligence checklists to reflect the new legal categories, and obtain documentation of their own registrations

Build EPR data infrastructure for climate disclosure alignment: Begin tracking plastic packaging volumes, recycled content use, and waste recovery rates in a manner consistent with both CPCB reporting requirements and emerging global disclosure standards (ISSB IFRS S2, CSRD, Global Plastics Treaty obligations); companies that integrate EPR data into their sustainability reporting now will avoid costly retrofitting as disclosure mandates tighten
sections.our_take: The Second Amendment to India's Plastic Waste Management Rules is, in substance, a regulatory maturation moment. The deletion of the two-year collection plan window and the explicit restatement of EPR as a mandatory obligation under Schedule II sends an unambiguous signal: the CPCB and MoEFCC are moving from framework-building to enforcement. The new definitional architecture — particularly the distinctions between biodegradable and compostable plastics, pre- and post-consumer waste, and the formal recognition of waste-to-energy and recycled content use — gives regulators the precise legal tools they need to hold businesses accountable in ways that were previously difficult to operationalise. For Indian companies with significant plastic packaging footprints, the window for a 'wait and watch' approach has firmly closed. More significantly, businesses should anticipate that these rules are a stepping stone: India is an active participant in the UN Global Plastics Treaty negotiations, and domestic rules will need to evolve to reflect whatever binding international obligations emerge — likely including mandatory recycled content targets, design-for-recyclability standards, and extended producer liability for transboundary plastic pollution. Companies that build adaptive, data-driven EPR compliance systems now will find themselves structurally ahead when the next wave of tightening arrives.

posts.title: CPCB Issues Urgent Notice to Producers on E-Waste Management Rules, 2022: What You Must Do Now
posts.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice to producers — including manufacturers, importers, and brand owners of electrical and electronic equipment — directing them to comply with their obligations under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022. Companies that have not yet re

sections.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice to producers — including manufacturers, importers, and brand owners of electrical and electronic equipment — directing them to comply with their obligations under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022. Companies that have not yet registered on the EPR portal, set collection targets, or filed returns are now at heightened regulatory risk. Non-compliance can attract penalties, production stoppages, and reputational damage under India's strengthening extended producer responsibility (EPR) framework.
sections.what_changed: The E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 replaced the earlier 2016 rules and introduced a significantly more structured Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for electrical and electronic equipment (EEE). Under EPR, producers — defined broadly to include manufacturers, importers, and brand owners selling EEE in India — are legally required to take back and channel end-of-life products through registered recyclers and dismantlers. The 2022 rules also introduced EPR certificates as a market-based compliance instrument, allowing producers to meet targets by purchasing certificates from registered recyclers. The CPCB's urgent notice signals that the regulator has identified a significant gap in compliance — many producers have either not registered on the centralised EPR portal, have not set or met their annual collection targets, or have failed to submit the mandatory annual returns. This notice is a formal warning that enforcement action is imminent for defaulting entities. It also reflects CPCB's broader push to operationalise EPR across waste streams (plastic, battery, tyre, and e-waste) as a cornerstone of India's circular economy agenda.
who_is_affected: Manufacturers of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) with operations or sales in India — including consumer electronics, IT and telecom equipment, large and small household appliances, lighting equipment, and medical devices covered under Schedule I of the Rules
Importers of EEE — any company bringing finished electronic goods or components into India for sale
Brand owners — companies that place EEE products in the Indian market under their own brand, even if manufactured by a third party
E-commerce platforms and producers selling directly to consumers online who may have obligations as brand owners or importers
Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) managing EPR compliance on behalf of producers
Small and medium enterprises (SMEs) in the electronics sector — the Rules apply regardless of company size if products fall under the notified categories
key_obligations: REGISTRATION (Immediate): All producers, importers, and brand owners must register on the CPCB's centralised EPR portal (https://eprewaste.cpcb.gov.in). Unregistered entities are in direct violation of Rule 5 of the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022.

EPR TARGETS: Producers must set and fulfil annual EPR targets for e-waste collection, channelisation, and recycling as prescribed under Schedule II of the Rules. Targets are based on sales volumes from previous years.

EPR CERTIFICATE PROCUREMENT: Where producers cannot meet collection targets through their own systems, they must procure EPR certificates from CPCB-registered recyclers or dismantlers within the compliance year.

ANNUAL RETURNS: Producers are required to file annual returns on the EPR portal detailing quantities of EEE placed on the market, e-waste collected, and EPR certificates held. Returns for the previous financial year must be filed promptly — check the portal for the current deadline.

TAKE-BACK SYSTEMS: Producers must establish or participate in a system for collecting e-waste from consumers, including through collection centres, reverse logistics, or PRO partnerships. Evidence of operational take-back mechanisms must be maintained.

RECORD-KEEPING: Maintain records of all e-waste generated, collected, transported, and channelised to authorised dismantlers/recyclers for a minimum of 5 years and make these available to CPCB/State PCBs on demand.

NON-COMPLIANCE RISK: CPCB has indicated enforcement action including cancellation of EPR registration, financial penalties under the Environment Protection Act, 1986, and potential suspension of production or import licences for persistent defaulters.
sections.climate_angle: E-waste compliance is not merely a regulatory checkbox — it sits at the intersection of climate risk, supply chain resilience, and global sustainability standards. The extraction of virgin raw materials for electronics (rare earth metals, cobalt, lithium, copper) is energy-intensive and carbon-heavy. A well-functioning EPR system for e-waste creates the infrastructure for a domestic circular economy, reducing dependence on imported virgin materials and cutting embodied carbon in the electronics value chain. For companies aligned with or preparing for global frameworks — including the ISSB's IFRS S2 (climate-related disclosures), the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) for entities with European exposure, and India's own BRSR Core mandatory disclosures for listed companies — e-waste management is increasingly a material topic. Supply chain Scope 3 emissions, responsible sourcing, and circular economy practices are now standard expectations from institutional investors and multinational buyers. Physical climate risks also intersect here: flooding, heat stress, and extreme weather events disrupt e-waste collection logistics and informal recycling clusters concentrated in cities like Delhi, Mumbai, and Bengaluru. Producers who build robust, formalised take-back systems now are better insulated against supply chain shocks as climate volatility intensifies. Furthermore, the EU's Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CS3D) and similar regulations will soon require Indian exporters and suppliers to demonstrate circular economy compliance — making CPCB EPR compliance a de facto export-readiness requirement.
what_to_do: AUDIT YOUR EPR STATUS IMMEDIATELY: Conduct an internal compliance audit to confirm your registration status on the CPCB EPR portal, verify that your product categories and sales volumes are correctly declared, and assess whether your EPR targets for the current financial year are on track to be met.

APPOINT OR REVIEW YOUR PRO ARRANGEMENT: If you are using a Producer Responsibility Organisation (PRO) to manage EPR obligations, obtain a written status report confirming collection activities, recycler partnerships, and certificate balances. If you do not have a PRO, engage one or build an in-house take-back system without delay.

PROCURE EPR CERTIFICATES TO BRIDGE GAPS: If your collection targets cannot be met through physical take-back before the year-end, immediately engage CPCB-registered recyclers to procure EPR certificates. Delays in certificate procurement are a common compliance gap — act before the financial year closes.

INTEGRATE E-WASTE KPIs INTO BRSR AND SUSTAINABILITY REPORTING: Map your e-waste collection and recycling data to BRSR Core Principle 2 (Sustainable and Safe Products) disclosures and to ISSB/TCFD-aligned climate reporting on circular economy and Scope 3 value chain impacts — this strengthens investor and buyer confidence.

ESTABLISH A REGULATORY MONITORING PROCESS: Assign a dedicated compliance owner to track CPCB portal notifications, EPR rule amendments, and State PCB directives. Given the pace of regulatory activity across EPR categories (plastic, battery, tyre, e-waste), a fragmented monitoring approach creates avoidable risk.
sections.our_take: CPCB's urgent notice is a clear signal that India's EPR enforcement era has well and truly begun. For years, e-waste rules existed largely on paper, with low registration rates and minimal enforcement action. That era is ending. The CPCB has invested significantly in its digital EPR portal infrastructure, and the issuance of formal urgent notices to producers — with implied threats of production and import licence consequences — marks a qualitative shift in regulatory intent. We expect this to be followed by show-cause notices to specific defaulters, and potentially publicised enforcement actions, as CPCB seeks to establish credibility ahead of EPR frameworks for additional waste categories. For business leaders, the message is unambiguous: e-waste EPR compliance is now a board-level risk topic, not an operational afterthought. Companies that treat this as a compliance-first, strategy-second opportunity — building circular supply chains, engaging certified recyclers, and transparently reporting performance — will be better positioned for the regulatory, investor, and customer scrutiny that is coming at pace.

posts.title: Recyclers & Refurbishers under E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022: What the Latest CPCB Notice Means for Your Business
posts.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice targeting recyclers and refurbishers operating under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, signalling heightened regulatory scrutiny of downstream EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) value chain actors. This notice reinforces

sections.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice targeting recyclers and refurbishers operating under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, signalling heightened regulatory scrutiny of downstream EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) value chain actors. This notice reinforces that compliance obligations under India's e-waste framework extend well beyond producers and importers — recyclers and refurbishers are now firmly in the regulatory spotlight. Companies in the e-waste processing ecosystem must act immediately to audit their registrations, EPR certificates, and reporting obligations to avoid penalties, suspension, or cancellation of authorisations.
sections.what_changed: India's E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 — which replaced the earlier 2016 Rules — overhauled the country's approach to managing end-of-life electronics by introducing a formalised EPR framework administered through a centralised online portal managed by CPCB. Under this framework, producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) are required to meet annual e-waste collection and recycling targets, but they can only fulfil these targets by channelling waste through CPCB-registered recyclers and refurbishers. The latest CPCB urgent notice appears to address non-compliance, irregular reporting, or operational irregularities among registered recyclers and refurbishers — entities that serve as the critical last mile in the EPR chain. This could encompass issues such as failure to update capacity declarations, discrepancies in EPR certificate issuance, non-submission of quarterly or annual returns, or operation beyond the scope of granted authorisations. The notice is a clear signal that CPCB is moving from framework-building to active enforcement, and that recyclers and refurbishers can no longer treat compliance as a back-office formality.
who_is_affected: CPCB-registered e-waste recyclers processing IT equipment, consumer electronics, large and small household appliances, lighting equipment, and other notified categories under the 2022 Rules
Refurbishers of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) who are registered or required to register under the 2022 Rules
Collection centres, dismantlers, and aggregators that feed into the formal recycling chain and may hold ancillary authorisations

Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) who depend on registered recycler and refurbisher networks to fulfil their annual EPR targets — their compliance is directly tied to the legitimacy of their recycling partners
EPR compliance service providers and intermediaries managing EPR certificate transactions on behalf of PIBOs
State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) and State Pollution Control Committees (SPCCs) that co-regulate authorisations at the state level
key_obligations: Registration & Authorisation Validity: Recyclers and refurbishers must ensure their CPCB registration and state-level authorisation (from SPCB/SPCC) are current, accurate, and reflect actual operational capacity — any lapse or mismatch must be rectified immediately

EPR Certificate Issuance: Recyclers are obligated to generate and transfer EPR certificates to PIBOs through the CPCB EPR portal only for quantities actually processed and verified — fraudulent or inflated certificate issuance is a specific enforcement target

Quarterly & Annual Returns: Submission of processing data, tonnage handled, and waste disposal records via the CPCB online portal must be completed within prescribed timelines — entities in default should file outstanding returns without delay
Capacity Declaration Accuracy: Declared processing capacity must match on-ground infrastructure; CPCB inspections are increasingly being used to verify physical capacity against portal declarations

Compliance with Notified E-Waste Categories: Recyclers must process only the categories of EEE for which they hold valid authorisation — processing unauthorised categories is a violation that can trigger cancellation of registration

Response to CPCB Notices: Any entity that has received a show-cause notice or direction under this urgent notice must respond within the stipulated timeframe (typically 15–30 days) with documentary evidence of compliance or a corrective action plan

Downstream Disposal Records: Recyclers must maintain auditable records of how residual waste, hazardous fractions, and recovered materials are disposed of or sold, in compliance with Hazardous Waste Management Rules
sections.climate_angle: E-waste regulation is not merely a domestic compliance matter — it sits at the intersection of India's climate commitments, circular economy transition, and global supply chain sustainability standards. The formal recycling of e-waste is essential for recovering critical minerals such as cobalt, lithium, copper, and rare earth elements that are foundational to clean energy technologies, including electric vehicles, solar panels, and energy storage systems. A well-functioning, compliant recycler ecosystem directly supports India's energy transition by reducing dependence on virgin mineral extraction, which carries significant carbon and ecological costs. From a physical climate risk perspective, informal or non-compliant e-waste recycling — which this notice implicitly targets by strengthening formal sector oversight — contributes to soil and groundwater contamination, particularly in climate-vulnerable geographies. As India faces increasing climate stress, protecting land and water resources through proper e-waste processing is a material environmental risk management imperative. On the global standards front, multinational companies sourcing from or operating in India are increasingly required under frameworks such as the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosures, and the EU Battery Regulation to demonstrate responsible end-of-life management of products placed on global markets. Indian recyclers and refurbishers that achieve and maintain strong CPCB compliance will be better positioned to serve as verified partners in these global supply chains. Conversely, non-compliant recyclers risk being excluded from the formal EPR ecosystem entirely, losing access to the growing volume of e-waste being channelled through regulated routes.
what_to_do: Conduct an immediate compliance audit: Review your CPCB portal account, registration status, authorisation expiry dates, and all pending return filings — identify and close any gaps before a regulatory inspection or notice reaches you

Reconcile EPR certificate data: Cross-check all EPR certificates issued against actual processing records and tonnage data; flag and investigate any discrepancies, and do not issue certificates for unprocessed or unverified quantities

Engage your legal and environmental compliance team: If you have received a CPCB notice or show-cause order, engage qualified environmental law counsel immediately and prepare a factual, documented response within the prescribed timeline

Upgrade your data management systems: Invest in digital record-keeping for intake, processing, residue disposal, and certificate issuance — CPCB's enforcement is increasingly data-driven, and paper-based systems will not withstand scrutiny

If you are a PIBO, verify your recycling partners now: Audit the registration and compliance status of every recycler and refurbisher in your EPR network — your own EPR target fulfilment is only as strong as the legitimacy of the certificates your partners generate
sections.our_take: This CPCB notice is a watershed moment for India's formal e-waste sector. Since the 2022 Rules were notified, the EPR certificate market has grown rapidly — but so have concerns about certificate fraud, capacity inflation, and shell recyclers issuing credits without genuine processing activity. CPCB's decision to issue an urgent notice specifically targeting recyclers and refurbishers signals that the regulator is no longer willing to allow the formal framework to be undermined by bad actors. We expect this to be the beginning of a sustained enforcement wave: inspections will increase, authorisations of non-compliant entities will be suspended or cancelled, and the knock-on effect will be felt by PIBOs whose EPR targets rest on certificates from now-invalidated recyclers. The medium-term outcome — a leaner, more credible formal recycling sector — is the right direction for India's circular economy ambitions. But the short-term disruption will be real, and only those recyclers and refurbishers who have invested in genuine compliance will emerge stronger. For producers and brands, this is also a wake-up call to treat recycler due diligence as a core procurement and ESG risk management function, not an afterthought.

posts.title: ESG & Climate Compliance Digest — 20 May 2026: E-Waste EPR Portal in Focus as Regulators Tighten the Screws
posts.summary: Today's compliance landscape is dominated by a cluster of eight urgent notices from the CPCB relating to the E-Waste Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) Portal — covering filing extensions, Environmental Compensation guidelines, recycler obligations, and the framework for EPR certificate generati

sections.summary: Today's compliance landscape is dominated by a cluster of eight urgent notices from the CPCB relating to the E-Waste Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) Portal — covering filing extensions, Environmental Compensation guidelines, recycler obligations, and the framework for EPR certificate generation. Taken together, these notices signal that India's e-waste regulatory machinery is undergoing a significant operational tightening, with no more 'soft landing' for producers, importers, or recyclers who have deferred compliance. Companies with any footprint in electronics manufacturing, import, retail, or recycling must treat today's digest as a call to action — deadlines are real, penalties are codified, and the portal is live. The convergence of these notices in a short window is not coincidental: it reflects CPCB's intent to close the gap between EPR registration and actual verified compliance before the next annual reporting cycle begins.
sections.what_changed: TODAY'S HEADLINE UPDATE — Annual Return Deadline Extended to 15 June (from 30 June 2025 baseline): CPCB has issued an urgent notice extending the deadline for filing annual returns on the E-Waste EPR Portal. While the original deadline stood at 30 June 2025, a further extension has been granted, with the operative cut-off now running through 15 June 2026 for the current cycle. This is the last extension signal being communicated — entities that miss this window face Environmental Compensation (EC) proceedings. EXTENSION FOR QUARTERLY AND ANNUAL RETURNS FOR FY 2023-24 (till 31 December 2024 — now lapsed, action required): An earlier notice had extended both quarterly and annual returns for FY 2023-24 to 31 December 2024. This deadline has now lapsed. Any entity that did not file within that window is in arrears and must proactively engage with CPCB's Environmental Compensation framework to regularise their status. Continued non-filing will attract escalating EC charges. ENVIRONMENTAL COMPENSATION (EC) GUIDELINES UNDER E-WASTE (MANAGEMENT) RULES, 2022: CPCB has formalised and published the Environmental Compensation guidelines applicable to entities that fail to meet their EPR obligations under the 2022 Rules. EC is not a fine in the traditional sense — it is a quantified liability calculated on the basis of the volume of e-waste for which the obligation was unmet, multiplied by prescribed rates. The guidelines clarify the methodology, the appeal mechanism, and the timelines for EC recovery. This is a materially important development: it transforms non-compliance from an administrative risk into a balance-sheet liability. GUIDELINES FOR DETERMINATION OF PROCESSING CAPACITY OF E-WASTE RECYCLING FACILITIES BY SPCBs/PCCs: CPCB has issued standardised guidelines for State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) and Pollution Control Committees (PCCs) to uniformly assess and certify the processing capacity of e-waste recycling facilities. This is significant because EPR certificate generation — the mechanism by which producers get credit for e-waste channelled to recyclers — is directly tied to verified recycler capacity. Inflated or unverified capacity claims have been a persistent loophole; these guidelines close it at the source. FRAMEWORK FOR GENERATION OF EPR CERTIFICATES UNDER E-WASTE (MANAGEMENT) RULES, 2022: A dedicated framework document has been issued governing how EPR Certificates are generated, validated, transferred, and retired on the portal. Producers and importers use EPR certificates as proof that they have met their collection and recycling targets. The framework introduces stricter traceability requirements — certificates must now be linked to verified recycler capacity (per the capacity determination guidelines above), actual weight of e-waste processed, and time-stamped portal entries. Bulk or retrospective certificate generation without underlying transaction evidence will no longer be accepted. NOTICE TO REGISTERED RECYCLERS UNDER E-WASTE (M) RULES, 2022: A targeted notice has been sent to all registered recyclers on the portal. The notice reinforces that recyclers must update their processing capacity data, ensure their facility certifications are current with the relevant SPCB/PCC, and that any EPR certificates they generate must strictly correspond to actual processing records. Recyclers found generating certificates beyond their verified capacity face suspension of registration. NOTICE REGARDING EXTENSION OF TIMELINE FOR FILING RETURNS (general portal notice): A broader portal-level notice reiterates the extended timelines and urges all registered entities — producers, importers, recyclers, refurbishers, and dismantlers — to log in, verify their data, and complete pending return filings. CPCB has indicated that the portal will flag non-compliant entities automatically after the extended deadline passes, triggering the EC process.
who_is_affected: Electronics Manufacturers (OEMs) — Any company manufacturing electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) listed in Schedule I of the E-Waste Rules, 2022, including consumer electronics, IT equipment, telecom devices, lighting equipment, and large/small household appliances.
Importers of EEE — Companies importing any Schedule I equipment into India, regardless of whether they are the brand owner or a trading entity.
E-Commerce Platforms — Platforms facilitating the sale of EEE are increasingly being examined for EPR liability; those with marketplace models should review their exposure under the 2022 Rules.
Authorised E-Waste Recyclers — All CPCB/SPCB-registered recycling facilities are directly named in today's notices and face capacity verification requirements.
Refurbishers and Dismantlers — Registered entities in the refurbishment and dismantling segments must file returns and ensure their portal data is current.

Large Industrial Consumers — Companies operating large data centres, manufacturing plants, or IT infrastructure with significant end-of-life EEE volumes may have producer-equivalent obligations depending on the nature of their operations.
Supply Chain & Procurement Teams — Companies sourcing electronics from vendors who are non-compliant EPR registrants face reputational and supply chain disruption risk.

ESG / Sustainability Reporting Teams — E-waste EPR compliance is increasingly a disclosure item under SEBI's BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting) framework for listed companies.
key_obligations: DEADLINE — 15 June 2026: File Annual Returns on the E-Waste EPR Portal for the current reporting cycle. This is the final extended deadline. No further extensions are expected.

IMMEDIATE ACTION: Any entity that missed the FY 2023-24 annual return deadline of 31 December 2024 must proactively assess their Environmental Compensation liability using the newly published EC guidelines and engage with CPCB before the portal auto-flags them.

ONGOING — Recyclers: Update and resubmit processing capacity data to your respective SPCB/PCC in accordance with the new capacity determination guidelines. EPR certificate generation will be gated against verified capacity from this point forward.

ONGOING — Producers/Importers: Review all EPR certificates in your account. Ensure each certificate is backed by verified recycler processing records. Any certificates generated under the old framework that cannot be substantiated may need to be retired or reconciled.

ONGOING — All Registered Entities: Log into the E-Waste EPR Portal, verify that your registration details, contact information, and category classifications are current. Stale data is a common reason for filing failures and penalty triggers.

REVIEW NOW: Cross-check your EPR targets (in metric tonnes) for the current financial year against actual collection and channelisation volumes. If you are tracking below target, initiate engagement with authorised recyclers immediately — last-minute certificate procurement without underlying transactions will not be accepted under the new framework.
GOVERNANCE: Ensure your Board/CSR/ESG Committee is briefed on the Environmental Compensation guidelines — EC liabilities must be provisioned for in financial statements where applicable.
sections.climate_angle: E-waste regulation sits at a critical intersection of the circular economy and India's broader climate transition strategy. The production and disposal of electronic equipment is one of the fastest-growing waste streams globally, and the carbon footprint of linear 'take-make-dispose' electronics consumption is substantial — both in embodied energy during manufacturing and in the toxic and greenhouse gas emissions from informal disposal. India generates approximately 1.6 million metric tonnes of e-waste annually, with formal recycling rates still well below 30%. Today's regulatory actions are therefore not merely administrative — they are structural interventions to redirect material flows toward formal, lower-carbon processing pathways. From a global standards alignment perspective, the EPR certificate framework and the capacity verification guidelines directly support the kind of supply chain traceability that is now required under the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosures. CSRD's supply chain due diligence requirements (under CSDDD) and ISSB's S2 both require companies to account for Scope 3 emissions — which include the end-of-life treatment of products sold. An Indian electronics manufacturer or importer that cannot demonstrate verified e-waste recycling is increasingly exposed not just to domestic regulatory risk, but to trade and procurement barriers in European and other global markets. The formalisation of Environmental Compensation as a quantified, balance-sheet-relevant liability also mirrors the direction of travel in carbon pricing globally. Just as carbon credits must be verified and retired, EPR certificates must now be traceable to real recycling activity. This convergence of 'circular economy finance' with the logic of verified environmental markets is a signal that India's environmental regulatory architecture is maturing rapidly — and companies that invest in robust EPR compliance today are building the data infrastructure they will need for BRSR Core disclosures, ISSB reporting, and eventual carbon accounting requirements tomorrow. Physical climate risk is also a factor: climate-stressed supply chains, extreme heat events disrupting informal recycling clusters, and flood risk to e-waste storage sites are all material concerns that make formal, resilient recycling infrastructure a climate adaptation priority — not just a compliance checkbox.
what_to_do: COMPLIANCE AUDIT (This Week): Assign a responsible owner — your Environment, Health & Safety (EHS) lead or ESG officer — to conduct an immediate audit of your E-Waste EPR Portal account. Confirm registration status, check pending returns, and document any gaps against the 15 June 2026 filing deadline.

EC LIABILITY ASSESSMENT (This Week): If any annual or quarterly returns for FY 2023-24 or FY 2024-25 were missed, use the newly published Environmental Compensation guidelines to calculate your potential EC exposure. Share this assessment with your CFO and legal team for provisioning and disclosure decisions.

RECYCLER DUE DILIGENCE (Within 2 Weeks): Contact your authorised e-waste recycling partners and request confirmation that (a) their SPCB/PCC capacity certification is current and compliant with the new capacity determination guidelines, and (b) that EPR certificates they will generate on your behalf are supported by actual processing records. Do not accept certificates from recyclers whose capacity status is unverified.

EPR CERTIFICATE RECONCILIATION (Before 15 June 2026): Reconcile your EPR certificate holdings against your EPR targets for the current FY. Identify any shortfall and procure additional verified certificates from compliant recyclers before the annual return filing deadline. Build in at least a two-week buffer for portal processing delays.

BRSR & BOARD REPORTING (Next Governance Cycle): Incorporate today's regulatory developments into your BRSR disclosure narrative. Flag the Environmental Compensation framework as a new material regulatory risk in your next Board ESG/Risk Committee update, and align your e-waste KPIs with ISSB S2 and GRI 306 (Waste) disclosures for international investor audiences.
sections.our_take: Today's cluster of eight CPCB notices is one of the most significant single-day regulatory signals in India's e-waste compliance history — not because any one notice is unprecedented, but because of what they represent collectively: the closing of the implementation gap. For years, India's EPR framework for e-waste has had the architecture of accountability — registration portals, certificate systems, return filings — but the enforcement infrastructure was patchy and extensions were routine. What today's notices communicate, reading between the lines, is that the era of 'extend and pretend' is ending. The Environmental Compensation guidelines give CPCB a financially potent enforcement tool; the capacity verification guidelines eliminate the most widely used compliance workaround; and the EPR certificate traceability framework removes the ambiguity that allowed paper compliance without physical recycling. For companies that have been treating EPR as a low-priority administrative task, the risk profile has changed materially — and it has changed today. Our strong recommendation: treat the 15 June 2026 deadline as hard, invest in recycler due diligence as a strategic supplier relationship rather than a transactional procurement exercise, and start building the data trails now that will be demanded not just by CPCB, but by global ESG disclosure frameworks within the next two to three reporting cycles.

posts.title: Framework for Generation of EPR Certificates Under E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022: What Producers, Importers & Brand Owners Must Do Now
posts.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued a framework governing the generation of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) certificates under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, formalising how producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) must account for end-of-life electronics they p
