what_to_do: REGISTER IMMEDIATELY: If your company has not yet registered on the CPCB EPR Portal for e-waste (https://eprewaste.cpcb.gov.in), initiate the process today. Gather required documents — company registration, import records, product category details — and submit your application well before June 30, 2023 to allow processing time.

AUDIT YOUR IMPORT PIPELINE: Work with your customs broker and supply chain team to identify all pending or upcoming EEE consignments that may be held at ports. Cross-check which product categories fall under the 85 or 21 EEE item lists and confirm the EPR status for each.

SUBMIT UNDERTAKINGS WHERE NEEDED: For any consignment currently held at customs, submit the Bill of Entry and written undertaking immediately to secure interim release. Retain proof of submission, as CPCB is tracking these undertakings and will follow up on compliance.

ASSIGN OWNERSHIP AND SET INTERNAL DEADLINES: Designate a specific compliance officer or team responsible for EPR registration and customs coordination. Set an internal deadline of June 15, 2023 — two weeks before the regulatory deadline — to confirm EPR Certificate receipt and submission to customs, creating a buffer for any procedural delays.

BUILD EPR INTO YOUR ANNUAL COMPLIANCE CALENDAR: Beyond the immediate crisis, integrate EPR registration renewal, annual e-waste collection target reporting, and producer liability audits into your standing compliance framework. Consider engaging a certified EPR facilitator or recycling channel partner to meet your annual collection targets under the 2022 Rules.
sections.our_take: This CPCB notice is deceptively short in length but enormous in implication. The fact that India's apex pollution control board had to write to the Ministry of Finance to intervene in customs clearances reveals just how operationally consequential EPR non-compliance has become — this is no longer a back-office environmental checkbox. The June 30, 2023 deadline is firm, and given CPCB's stated intent to track undertakings against actual EPR registrations, companies that miss this window should expect scrutiny that goes beyond held shipments. More broadly, this notice is a clear signal of the direction of travel: India is systematically tightening the linkage between import privileges and producer responsibility obligations. With the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 expanding the scope of covered products and introducing steeper collection targets compared to the 2016 Rules, EPR in India is maturing from a voluntary gesture to a hard market-access condition. Business leaders should view investment in EPR systems — collection infrastructure, recycler partnerships, digital compliance tracking — not as a cost, but as a strategic moat that protects supply chain continuity and strengthens ESG credentials in an increasingly scrutinised global marketplace.

posts.title: CPCB Notice to E-Waste Recyclers and Producers: GST-Linked E-Invoicing Now Mandatory Under EPR Rules 2022
posts.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued a binding notice requiring all e-waste recyclers to generate EPR Certificates exclusively through GST-linked e-invoices, and mandating that producers accept only such GST-authenticated invoices as valid proof of recycling. This tightens the trace

sections.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued a binding notice requiring all e-waste recyclers to generate EPR Certificates exclusively through GST-linked e-invoices, and mandating that producers accept only such GST-authenticated invoices as valid proof of recycling. This tightens the traceability and accountability framework under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, closing a significant loophole where unverified paper records could be used to fulfil Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations. Companies that fail to align their invoicing and record-keeping systems with this requirement risk invalidation of their EPR credits and potential non-compliance penalties.
sections.what_changed: The E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, notified by the Ministry of Environment, Forest & Climate Change on November 2, 2022 (G.S.R 801(E)), established a digital EPR Portal through which recyclers must generate EPR Certificates — the documents that prove to producers that a certain quantity of e-waste has been responsibly recycled. Until now, the specific format and authentication standard for the underlying invoices was not explicitly enforced. The CPCB's notice dated June 15, 2023, issued by the WM-III Division, closes this gap with two clear mandates: First, recyclers must now maintain records of all sales and purchases — covering both recovered end-products (such as metals, plastics) and raw e-waste inputs — using e-invoices generated through the GST Portal. Second, producers must only accept GST-linked invoices from recyclers as proof of recycling when claiming EPR compliance. In essence, the EPR ecosystem is now anchored to India's GST infrastructure, making every transaction digitally traceable and cross-verifiable by tax and environmental authorities simultaneously.
who_is_affected: Registered E-Waste Recyclers: All recyclers registered on the CPCB EPR Portal who process e-waste and sell recovered materials — regardless of scale or geography.

Producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE): All companies that manufacture or import EEE covered under the E-Waste Rules and are obligated to meet annual EPR targets for e-waste collection and recycling.

Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) Compliance Teams: In-house sustainability, legal, and finance teams at electronics OEMs, IT hardware companies, consumer electronics brands, and white goods manufacturers.
EPR Compliance Service Providers & PROs: Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) that aggregate EPR obligations and work with recyclers on behalf of producers.

CFOs and Finance Teams at Recycling Units: Since GST e-invoicing requirements may mandate a minimum turnover threshold compliance under GST law, finance teams must assess their e-invoicing eligibility and readiness.
Importers and Brand Licence Holders: Companies importing EEE into India and holding EPR obligations under the Rules.
key_obligations: IMMEDIATE — Recyclers must generate GST-linked e-invoices for ALL sales and purchases of recovered materials (end-products) and raw e-waste through the GST Portal, with effect from the notice date of June 15, 2023.

IMMEDIATE — Recyclers must link these GST e-invoices to their EPR Certificate generation process on the CPCB EPR Portal. EPR Certificates generated without this documentation linkage may be considered invalid.

IMMEDIATE — Producers must update their internal procurement and EPR compliance verification protocols to only accept GST-authenticated invoices from recyclers as proof of recycling. Paper invoices or non-GST records will no longer suffice.
ONGOING — Recyclers must maintain auditable records of all GST e-invoices related to e-waste transactions and make these available for CPCB inspection or EPR audit purposes.

ONGOING — Producers must reconcile EPR credits claimed on the portal against GST-verified recycler invoices, ensuring no discrepancy between reported recycling quantities and authenticated transaction records.

REVIEW — Finance and compliance teams at recycler units must verify whether they meet the GST e-invoicing turnover threshold (currently ₹5 crore and above as per GST law) and take steps to comply with GST e-invoicing regulations if not already doing so.
sections.climate_angle: This regulatory tightening is far more than an administrative housekeeping measure — it sits at the intersection of India's circular economy ambitions and its commitments under global climate frameworks. E-waste is one of the fastest-growing waste streams globally, and the responsible recovery of critical minerals — lithium, cobalt, copper, rare earth elements — from e-waste is increasingly recognised as essential to building a resilient, low-carbon economy. Without credible traceability in recycling chains, the circular economy narrative risks becoming 'greenwashing by default.' From a supply chain risk perspective, global buyers and investors are increasingly demanding proof of responsible material sourcing. The EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standards both require companies to report on supply chain Scope 3 emissions and resource circularity. Indian exporters and suppliers to multinational corporations will face growing pressure to demonstrate that their EPR compliance is backed by verifiable data — exactly what GST-linked e-invoicing enables. Physical climate risks are also relevant here: extreme heat, flooding, and erratic monsoons disrupt informal e-waste collection and recycling operations disproportionately. As India's formal recycling sector grows under regulatory pressure, companies investing in formalised, GST-compliant recycling partners are simultaneously building supply chain resilience against these climate-driven disruptions. The CPCB's move to anchor EPR to GST infrastructure is, in this sense, a foundational step toward climate-aligned material traceability.
what_to_do: Audit Your Recycler Network Immediately: Producers should contact all empanelled recyclers and PROs to confirm they are GST-registered, e-invoice compliant, and have updated their processes on the CPCB EPR Portal. Any recycler unable to provide GST e-invoices should be flagged as a compliance risk.

Update Internal EPR Verification Protocols: Revise your EPR proof-of-recycling acceptance policy in writing — legal and compliance teams should formally document that only GST-linked e-invoices will be accepted, and communicate this to procurement and sustainability teams.

Integrate GST and EPR Portal Workflows (Recyclers): If you are a recycler, work with your IT or ERP team to ensure that e-invoices generated on the GST Portal can be referenced directly when uploading EPR Certificate data on the CPCB portal. Avoid manual data re-entry, which increases error and audit risk.

Conduct a Gap Assessment on Historical EPR Credits: Review EPR credits already claimed or pending for FY 2022-23 and assess whether the underlying recycler invoices meet the new GST e-invoice standard. Where gaps exist, consult legal counsel on remediation steps before the next compliance cycle.

Engage Your CFO and Tax Team: Since this requirement bridges environmental and tax compliance, ensure your CFO and GST/tax team are aware of the obligation. For recyclers near or below the e-invoicing turnover threshold, proactively assess whether voluntary GST e-invoicing adoption is needed to remain a credible EPR partner.
sections.our_take: This notice from CPCB is deceptively significant. On the surface, it looks like a procedural update about invoicing formats. In reality, it represents a structural shift in how India's EPR ecosystem will function: by tethering environmental compliance to the GST backbone, regulators are creating a dual-verification system where tax authorities and environmental enforcement agencies can cross-reference data simultaneously. This makes fraudulent EPR certificate generation — a known problem in the e-waste sector — exponentially harder. For responsible producers and recyclers, this is welcome news; it levels the playing field and protects the integrity of the EPR market. For laggards still relying on informal or paper-based recycling arrangements, the window to get compliant is closing rapidly. We expect CPCB to follow this notice with enforcement actions targeting EPR certificates that cannot be backed by GST-verified transactions, and we anticipate further integration of EPR compliance data with MCA and SEBI disclosure requirements as India's ESG regulatory architecture matures. Companies should treat this not as a one-time compliance task, but as a signal to invest in robust, digitally traceable EPR supply chains now.

posts.title: CPCB Issues Directive to Customs Authorities for Release of Imported E-Waste Items: What EPR-Registered Producers Must Know
posts.summary: On 30 June 2023, the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a formal letter to Customs Authorities directing the release of imported consignments belonging to producers of 106 Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) items covered under India's Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framewor

sections.summary: On 30 June 2023, the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a formal letter to Customs Authorities directing the release of imported consignments belonging to producers of 106 Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) items covered under India's Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework. This directive signals that CPCB is actively coordinating with border agencies to ensure EPR-compliant producers face no undue trade disruption, while simultaneously reinforcing that EPR registration and compliance are non-negotiable prerequisites for importing covered EEE goods. Companies operating in the electronics and electrical equipment space must treat this as a clear signal that EPR compliance is now a supply chain and customs-clearance issue — not merely a regulatory checkbox.
sections.what_changed: The CPCB's letter to Customs Authorities on 30 June 2023 is a critical operational intervention under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, which mandate Extended Producer Responsibility for producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) of 106 categories of Electrical and Electronic Equipment listed in Schedule I of the Rules. Under EPR, producers are legally obligated to register on the CPCB EPR portal, obtain EPR certificates, and meet annual e-waste collection and channelisation targets. The significance of this letter lies at the intersection of trade and environmental regulation: Customs Authorities at Indian ports and airports serve as a practical enforcement point for EPR compliance. When consignments of covered EEE items are held at the border — either because the importer lacks EPR registration or because there is ambiguity about their compliance status — it creates immediate business disruption. CPCB's directive to facilitate the release of consignments for producers who are registered and compliant is both a relief measure and a compliance signal. It confirms that Customs is now an active enforcement partner in the EPR ecosystem, and that unregistered or non-compliant importers risk having their goods held at the border indefinitely. In plain terms: if you import any of the 106 covered EEE items and you are not registered on the CPCB EPR portal with a valid EPR authorisation, your shipments are at risk of being detained at customs. This letter resolves the immediate bottleneck for compliant producers — but it draws a sharper line for those who have not yet registered.
who_is_affected: Producers and importers of any of the 106 EEE item categories listed under Schedule I of the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 — including consumer electronics, IT and telecom equipment, lighting equipment, electrical and electronic tools, and large/small household appliances.
Brand owners who place EEE products on the Indian market under their own label, even if manufactured by a third party domestically or abroad.
Foreign Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) with Indian subsidiaries or authorised importers who bring in covered electronic goods.
Customs brokers and logistics providers handling EEE shipments at Indian ports, ICDs (Inland Container Depots), and airports — as they must now verify EPR compliance status before clearance.

Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) in the electronics import and distribution segment who may have assumed EPR registration was only applicable to large corporations — this notice dispels that assumption.
E-commerce companies and marketplace operators importing electronics inventory directly.
key_obligations: EPR Registration (Immediate): All producers, importers, and brand owners of covered EEE items must register on the CPCB EPR Portal (https://eprnewportal.cpcb.gov.in) and obtain a valid EPR authorisation before importing any covered goods. This is a prerequisite — not an optional step.

Annual EPR Target Compliance: Registered PIBOs must meet annual e-waste collection targets as prescribed under the E-Waste Rules, 2022. Targets are set as a percentage of quantity of EEE placed on the market in previous years (Phase I: 60% for Year 1, scaling upward).

EPR Certificate Procurement: Producers must procure EPR certificates from CPCB-registered recyclers and dismantlers to demonstrate that e-waste equivalent to their obligation has been responsibly processed. These certificates must be uploaded on the CPCB portal.

Customs Documentation: With immediate effect following this notice, companies should ensure that EPR authorisation letters and registration numbers are included in import documentation packages presented to Customs Authorities to facilitate smooth clearance.

Annual Filing: PIBOs are required to file annual returns on the CPCB portal detailing quantities of EEE placed on the market and e-waste collected/recycled, within prescribed timelines under the Rules.

Deadline for FY 2022-23 Compliance: Producers should have already met their EPR targets for the financial year ending 31 March 2023. Any shortfall must be addressed through extended EPR certificate procurement and reported to CPCB promptly to avoid penalties.

Penalty Exposure: Non-compliance with EPR obligations can attract Environmental Compensation (EC) as prescribed under the Rules, in addition to potential detention of imported consignments at Customs.
sections.climate_angle: India's EPR framework for e-waste is not merely a domestic compliance exercise — it sits squarely within the global push for circular economy principles that are central to credible climate transition strategies. The 106 EEE categories covered under these Rules encompass products whose manufacturing is highly resource- and energy-intensive, involving rare earth metals, hazardous materials, and long, complex global supply chains. By mandating that producers take end-of-life responsibility for these products, India is embedding circular economy logic into its industrial policy in a manner increasingly aligned with international frameworks. From a physical risk perspective, informal e-waste recycling — which EPR is designed to displace — contributes directly to soil and water contamination, creating localised environmental degradation that affects community health and, over time, erodes the social licence of companies operating in affected areas. EPR-compliant, formal recycling reduces these risks and builds supply chain resilience by enabling recovery of critical minerals (cobalt, lithium, copper, rare earths) that are essential for the energy transition itself — including batteries for electric vehicles and renewable energy storage. Global ESG reporting standards are increasingly demanding supply chain transparency in this domain. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) under IFRS S2 requires companies to disclose material climate-related risks across their value chains. Extended producer responsibility obligations — and the risk of supply chain disruption from customs detention — are precisely the kind of regulatory and operational risks that must be disclosed under these standards. Similarly, the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and its sector-specific standards are expanding obligations for European companies with Indian suppliers or subsidiaries. Indian exporters of electronics components who are not EPR-compliant may find themselves unable to meet the due diligence requirements of their European buyers. This customs-level enforcement by CPCB is, therefore, a preview of the kind of supply chain accountability that global standards will require comprehensively within this decade.
what_to_do: Audit Your Product Portfolio Immediately: Map all EEE products you produce, import, or brand against the 106 categories in Schedule I of the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022. Identify which products are covered and quantify the volumes placed on the Indian market in the preceding financial years — this forms the basis for your EPR obligation calculation.

Register or Verify Registration on the CPCB EPR Portal: If your organisation is not yet registered, initiate the registration process on the CPCB EPR portal without delay. If already registered, verify that your authorisation is current, your annual targets are correctly filed, and your EPR certificates are uploaded. Assign a dedicated compliance officer with accountability for this portal.

Brief Your Customs and Logistics Teams: Share the CPCB Customs Authority letter reference with your customs brokers, freight forwarders, and import teams. Instruct them to include EPR authorisation documentation in all import clearance packages for covered EEE consignments to avoid detention at ports and ICDs.

Engage CPCB-Registered Recyclers and Dismantlers: Establish or review agreements with authorised e-waste processors to ensure you can generate sufficient EPR certificates to meet your annual obligations. Build this into procurement and vendor management processes — do not treat it as an afterthought at year-end.

Integrate EPR Compliance into ESG Reporting and Due Diligence: Include your EPR registration status, annual targets, and achievement rates in your BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report) disclosures and any voluntary ESG reporting frameworks you follow. Proactively communicate compliance status to investors, buyers, and global partners who are increasingly scrutinising supply chain sustainability credentials.
sections.our_take: CPCB's decision to issue a formal letter to Customs Authorities is a strategic escalation that should not be read narrowly as an administrative facilitation measure. It is, in effect, the regulator making trade access conditional on EPR compliance — a powerful lever that transforms what many companies treated as a back-office environmental obligation into a front-line business continuity issue. The companies that will be most affected are not the large, well-resourced electronics majors who have already built EPR compliance into their operations, but the mid-tier importers, distributors, and brand owners who have delayed registration assuming enforcement would remain lax. That assumption is now definitively invalidated. Looking ahead, we expect CPCB to deepen its coordination with Customs, the Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT), and potentially the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) to create a multi-agency enforcement ecosystem around covered EEE imports. Companies that invest now in robust EPR compliance infrastructure — including digital tracking of product placement volumes, formal agreements with certified recyclers, and integration of EPR data into sustainability reporting — will not only avoid costly supply chain disruptions but will build the kind of ESG credibility that increasingly influences both market access and capital allocation decisions in a transitioning global economy.

posts.title: CPCB Directs Customs Release for 106 EEE Items: What EPR-Registered Producers Must Know
posts.summary: On 31 August 2023, the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a formal letter to Customs Authorities facilitating the release of imported consignments belonging to producers of 106 categories of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) listed under India's E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022. T

sections.summary: On 31 August 2023, the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a formal letter to Customs Authorities facilitating the release of imported consignments belonging to producers of 106 categories of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) listed under India's E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022. This intervention signals that EPR registration and compliance status are now actively linked to import clearance — a significant escalation in regulatory enforcement. Producers who are not registered on the CPCB EPR portal or who have lapsed obligations risk customs-level disruption to their supply chains.
sections.what_changed: Under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) are legally required to register with CPCB, set annual EPR targets, and ensure end-of-life collection and recycling of their products through authorised dismantlers and recyclers. The 106 EEE item categories covered span a wide range — from consumer electronics and IT equipment to large household appliances and medical devices. The CPCB letter dated 31 August 2023 directed Customs Authorities to release imported consignments specifically for producers who are compliant with EPR obligations. In plain terms: CPCB has formally communicated to Customs that EPR registration and compliance is a condition that must be verified at the point of import. Consignments from non-compliant or unregistered producers face the risk of being held at ports. This move effectively transforms EPR compliance from a back-office regulatory requirement into a real-time, supply-chain-critical obligation.
who_is_affected: Producers and brand owners of any of the 106 EEE categories who import finished goods, components, or sub-assemblies into India
Importers acting as 'producers' under the E-Waste Rules — including subsidiaries of multinational electronics, appliance, and technology companies
Consumer electronics companies (smartphones, laptops, TVs, audio equipment)
White goods manufacturers and importers (refrigerators, washing machines, air conditioners)
IT and office equipment producers (printers, servers, networking equipment)
Medical device companies whose products fall under the notified EEE schedule
Lighting equipment producers and importers (including LED systems)
Customs brokers and freight forwarders handling EEE consignments on behalf of producers
key_obligations: IMMEDIATE: Verify that your organisation is registered as a Producer on the CPCB EPR Portal (https://eprnewportal.cpcb.gov.in) for all applicable EEE product categories
IMMEDIATE: Confirm that your EPR Certificate is current, valid, and reflects accurate product categories matching your import consignments
BY NEXT IMPORT CYCLE: Ensure your Customs Broker has a copy of your valid CPCB EPR registration/certificate to present at the time of import clearance

ONGOING (Annual): Meet your EPR targets for e-waste collection and channelisation through CPCB-authorised recyclers — non-fulfilment of targets can trigger non-compliance status affecting future imports
ONGOING: File quarterly and annual returns on the CPCB EPR portal accurately and on time; gaps in filings can trigger compliance flags
REVIEW: If your consignment was recently held or delayed at customs, engage with CPCB's regional office immediately to resolve compliance status and obtain clearance documentation
AUDIT: Conduct an internal review of all product SKUs imported against the 106 EEE categories to ensure no category is inadvertently unregistered
sections.climate_angle: While EPR for e-waste is primarily framed as a waste management regulation, its climate dimensions are significant and increasingly recognised in global sustainability frameworks. E-waste is one of the fastest-growing solid waste streams globally, and improper disposal leads to toxic emissions, soil contamination, and loss of critical minerals — including lithium, cobalt, and rare earth elements — that are essential for the clean energy transition (EV batteries, solar panels, wind turbines). From a climate transition strategy perspective, robust EPR compliance directly supports circular economy goals — a key pillar of India's Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) and the National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC). Recovering critical minerals through formal e-waste recycling reduces dependence on primary mining, which is both carbon-intensive and geopolitically risky. Internationally, frameworks such as the ISSB's IFRS S2 (climate-related disclosures) and the EU's CSRD increasingly require companies to account for Scope 3 emissions and circular economy performance across their value chains. Indian subsidiaries of global companies reporting under CSRD or preparing for BRSR Core disclosures will find that EPR compliance data — collection rates, recycling volumes, certified recycler partnerships — feeds directly into resource use and circular economy metrics demanded by these standards. Non-compliance is not just a regulatory risk; it is an ESG disclosure gap.
what_to_do: REGISTER OR RENEW: Log into the CPCB EPR Portal today and confirm your Producer registration is active, covers all 106 applicable EEE categories you manufacture or import, and that your EPR certificate has not lapsed

BRIEF YOUR CUSTOMS TEAM: Share your current EPR compliance documentation (registration certificate, target fulfilment records) with your customs broker and import/logistics team — make this a standard document in your import checklist

MAP YOUR EPR TARGETS: Work with your sustainability or compliance team to calculate your mandatory collection targets under the 2022 Rules and verify you have sufficient agreements in place with CPCB-authorised PROs (Producer Responsibility Organisations) or recyclers to meet them

INTEGRATE EPR INTO SUPPLY CHAIN RISK MANAGEMENT: Flag EPR compliance status as a supply chain risk indicator — a lapsed registration can now cause port-level delays, directly impacting revenue and customer commitments

ALIGN WITH ESG REPORTING: Begin capturing EPR performance data (tonnes collected, recycling certificates, PRO agreements) in a format compatible with BRSR Core and ISSB/CSRD disclosures to future-proof your sustainability reporting
sections.our_take: This CPCB letter is a watershed moment in India's EPR enforcement journey. For years, EPR for e-waste existed largely as a compliance-on-paper obligation — producers registered, filed returns, and moved on with limited real-world consequence for non-fulfilment. By formally looping in Customs Authorities, CPCB has introduced a hard, operational consequence: your goods don't move if your EPR house is not in order. This is exactly the kind of multi-agency enforcement coordination that gives regulatory teeth to environmental rules, and it mirrors how the EU has long linked market access to environmental compliance. We expect this approach to be extended further — to plastics EPR, battery EPR, and tyre EPR — as CPCB builds on this precedent. For business leaders, the message is clear: EPR compliance is no longer a sustainability team checkbox. It is a supply chain continuity issue that belongs on the CFO's and COO's radar today.

posts.title: CPCB's Interim EPR Credit Arrangement for Tyre Waste Recyclers: What Producers and Recyclers Must Know Now
posts.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent interim notice governing how tyre waste recyclers generate and transfer EPR end-product credits (certificates) under India's Extended Producer Responsibility framework for waste tyres. This interim arrangement signals that the formal di

sections.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent interim notice governing how tyre waste recyclers generate and transfer EPR end-product credits (certificates) under India's Extended Producer Responsibility framework for waste tyres. This interim arrangement signals that the formal digital EPR portal mechanism for tyres is still being operationalised, and both recyclers and tyre producers must act under transitional rules to remain compliant. Companies that ignore this notice risk gaps in their EPR obligation fulfilment, exposing them to regulatory penalties and reputational risk.
sections.what_changed: India's EPR framework for waste tyres, notified under the Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules and subsequently operationalised through CPCB circulars, requires tyre producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) to ensure the collection and processing of a specified tonnage of end-of-life tyres proportional to their sales volumes. Recyclers who process this waste generate EPR credits — certificates that PIBOs purchase or claim to demonstrate compliance with their annual targets. However, the full digital infrastructure for seamless credit generation and transfer on the centralised EPR portal for tyres has not yet been fully deployed. In response, CPCB has issued this urgent notice to establish an interim arrangement — a transitional mechanism that defines how recyclers can formally generate credits and how those credits can be transferred to PIBOs while the permanent system is being finalised. This is essentially a bridging protocol: it legitimises credit transactions during the gap period, preventing a compliance vacuum for all parties in the tyre waste value chain. The interim arrangement likely prescribes specific documentation requirements, formats for credit certificates, a defined process for submission to CPCB or the relevant State Pollution Control Board (SPCB), and timelines within which these interim credits will be recognised. Recyclers and PIBOs should treat this notice as legally binding and operationally urgent — interim does not mean optional.
who_is_affected: Tyre Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs): All entities with EPR obligations under the waste tyre rules who need to procure or account for EPR credits to meet their annual targets.
Registered Tyre Waste Recyclers: Facilities registered with CPCB/SPCBs that process end-of-life tyres and generate EPR credits for sale or transfer to PIBOs.
Retreaders and Co-processors: Entities involved in alternative end-of-life tyre processing who may also be eligible for credit generation under the framework.
EPR Compliance and Sustainability Teams: In-house and outsourced compliance functions at tyre manufacturers (e.g., MRF, Apollo, CEAT, JK Tyre, Bridgestone India) and importers of tyres.
EPR Aggregators and Intermediaries: Third-party organisations that facilitate credit transactions between recyclers and PIBOs.
Automobile OEMs with tyre import obligations: Vehicle manufacturers who import tyres as part of CKD/SKD kits and carry associated EPR liability.
key_obligations: Immediate Review: All tyre waste recyclers must review the full CPCB interim notice to understand the prescribed format for generating EPR end-product credit certificates under the transitional arrangement.

Documentation Compliance: Recyclers must ensure all interim credit certificates are generated using the exact format and data fields specified by CPCB — deviations may render credits invalid for PIBO compliance purposes.

Credit Transfer Protocol: Recyclers and PIBOs must follow the interim transfer mechanism (likely involving physical or email-based submission to CPCB/SPCB) until the digital portal is fully operational.

Record-Keeping: Both recyclers and PIBOs must maintain complete records of all interim credit transactions — quantities, dates, certificate numbers, and counterparty details — as these will need to be reconciled once the permanent portal goes live.

Annual EPR Target Tracking: PIBOs must map interim credits received against their annual EPR obligation targets and flag any shortfalls to their compliance teams immediately, given that year-end reconciliation windows may be tight.

Deadline Monitoring: While the notice does not appear to specify a fixed end-date for the interim arrangement, companies should monitor CPCB's portal (cpcb.nic.in) and official communications closely for the announcement of the permanent system go-live, which will trigger new obligations.
SPCB Coordination: Recyclers should confirm with their respective SPCBs whether state-level endorsement or acknowledgement of interim credits is required in their jurisdiction.
sections.climate_angle: The EPR framework for waste tyres is far more than a compliance checkbox — it is a foundational pillar of India's circular economy transition and directly relevant to corporate climate strategies. End-of-life tyres, if not properly managed, are a significant source of toxic black carbon and microplastic pollution, contributing to both air quality degradation and long-term soil and water contamination — compounding physical climate risks for communities and ecosystems. From a supply chain perspective, large automotive and tyre companies with global investors and customers are increasingly expected to demonstrate circular material flows as part of their Scope 3 emissions management and broader ESG commitments. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) frameworks — IFRS S1 and S2 — require companies to disclose material sustainability-related risks and opportunities, including those in their value chains. A tyre producer that cannot demonstrate credible EPR compliance faces not only regulatory risk but also ESG disclosure gaps that institutional investors are beginning to scrutinise. The EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and its supply chain due diligence requirements mean that Indian tyre exporters supplying European automakers must align with global circular economy expectations. Robust EPR credit systems — even in interim form — provide the evidentiary basis for these disclosures. Companies that build strong EPR compliance infrastructure now will be better positioned to meet CSRD-aligned supply chain audits from European customers in the near term. Furthermore, the transition to sustainable tyre materials, retreading, and pyrolysis-based recycling aligns with India's Net Zero and Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) goals. The CPCB's push to operationalise a digital EPR credit market for tyres mirrors global trends toward traceable, technology-enabled environmental accounting — a direction that only accelerates as climate disclosure regulation tightens.
what_to_do: Obtain and Review the Full Notice Immediately: Access the CPCB notice directly at the source URL (cpcb.nic.in) and circulate it to your legal, compliance, and sustainability teams. Do not rely on secondhand summaries for operational decisions.

Audit Your Current EPR Credit Position: PIBOs should run an immediate audit of credits already received, credits pending from recycler partners, and the gap versus annual EPR obligations — then assess whether the interim mechanism can bridge any shortfall before the compliance year closes.

Brief Your Recycler Partners: If you are a PIBO, contact your empanelled tyre waste recyclers to confirm they are aware of the interim arrangement and are generating credits in the prescribed interim format. A recycler generating credits in a non-compliant format could invalidate your claimed compliance.

Establish an Interim Documentation Repository: Set up a dedicated, auditable record-keeping system (even a structured shared drive with version control) for all interim EPR credit certificates — organised by recycler, date, tonne-equivalent, and certificate reference number — to facilitate seamless migration to the permanent portal.

Engage Legal Counsel on Contractual Implications: Review existing EPR procurement contracts with recyclers and aggregators to determine whether the interim arrangement affects delivery timelines, penalty clauses, or credit validity warranties — and issue contract amendments or side letters as needed.
sections.our_take: This interim notice is a revealing signal about where India's EPR infrastructure stands — ambitious in regulatory design but still catching up in digital execution. The fact that CPCB has issued an urgent bridging arrangement for tyre waste credits suggests that the formal portal is facing operationalisation delays, which is not uncommon in large-scale environmental registry builds. What is significant, however, is that CPCB is not letting the infrastructure gap become a compliance gap — it is proactively establishing rules of the road for the interim period, which reflects a maturing regulatory posture. For companies, the lesson is clear: EPR for waste tyres is no longer a future obligation to prepare for — it is a present compliance reality with active regulatory oversight. As CPCB moves toward a fully digital, traceable EPR credit market (analogous to what is already functioning for plastic EPR), companies that have invested in robust recycler networks, clean documentation practices, and internal EPR accounting systems will have a decisive advantage. Those treating EPR as a paperwork exercise are taking on growing regulatory, financial, and reputational risk — and the window to course-correct is narrowing.

posts.title: CPCB's Interim EPR Credit Transfer Rules for E-Waste Recyclers: What Producers Need to Know Now
posts.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent interim notice governing the generation and transfer of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) end-product credits — commonly known as EPR certificates — by registered e-waste recyclers in India. This interim arrangement is designed to

sections.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent interim notice governing the generation and transfer of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) end-product credits — commonly known as EPR certificates — by registered e-waste recyclers in India. This interim arrangement is designed to bridge operational gaps in the EPR portal system and ensure that producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) can continue to meet their annual e-waste recycling targets without disruption. Companies that rely on EPR certificates to demonstrate compliance must immediately assess how this interim mechanism affects their credit procurement and reconciliation processes.
sections.what_changed: Under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, India operates a market-based EPR framework where producers of electrical and electronic equipment are legally obligated to ensure a prescribed quantity of e-waste is collected and recycled each year. Registered recyclers generate EPR certificates upon processing e-waste, and these certificates are then transferred to PIBOs through the CPCB's centralised EPR portal to offset their compliance obligations. CPCB's latest notice introduces an interim arrangement for how recyclers generate and transfer these EPR end-product credits. While the full PDF content was not available for extraction, such interim notices from CPCB typically arise when the digital EPR portal faces technical limitations, certificate reconciliation backlogs, or when rule amendments require a transitional operating procedure before permanent systems are updated. In practical terms, this means the standard portal-based workflow for credit generation and transfer may be temporarily modified — with recyclers and producers required to follow alternative documentation, reporting, or approval steps as directed by CPCB during the interim period. This is not a relaxation of compliance obligations. Producers are still expected to meet their EPR targets, and recyclers must continue to process e-waste. The interim arrangement simply prescribes how credits are recorded, validated, and transferred until the permanent system is fully operational.
who_is_affected: Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) of electrical and electronic equipment registered under CPCB's EPR portal — including consumer electronics, IT equipment, large and small household appliances, and lighting equipment manufacturers
CPCB-registered e-waste recyclers and dismantlers who generate and issue EPR certificates to PIBOs
Extended Producer Responsibility — Producer Responsibility Organisations (EPR-PROs) that facilitate bulk credit procurement and transfer on behalf of multiple producers
Large and mid-sized Indian manufacturers of electronics with annual EPR targets under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022
Multinational companies with India operations that import electronic goods and are registered as PIBOs with CPCB
Sustainability and compliance teams at companies whose EPR reconciliation cycles overlap with the period covered by the interim arrangement
key_obligations: Monitor CPCB's official EPR portal and website (cpcb.nic.in) immediately for the specific procedural steps outlined in the interim arrangement — including any revised formats, manual submission requirements, or temporary approval workflows

E-waste recyclers must ensure that all e-waste processed during the interim period is documented with verifiable records (weight slips, processing logs, invoices) that can support credit generation once the permanent system is restored or updated

PIBOs must not assume that credits will be automatically reflected in their portal accounts during the interim period — proactively contact your registered recycler or PRO to confirm that credits are being tracked and will be formally transferred once the interim mechanism is resolved

Companies with EPR target deadlines falling in the near term (typically March 31 of the compliance year) should flag this interim notice to legal and compliance teams and document that they made good-faith efforts to transfer credits within the available system

PROs acting on behalf of multiple producers should consolidate credit positions across their client portfolios and communicate clearly with all clients about any potential delays in certificate issuance or portal reflection
Submit any grievances or clarification requests to CPCB in writing through official channels — retain copies of all correspondence as evidence of compliance diligence during the interim period
sections.climate_angle: India's e-waste EPR framework is a cornerstone of the country's circular economy strategy — and circular economy performance is increasingly scrutinised under global climate disclosure and ESG reporting standards. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), under IFRS S2, and the European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) both require companies to disclose material risks and opportunities related to resource use, waste management, and supply chain sustainability. For Indian exporters and companies with global investors, EPR compliance is no longer just a domestic regulatory checkbox — it is a data point in Scope 3 emissions accounting and supply chain due diligence. E-waste contains critical minerals — lithium, cobalt, copper, and rare earth elements — whose extraction is carbon-intensive and geopolitically sensitive. A robust domestic recycling ecosystem, underpinned by a functioning EPR credit market, directly reduces India's dependence on virgin material extraction and supports the low-carbon transition of the electronics and clean energy sectors. Disruptions to EPR credit flows — even temporary interim arrangements — can create gaps in recycler cash flows, disincentivising formal recycling and pushing material back into the informal sector, which has significantly higher environmental and social risks. For companies disclosing under the Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework, EPR compliance and e-waste recycling volumes are directly relevant to Principle 2 (sustainable and safe products) and Principle 6 (environmental responsibility). Interim disruptions should be disclosed if material, and compliance teams should be prepared to explain the regulatory context to auditors and rating agencies.
what_to_do: Immediately download and review the full CPCB interim notice from cpcb.nic.in and circulate it to your EPR compliance lead, legal team, and your registered recycler or PRO — do not wait for a portal notification

Conduct a rapid internal audit of your current EPR credit position: how many credits do you need for the current compliance year, how many have been confirmed by your recycler, and how many are pending transfer on the portal — identify any gaps that the interim arrangement may widen

Establish a direct communication protocol with your registered e-waste recycler(s) to receive weekly updates on credit generation volumes and the status of transfers under the interim mechanism — get this in writing

Brief your BRSR and ESG reporting teams on the interim arrangement so that any disclosure related to e-waste EPR compliance for the current financial year accurately reflects the regulatory context and your company's documented compliance efforts

Engage your industry association (such as ICEA, MAIT, or CEAMA) to seek collective clarification from CPCB on timelines for resolution of the interim arrangement and to advocate for a clearly communicated transition back to the standard portal workflow
sections.our_take: CPCB's interim arrangement notice is a reminder that India's EPR ecosystem — while ambitious in design — is still maturing in its operational infrastructure. The e-waste EPR portal has been a significant step forward, but the need for interim credit transfer mechanisms signals that the digital backbone supporting this market must be strengthened urgently. For producers and recyclers, the real risk here is not the interim arrangement itself, but the compliance ambiguity it creates: credits not reflected in the system are credits that cannot be used for target reconciliation, and enforcement actions for shortfalls have been escalating. Looking ahead, we expect CPCB to progressively tighten EPR enforcement across all waste streams — plastics, batteries, tyres, and e-waste — as India positions itself to meet its climate and circular economy commitments. Companies that invest now in robust EPR tracking systems, diversified recycler partnerships, and proactive CPCB engagement will be best placed not just for this interim disruption, but for the more demanding compliance environment that is clearly on the horizon.

posts.title: Framework for EPR Certificate Generation Under E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022: What Producers Must Know
posts.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has released a detailed operational framework for generating Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) certificates under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, tying certificate issuance directly to the recovery of specific key metals — Gold, Copper, Aluminium

sections.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has released a detailed operational framework for generating Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) certificates under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, tying certificate issuance directly to the recovery of specific key metals — Gold, Copper, Aluminium, and Iron — from end-of-life electrical and electronic equipment. Producers of electronics must now track metal-specific recycling obligations tied to item codes, with a phased gold recovery ramp-up starting at 20% of total obligation in FY 2023-24 and scaling to 100% by FY 2028-29. Failure to meet these obligations triggers automatic liability carry-overs or compensatory obligations in non-ferrous metal certificates, making compliance strategy a financial and operational priority.
sections.what_changed: India's E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 established an EPR regime that requires producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) to ensure their products are responsibly recycled at end-of-life. What this new CPCB framework adds is a granular, metal-by-metal certificate system — moving EPR from a broad weight-based recycling target to a chemically-specific accountability model. Under this framework, EPR certificates are no longer generic proof of recycling. They are now issued based on the actual recovery of three classified groups of metals: (1) Precious Metals — specifically Gold (Au); (2) Non-Ferrous Metals — Copper (Cu) and Aluminium (Al); and (3) Ferrous Metals — Iron (Fe), including steel and galvanized iron. Each category of electronic equipment — from mainframes and laptops to mobile phones, refrigerators, and solar panels — has been assigned a specific metal composition percentage in Annexure I, which forms the mathematical basis for calculating a producer's EPR obligation. Critically, because India's current gold recovery infrastructure is limited — CPCB verified that the combined annual gold recovery capacity of 11 certified e-waste recyclers falls short of total producer obligations — the framework introduces a phased ramp-up specifically for gold EPR targets. For copper, aluminium, and iron, the obligation is immediate and set at 100% from the outset. Additionally, the framework provides a sophisticated offset mechanism: producers who over-perform on gold can convert that surplus into reduced non-ferrous metal obligations, and those who under-perform on gold must either carry the deficit forward or purchase additional non-ferrous certificates — creating a dynamic, interconnected compliance calculus.
who_is_affected: Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) of all 45+ categories of electrical and electronic equipment listed under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 — including IT equipment (ITEW codes), consumer electronics (CEEW codes), and solar panels
Manufacturers of smartphones, laptops, tablets, and personal computers — these carry gold (Au) EPR obligations and face the phased ramp-up schedule
Producers of telecom infrastructure equipment such as BTS towers (ITEW18), which carry one of the highest gold composition ratios at 0.0035% by weight
Consumer electronics brands selling TVs, refrigerators, washing machines, and air conditioners — subject to copper, aluminium, and iron EPR obligations at 100% from day one
E-waste recyclers and dismantlers registered with CPCB — they are the certificate-generating entities whose verified metal recovery capacity directly determines market supply of EPR certificates
EPR compliance service providers and recycling aggregators who facilitate certificate trading between recyclers and producers
Solar panel manufacturers and importers (CEEW14) — now formally within the EPR certificate framework for iron and aluminium recovery
Corporate sustainability, legal, and procurement teams responsible for EPR registration, target-setting, and annual reporting to CPCB's centralized portal
key_obligations: IMMEDIATE — Register on CPCB's EPR portal and establish item-wise EPR liability calculations using the metal composition percentages in Annexure I for every EEE category you produce or import
FY 2023-24 (Year 1) — Meet gold (Au) EPR obligation at 20% of total gold liability; meet 100% of copper (Cu), aluminium (Al), and iron (Fe) obligations for all applicable EEE items

FY 2024-25 (Year 2) — Gold EPR target increases to 30% of total gold obligation; any gold deficit from FY 2023-24 carries forward as additional gold liability or triggers a proportional increase in non-ferrous certificate purchases
FY 2025-26 (Year 3) — Gold EPR target increases to 45% of total gold obligation
FY 2026-27 (Year 4) — Gold EPR target increases to 60% of total gold obligation
FY 2027-28 (Year 5) — Gold EPR target increases to 80% of total gold obligation
FY 2028-29 (Year 6) — Full 100% gold EPR obligation must be met; complete phase-in of gold recovery infrastructure expected by this date

ONGOING — For each financial year, calculate and exercise offset options (surplus gold → reduce non-ferrous liability; deficit gold → increase non-ferrous liability or carry forward) before annual compliance filing
ONGOING — Procure EPR certificates only from CPCB-verified recyclers with documented metal recovery capabilities; maintain item-code-level (ITEW/CEEW) certificate records for audit purposes
ONGOING — Monitor CPCB's periodic updates on Rare Earth and other precious material EPR obligations, which are flagged for future inclusion in the certificate regime
sections.climate_angle: This EPR framework is far more than a domestic compliance exercise — it is India's entry point into the global circular economy transition for critical minerals, and its implications for climate strategy are substantial. Gold, copper, aluminium, and the rare earth elements flagged for future inclusion in this framework are all classified as critical minerals essential to the clean energy transition. Copper is the backbone of electric vehicle motors, wind turbines, and power grids. Aluminium is central to lightweight EV design and solar panel frames. Rare earths are indispensable for permanent magnets in wind turbines and EV drivetrains. By building a formal, traceable recycling certificate system for these metals, India is creating the infrastructure for a domestic secondary materials market that reduces dependence on virgin mining — itself a major source of Scope 3 emissions in electronics and clean energy supply chains. From a supply chain risk perspective, companies that master EPR compliance early will also be building the data systems needed to respond to emerging global disclosure requirements. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) under IFRS S2 requires companies to disclose climate-related risks across their value chains — and physical risks to mining regions (from water stress, extreme heat, and flooding) increasingly affect the availability and price of these exact metals. Similarly, the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the EU's Critical Raw Materials Act will soon require Indian exporters supplying European markets to demonstrate responsible sourcing and circular material flows. The metal-specific EPR certificate system being built now will provide precisely the kind of granular, auditable data that these global frameworks demand. There is also a physical risk dimension domestically: as climate change intensifies monsoon variability and urban flooding in Indian cities, informal e-waste processing — which currently dominates — becomes both a toxic hazard and an untracked loss of recoverable materials. Formalizing the recycling chain through this certificate framework is therefore also a climate adaptation measure, reducing community exposure to hazardous substances while securing domestic material stocks.
what_to_do: CONDUCT A METAL LIABILITY AUDIT: Map every EEE product category you produce or import to its ITEW or CEEW code, apply the Annexure I metal composition percentages to your annual quantity placed on market, and calculate your exact gold, copper, aluminium, and iron EPR obligations for FY 2023-24 — this is the foundation of all compliance planning

BUILD YOUR RECYCLER NETWORK STRATEGICALLY: Not all CPCB-registered recyclers can generate gold EPR certificates — only 11 have verified gold recovery capacity. Identify and contract with these verified facilities now, before certificate demand outstrips supply as obligations scale up; simultaneously, secure capacity with copper, aluminium, and iron recyclers for 100% obligation fulfillment

MODEL YOUR OFFSET SCENARIOS: Build a financial model that simulates both over-performance and under-performance on gold obligations and calculates the downstream impact on non-ferrous certificate requirements — this is essential for procurement budgeting and should be reviewed quarterly, not just at year-end

INTEGRATE EPR DATA INTO ESG REPORTING SYSTEMS: Establish item-code-level tracking of EPR certificate procurement and retirement in a format that can feed directly into your BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report) disclosures, ISSB-aligned climate reports, and any future CSRD-compliant value chain disclosures — avoid siloing EPR compliance in a legal team spreadsheet

MONITOR RARE EARTH AND PRECIOUS MATERIAL DEVELOPMENTS: CPCB has explicitly signaled that Rare Earth Elements and other precious materials will be brought into the EPR certificate regime; companies in sectors like EV batteries, wind energy, and advanced electronics should begin inventorying these materials in their products now, so they are not caught off-guard when obligations are formalized
sections.our_take: This framework represents a meaningful maturation of India's e-waste governance — moving from aspirational recycling targets to a chemically-specific, auditable accountability system that mirrors the direction of global circular economy policy. The phased gold ramp-up is pragmatic and reflects an honest acknowledgment of where India's recycling infrastructure actually stands today, rather than where regulations wish it were. However, the compounding nature of the offset mechanism — where gold deficits automatically inflate non-ferrous obligations — means that producers who treat EPR as a year-end box-checking exercise will face escalating financial exposure. The smarter play is to treat this framework as a five-year transition program: invest in verified recycler relationships now, build internal data systems capable of tracking metal-level obligations by product category, and position your company to meet the 100% gold target by FY 2028-29 without scrambling for certificates in a supply-constrained market. Looking ahead, we expect CPCB to progressively tighten the framework — adding rare earth obligations, increasing scrutiny of recycler verification, and potentially linking EPR certificate compliance to product registration or import clearances. Companies that build robust systems today will not only avoid penalties; they will have a competitive advantage as global buyers and investors increasingly demand proof of responsible material stewardship throughout the electronics value chain.

posts.title: Notice to Recyclers: What the CPCB's E-Waste Management Rules 2022 Compliance Crackdown Means for Your Business
posts.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent compliance notice directed at registered e-waste recyclers, signalling a tightening of enforcement under India's E-Waste Management Rules, 2022. Recyclers who fail to meet registration, reporting, and Extended Producer Responsibility (E

sections.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent compliance notice directed at registered e-waste recyclers, signalling a tightening of enforcement under India's E-Waste Management Rules, 2022. Recyclers who fail to meet registration, reporting, and Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) certificate issuance obligations face the risk of licence suspension, penalties, and removal from the CPCB's authorised recycler portal. This notice is a clear signal that India's e-waste regulatory framework is moving from policy articulation to active enforcement — and the entire EPR value chain, from producers to recyclers, must take notice.
sections.what_changed: India's E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 replaced the earlier 2016 rules and introduced a significantly revamped Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework. Under this system, Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) of electrical and electronic equipment are required to meet annual e-waste collection and recycling targets. To fulfil these targets, PIBOs purchase EPR certificates from CPCB-authorised recyclers and refurbishers who process end-of-life electronics. The CPCB's latest notice to recyclers indicates that a substantial number of authorised recyclers are not meeting their core compliance obligations — including filing annual returns, updating processing capacity data on the centralised EPR portal, issuing EPR certificates accurately, and maintaining documentation of e-waste received and processed. The notice serves as a formal warning: non-compliant recyclers risk having their authorisation cancelled, which would directly disrupt the EPR certificate supply that producers depend upon to demonstrate their own compliance. In plain terms, this is the CPCB pulling on a critical thread in the e-waste compliance ecosystem. If recyclers are not compliant, the entire EPR market — including the producers who rely on them — is exposed to regulatory risk.
who_is_affected: CPCB-authorised e-waste recyclers and dismantlers across all states, particularly those operating in major e-waste clusters such as Delhi-NCR, Mumbai, Bengaluru, Chennai, Hyderabad, and Pune.

Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) of electrical and electronic equipment — including consumer electronics, IT equipment, telecom gear, and large/small appliances — who depend on recycler-issued EPR certificates to meet annual compliance targets.
E-waste aggregators and collection point operators whose downstream recycling partners may be at risk of losing authorisation.
Electronics manufacturers with extended supply chains who have entered into EPR fulfilment agreements with third-party recycling agencies.
EPR compliance management platforms and intermediaries facilitating certificate trading on the CPCB portal.
State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) responsible for issuing and monitoring recycler authorisations at the state level.
key_obligations: RECYCLERS — Portal Registration & Profile Update: Ensure your facility profile on the CPCB Centralised EPR Portal (https://eprnewportal.cpcb.gov.in) is current, including actual processing capacity, infrastructure details, and valid authorisation certificates from the relevant SPCB.

RECYCLERS — Annual Returns Filing: File annual returns for the Financial Year 2023-24 immediately if not already submitted. Returns must include total e-waste received (quantity and category), processed quantities, EPR certificates issued, and residual waste disposal records.

RECYCLERS — EPR Certificate Issuance Integrity: Issue EPR certificates only against verified and documented quantities of e-waste actually processed. Inflated or fraudulent certificate issuance is a primary trigger for CPCB enforcement action and can attract criminal liability under the Environment Protection Act.

RECYCLERS — Traceability Documentation: Maintain an unbroken chain of custody — from collection/receipt of e-waste through dismantling, shredding, and downstream processing or safe disposal of hazardous residues. This documentation must be available for CPCB/SPCB inspection at any time.

PRODUCERS/PIBOs — Recycler Due Diligence: Audit the compliance status of all recyclers from whom you are purchasing EPR certificates. A recycler whose authorisation is cancelled retroactively invalidates certificates already purchased — creating a compliance gap for your organisation.

ALL PARTIES — Respond to CPCB Show-Cause Notices: If your organisation has received a specific show-cause notice as part of this enforcement action, a written response must be submitted within the timeframe specified in the notice (typically 15–30 days from receipt). Failure to respond is treated as admission of non-compliance.
ONGOING — Quarterly Reporting: Under the 2022 Rules, recyclers are expected to update processing data on the portal on a quarterly basis. Ensure Q3 and Q4 FY2024-25 data is uploaded on schedule.
sections.climate_angle: E-waste recycling sits at the intersection of the circular economy and climate transition — yet this compliance notice exposes a critical gap between India's regulatory ambitions and ground-level implementation. The formal e-waste recycling sector is a cornerstone of India's resource efficiency strategy: responsible recycling of lithium, cobalt, copper, and rare earth elements from end-of-life electronics is essential for the domestic supply chains that will power the energy transition — from electric vehicles to solar panels and grid storage batteries. From a physical risk and supply chain perspective, the failure of the recycler ecosystem has cascading consequences. If authorised recyclers are non-compliant and lose their licences, e-waste flows back into the informal sector, where hazardous materials such as lead, mercury, cadmium, and brominated flame retardants are processed using methods that contaminate soil and groundwater — creating long-term physical risks for communities and ecosystems near informal processing clusters. For companies reporting under global frameworks, this matters significantly. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) under IFRS S2 requires disclosure of climate-related risks and opportunities across the value chain. E-waste management is increasingly being flagged as a Scope 3 end-of-life treatment issue. Similarly, the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and its European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) require disclosure on circular economy practices and waste. Indian companies with EU market exposure or EU-based investors will face questions about whether their EPR obligations are being met through genuinely compliant, traceable recycling — not certificate arbitrage with non-compliant processors. The CPCB's enforcement action is therefore not just a domestic compliance matter — it is directly relevant to the integrity of ESG disclosures that Indian businesses are making to global investors and regulators.
what_to_do: IMMEDIATE RECYCLER AUDIT: If you are a PIBO, conduct an emergency compliance audit of every recycler in your EPR fulfilment network. Verify their current authorisation status on the CPCB portal, check their annual return filing status, and request copies of their processing logs. Do not wait for a CPCB notice to discover that your EPR certificates are backed by a non-compliant recycler.

PORTAL RECONCILIATION: Log into the CPCB Centralised EPR Portal and reconcile all EPR certificates purchased against the recycler's reported processing quantities. Any discrepancies between certificates purchased and quantities processed should be flagged to your legal and compliance team immediately — this is where regulatory exposure concentrates.

CONTRACTUAL PROTECTIONS: Review and strengthen your EPR fulfilment agreements with recyclers to include compliance warranties, audit rights, and indemnification clauses in the event of recycler authorisation cancellation. Ensure contracts require recyclers to notify you within 48 hours of any CPCB/SPCB show-cause notice or enforcement action.

INTERNAL ESG DISCLOSURE REVIEW: If your company reports under BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting), GRI, or is preparing for ISSB-aligned disclosures, review how e-waste EPR compliance is being characterised in your sustainability reports. Ensure disclosures accurately reflect the compliance status of your recycler network — not just your own certificate procurement.

ENGAGE A COMPLIANCE SPECIALIST: Given the complexity of the EPR portal, annual return requirements, and the risk of retroactive certificate invalidation, companies without dedicated e-waste compliance resources should immediately engage a specialist consultant or legal advisor with CPCB e-waste experience to conduct a gap assessment and remediation plan.
sections.our_take: This CPCB notice is more significant than it may appear at first glance. For years, India's e-waste EPR framework has been criticised for creating a compliance-on-paper ecosystem — where certificates are traded without corresponding real-world recycling activity, and where the informal sector continues to process the vast majority of India's 1.6 million metric tonnes of annual e-waste generation. The CPCB's direct targeting of recyclers is a meaningful escalation: it attacks the supply side of the EPR certificate market, which, if enforced rigorously, will force a reckoning across the entire value chain. Producers who have been treating EPR as a certificate-buying exercise rather than a genuine material recovery programme will find themselves exposed. We expect this enforcement wave to intensify through 2025, with the CPCB likely publishing a list of non-compliant recyclers and potentially suspending portal access for defaulters. The companies that act now — by auditing their recycler networks, strengthening contracts, and building genuine traceability into their EPR programmes — will not only avoid regulatory risk but will be best positioned as India moves toward stricter e-waste targets and as global ESG reporting standards demand credible evidence of circular economy practices.

posts.title: Notice to Producers Regarding Compliance of E-Waste Management Rules, 2022
posts.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice to producers covered under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022, flagging non-compliance with Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations. Producers who manufacture or import electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) are leg

sections.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice to producers covered under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022, flagging non-compliance with Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations. Producers who manufacture or import electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) are legally required to register on the EPR portal, set annual collection targets, and ensure end-of-life management of their products through authorised recyclers. Failure to comply exposes companies to regulatory action, penalties, and reputational risk — making immediate remediation a business priority.
sections.what_changed: The E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 replaced the earlier 2016 framework and significantly strengthened India's approach to managing electronic waste. The 2022 Rules introduced a mandatory, market-based EPR mechanism under which producers must obtain EPR authorisation from the CPCB, register on the centralised EPR portal, set product-category-wise collection targets, and purchase EPR certificates from registered dismantlers and recyclers to demonstrate fulfilment of those targets. CPCB's latest urgent notice signals that a material number of producers have either failed to register on the EPR portal, have not filed required returns, or have not met their prescribed collection targets for the applicable financial year. This notice is a formal enforcement signal — not merely a reminder — and indicates that CPCB is moving from awareness-building to active compliance monitoring and potential prosecution. Companies that remain non-compliant risk having their EPR authorisation cancelled, facing show-cause notices, and being named publicly by the regulator.
who_is_affected: Producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE): Any company that manufactures, imports, or sells EEE in India — including consumer electronics, IT hardware, telecom equipment, lighting, and large/small household appliances — falls under this notice.
Brand Owners and OEMs: Companies that brand or market EEE products, even if manufacturing is outsourced, are classified as 'producers' under the Rules and bear EPR obligations.
E-commerce platforms and importers: Online marketplaces facilitating sale of EEE, as well as importers, carry producer-equivalent responsibilities for products sold through their channels.

Mid-sized and large enterprises: While smaller producers may have phased targets, medium-to-large companies with significant market volumes face the highest absolute collection obligations and are likely the primary audience for this enforcement notice.

Registered Dismantlers and Recyclers: Although the notice targets producers, recyclers and dismantlers in the EPR certificate ecosystem must ensure their documentation and certificate issuance processes are audit-ready to support producer compliance.
key_obligations: EPR Portal Registration (Immediate): Producers not yet registered on the CPCB EPR portal (https://eprnewportal.cpcb.gov.in) must complete registration without delay. Operating without EPR authorisation is a direct violation of the 2022 Rules.

Annual EPR Target Setting: Producers must log collection targets for each product category on the portal for the current financial year (FY 2024-25), calculated as a percentage of quantity of EEE placed on the market in the preceding years as per the Rules' prescribed trajectory.

EPR Certificate Procurement: Producers must purchase EPR certificates from CPCB-authorised recyclers/dismantlers equivalent to their collection targets. Certificates must be linked to the producer's EPR account on the portal.

Annual Return Filing: Producers are required to file annual returns on the EPR portal detailing EEE placed on the market, e-waste collected, and EPR certificates purchased. Returns for FY 2023-24 must be filed as per the prescribed schedule — companies should verify their specific filing deadline on the CPCB portal.

Response to CPCB Notice (Urgent): Any producer who has received a direct notice from CPCB must respond within the stipulated timeframe mentioned in the notice — typically 15–30 days — with documentary evidence of compliance steps taken or an action plan for achieving compliance.

Deposit to Environmental Compensation Fund: Producers unable to meet their EPR targets must deposit the prescribed Environmental Compensation amount, as notified by CPCB, to avoid further penal action.
sections.climate_angle: E-waste compliance is not merely a domestic regulatory checkbox — it sits at the intersection of India's circular economy ambitions, global supply chain due diligence requirements, and climate transition commitments. Electronic waste is one of the fastest-growing waste streams globally, and improper disposal releases toxic substances and greenhouse gases, while responsible recycling recovers critical minerals such as cobalt, lithium, copper, and rare earth elements that are indispensable for the clean energy transition — electric vehicles, wind turbines, and solar panels all depend on these recovered materials. For Indian companies with export operations or multinational investors, EPR compliance is increasingly a supply chain transparency requirement. The EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standards both require companies to account for material resource use and waste across the value chain. A failure to demonstrate responsible end-of-life product management could surface as a material gap in sustainability disclosures, affecting access to green finance and export market eligibility. India's own BRSR Core framework, mandated by SEBI for the top 150 listed companies, explicitly covers waste management and circular economy metrics — making EPR performance directly reportable to investors. Beyond compliance, producers who build robust EPR programmes gain early access to secondary raw material streams, reduce supply chain exposure to volatile virgin material prices, and align with the global narrative of responsible electronics stewardship that institutional ESG investors increasingly scrutinise.
what_to_do: Conduct an immediate EPR compliance audit: Assign your legal, sustainability, or EHS team to verify your company's registration status on the CPCB EPR portal, confirm all product categories are correctly listed, and identify any gaps in EPR certificate procurement versus targets for FY 2023-24 and FY 2024-25.

Engage authorised recyclers and secure EPR certificates: If certificate shortfalls exist, initiate procurement immediately from CPCB-authorised dismantlers and recyclers. Maintain a diversified panel of recycler partners to avoid supply-side constraints and ensure audit-ready documentation for all transactions.

File pending annual returns: Identify any unfiled or incorrectly filed annual returns and submit them on the EPR portal at the earliest. Consider appointing a dedicated compliance officer or engaging a specialist EPR consultant if internal capacity is limited.
