who_is_affected: Indian public sector and private sector banks and NBFCs with SBTi commitments or net-zero pledges (SBI, HDFC Bank, Axis Bank, Kotak, Yes Bank, IDFC First) — their Scope 1, 2, and financed emissions (Scope 3 Category 15) residual abatement strategies are directly implicated

BSE 500 listed companies in hard-to-abate sectors — steel, cement, aluminium, chemicals, aviation — where residual emissions after maximum technical abatement will require high-durability CDR under SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard compliance
Indian conglomerates and exporters facing EU CBAM (Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism) obligations from 2026 and needing credible carbon accounting that distinguishes abatement from removal
CFOs and treasury heads at large listed companies managing voluntary carbon credit portfolios as part of ESG-linked financing covenants or green bond framework commitments
Chief Sustainability Officers at Top 1000 listed companies under SEBI BRSR Core — responsible for Scope 3 disclosure accuracy and residual emissions quantification
ESG analysts and fund managers at domestic and FII institutions running SBTi-aligned or net-zero-aligned equity and fixed income mandates across Indian financials and industrials

India's emerging CDR and carbon project developers — biochar, afforestation, and soil carbon project originators who need to understand where the global quality bar is being set by institutional buyers like TD Bank
key_obligations: SEBI BRSR Core — mandatory for Top 150 listed entities (by market cap) from FY2023-24, Top 250 from FY2024-25: Principle 6 requires disclosure of GHG emissions (Scope 1, 2, and optionally Scope 3) and any carbon offset/removal strategy must be accurately characterised — confusing low-quality avoidance offsets with high-durability removals creates material misstatement risk under SEBI's LODR (Listing Obligations and Disclosure Requirements) Regulations

SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard: Companies with validated near-term and long-term science-based targets must neutralise residual emissions (typically 5-10% of base-year footprint) using 'beyond value chain mitigation' — SBTi's guidance increasingly points toward high-permanence CDR for this category; companies that have made SBTi commitments without a credible CDR procurement roadmap face validation rejection or target withdrawal

India Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) — BEE operationalisation timeline: Obligated entities under the PAT scheme and new CCTS framework must begin understanding how CDR credits will or will not be recognised under India's compliance carbon market; engage with BEE and MoEFCC now before registry rules are locked

EU CBAM — transitional phase reporting obligations active from October 2023, financial obligations from January 2026: Indian steel, aluminium, cement, and fertiliser exporters to the EU must report embedded carbon accurately; any offset or removal claims in carbon accounting will be scrutinised under CBAM's methodological requirements, which do not currently recognise voluntary offsets as reducing CBAM liability

GHG Protocol Corporate Standard — the foundational accounting rule: companies must clearly separate 'avoided emissions,' 'carbon offsets,' and 'carbon removals' in their net-zero accounting; conflating these categories in BRSR or sustainability reports is an audit and investor relations risk
sections.climate_angle: The TD-Climeworks deal reflects the hardening global consensus — embedded in ISSB IFRS S2 climate-related disclosures, the SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard, and the Paris Agreement's Article 6.4 mechanism — that net-zero credibility ultimately rests on the quality and permanence of carbon removals used to neutralise residual emissions, not on the volume of cheap avoidance credits accumulated. For Indian companies, this has a direct BRSR and transition finance consequence. SEBI's BRSR Core framework, which SEBI intends to evolve toward ISSB IFRS S2 alignment (as signalled in SEBI's April 2023 consultation and subsequent communications), will increasingly require companies to disclose not just that they are purchasing carbon credits, but the durability class, vintage, registry, and third-party verification standard of those credits. Indian companies that have built their net-zero narratives on Verra VCS or Gold Standard nature-based credits — many of which are now under ICVCM scrutiny and failing Core Carbon Principles assessments — face a credibility reset with institutional investors running ISSB-aligned or TCFD-successor due diligence. The transition finance implication is equally stark: green bonds, sustainability-linked loans, and ESG-rated instruments issued by Indian companies often embed carbon neutrality or net-zero milestone covenants. If the carbon credits underpinning those milestones are downgraded or invalidated by evolving global standards, covenant breach risk becomes a real treasury and credit risk — not just a reputational one. India's CCTS, when it matures, must grapple with this hierarchy: will it create a high-durability CDR credit category that aligns with global institutional standards, or will it produce a domestic credit architecture that is commercially isolated from the emerging global CDR market that TD Bank and peers are now institutionalising?
what_to_do: CFOs and Treasury teams at SBTi-committed or net-zero-pledged companies: Within the next 90 days, commission an internal audit of your current voluntary carbon credit portfolio — map every credit by technology type, durability class (avoidance vs. removal), registry standard, and ICVCM Core Carbon Principles compliance status; quantify the exposure to credit invalidation or devaluation

Chief Sustainability Officers at Top 250 BRSR Core companies: Review your BRSR Principle 6 disclosures and any sustainability report carbon neutrality claims for alignment with GHG Protocol's distinction between 'offsets' and 'removals'; ensure you are not conflating the two categories in ways that create audit or investor relations exposure under SEBI LODR

Sustainability and finance teams at hard-to-abate sector companies (steel, cement, aluminium, chemicals): Begin scenario modelling for residual emissions under a high-durability CDR procurement requirement; build a 10-year CDR cost curve into your transition plan, recognising that DAC and BECCS credits will likely cost USD 200-600/tonne vs. USD 5-15/tonne for nature-based avoidance credits — this is a material capex and opex planning issue

Board Risk Committees at listed financial institutions with financed emissions disclosures: Place 'carbon credit quality risk in net-zero commitments' on the next board agenda; seek management assurance that CDR procurement strategy is consistent with SBTi validation requirements and forthcoming ISSB S2-aligned SEBI expectations

Government affairs and regulatory engagement teams: Engage proactively with BEE and MoEFCC on CCTS registry design to advocate for a CDR credit category aligned with ICVCM Core Carbon Principles and GHG Protocol removal accounting standards — first-mover input into CCTS architecture is a material competitive advantage

ESG analysts and fund managers: Upgrade your due diligence questionnaire for Indian corporates to explicitly probe the durability class and ICVCM compliance status of carbon credits being used in net-zero claims; companies that cannot answer this question clearly represent an elevated greenwashing and covenant breach risk in your portfolio
sections.our_take: The TD-Climeworks deal is not a curiosity from the Canadian financial services market — it is a leading indicator of where the global institutional standard for net-zero credibility is heading, and India's corporate and regulatory ecosystem has perhaps 24-36 months before that standard becomes an enforceable expectation at the capital markets interface. Here is what I expect to unfold. First, SEBI's BRSR evolution toward ISSB IFRS S2 alignment — which SEBI has signalled at multiple levels — will force a quality distinction between carbon avoidance credits and carbon removal credits in Indian disclosure frameworks, likely within the next two BRSR revision cycles. Companies that have not prepared for this distinction will face restatement risk and investor scrutiny. Second, the CCTS, once it moves beyond PAT-scheme legacy architecture into a genuine carbon price discovery mechanism, will face pressure from international investors and India's own export-sector companies to recognise high-durability CDR credits — particularly as EU CBAM interoperability becomes a commercial necessity. Third, global CDR procurement is entering a decade of rapid cost decline and quality standardisation: DAC costs are projected to fall from USD 1,000/tonne today toward USD 200-300/tonne by 2035 as facilities scale, and institutional buyers like TD Bank are locking in long-term supply now precisely to ride that cost curve. Indian companies that wait until CDR is cheap before building procurement relationships will find themselves at the back of the queue when SBTi or SEBI-equivalent frameworks make high-durability removals a compliance requirement. The boards that understand this today — and begin building CDR literacy into their climate finance strategy now — will have a meaningful first-mover advantage in transition finance access, SBTi validation, and investor-grade net-zero credibility. The boards that do not will be explaining to their audit committees in 2028 why their carbon neutrality claims no longer withstand scrutiny.

posts.title: UK's 87% Emissions Cut by 2040: What India's Exporters, Investors, and Climate Policymakers Must Read Between the Lines
posts.summary: The UK government's proposed Seventh Carbon Budget — capping economy-wide GHG emissions at 535 MtCO2e for 2038–2042 and targeting an 87% reduction from 1990 levels by 2040 — is not a distant policy signal. For Indian exporters with UK supply chain exposure, Indian companies seeking London-listed cap

sections.summary: The UK government's proposed Seventh Carbon Budget — capping economy-wide GHG emissions at 535 MtCO2e for 2038–2042 and targeting an 87% reduction from 1990 levels by 2040 — is not a distant policy signal. For Indian exporters with UK supply chain exposure, Indian companies seeking London-listed capital or UK institutional investment, and India's own domestic climate policy architects, this budget redraws the commercial and regulatory landscape well before 2038. Reporting by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/uk-sets-target-to-reduce-carbon-emissions-87-by-2040/) confirms the budget aligns with the Climate Change Committee's prior recommendation and is now before Parliament for approval. The single most important insight for Indian stakeholders: the UK's sectoral decarbonisation sequencing — energy first, then buildings and transport, then agriculture and land use — will cascade directly into UK procurement standards, import carbon requirements, and investor mandates affecting Indian steel, textiles, chemicals, leather, and agri-commodity exporters. Indian CFOs and CSOs who treat this as a UK-domestic story do so at material commercial risk.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today confirms that UK Energy Secretary Ed Miliband introduced the Seventh Carbon Budget to Parliament on June 2, 2026, adopting the Climate Change Committee's recommended trajectory in full — an 87% reduction in economy-wide emissions against a 1990 baseline, covering the 2038–2042 budget period. This is the most aggressive legally-binding national carbon budget any G7 economy has legislated to date for the 2040 horizon, and it extends a statutory architecture — the Climate Change Act 2008 — that has already delivered a 54% emissions reduction as of 2025, with the pace of reduction more than doubling since carbon budgets were introduced. What this overturns or builds on is critical context: the UK's previous carbon budget trajectory was already considered among the world's most ambitious, but the Seventh Budget explicitly shifts the decarbonisation focus away from the electricity sector (largely accomplished) toward buildings, surface transport, agriculture, and industrial processes. This is the inflection point Indian exporters must internalize. The UK's Scope 3 import supply chain — steel for construction, textiles for retail, leather for fashion, processed foods and agri-commodities — will face mounting carbon content scrutiny as UK buyers themselves come under statutory pressure to decarbonise their value chains under CSRD-equivalent UK reporting obligations (the UK's Sustainability Disclosure Standards, or UK SDS, currently in final DESNZ/FCA consultation). The CCC's analysis explicitly states that net savings only emerge during the Seventh Budget period itself, meaning the near-term (2026–2037) is a period of front-loaded capital deployment and supply chain transformation — precisely when UK corporate procurement will tighten supplier emission requirements. For India's domestic policy architecture, this matters in three specific ways. First, it strengthens the political economy case for India's own Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022, and for the PAT Scheme's evolution into a more rigorous cap-and-trade instrument — UK precedent of 18 years of statutory carbon budgets delivering results will be cited by MoEFCC and BEE in inter-ministerial debates. Second, SEBI's BRSR Core mandatory disclosures (applicable to the top 150 listed companies by market cap for FY2023-24, expanding to top 250 for FY2024-25 and beyond) already require Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG disclosures with reasonable assurance — the UK budget accelerates the timeline by which Indian companies' Scope 3 data will be demanded by UK-based institutional investors and lenders. Third, Indian companies with UK-listed GDRs, UK institutional shareholders, or UK bank credit facilities will find that their UK counterparties' own Seventh Budget compliance plans will require them to engage, monitor, and in some cases set contractual carbon reduction milestones with Indian suppliers.
who_is_affected: Indian steel and aluminium manufacturers exporting to UK construction, automotive, and infrastructure supply chains — facing embedded carbon scrutiny as UK industry decarbonises under the Seventh Budget's industrial emissions pathway
Indian textile, apparel, and leather exporters with UK retail customers — UK brands under UK SDS and CSRD-equivalent rules will demand Scope 3 supplier emission disclosures and reduction roadmaps

Indian agri-commodity processors and food exporters — the Seventh Budget's explicit targeting of agriculture and land use emissions in the 2038–2042 window will accelerate UK buyer due diligence on deforestation, methane, and fertiliser-linked emissions in Indian supply chains

BSE 500 listed companies with UK institutional shareholders (Schroders, Aviva, Legal & General, HSBC Asset Management) — these investors are now operating under the most stringent domestic statutory carbon obligation in the G7 and will intensify engagement on Indian portfolio company climate targets, BRSR Core disclosures, and SBTi alignment

Indian companies raising capital via London Stock Exchange (LSE) or seeking UK Export Finance (UKEF) or British International Investment (BII) funding — carbon budget compliance will tighten ESG due diligence requirements for UK public finance institutions

CFOs and Treasurers at Indian conglomerates with UK subsidiaries or JVs — intercompany transfer pricing and consolidated GHG accounting will need to reflect UK subsidiary decarbonisation capex obligations
CSOs and Sustainability Heads at Indian listed companies — BRSR Core's Scope 3 disclosure trajectory will increasingly be benchmarked against UK and EU supply chain standards

Risk Committees and Audit Committees at Indian banks with UK correspondent banking relationships or UK wholesale funding — climate transition risk in UK-exposed loan books requires updated scenario analysis under RBI's climate risk guidance
key_obligations: UK Seventh Carbon Budget (535 MtCO2e cap): Parliament approval expected H2 2026; delivery plan to follow — Indian companies with UK exposure should begin supply chain carbon mapping now, ahead of UK procurement tightening in 2027–2030

SEBI BRSR Core — Scope 1 & 2 GHG disclosures with reasonable assurance: mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24; top 250 from FY2024-25; Scope 3 value chain disclosures currently on a comply-or-explain basis but expected to become mandatory by FY2026-27 per SEBI's stated trajectory

UK Sustainability Disclosure Standards (UK SDS): FCA and DESNZ consultation ongoing as of mid-2026; expected mandatory applicability to UK-listed and large UK-registered companies from 2027 — Indian exporters supplying UK corporates should anticipate Scope 3 supplier data requests from 2027 onwards

ISSB IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures): India has not yet mandated ISSB adoption, but ICAI's Sustainability Standards Board (SSB) is developing India-specific sustainability standards aligned to ISSB — the UK budget's ambition accelerates ISSB's relevance for Indian companies with UK investor or lender exposure

EU CBAM (Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism): While UK-specific CBAM legislation is still under consultation, the Seventh Budget's industrial decarbonisation ambition makes a UK CBAM for steel, aluminium, cement, chemicals, and fertilisers politically and technically more likely by 2028–2030 — Indian exporters in these sectors must begin embedded carbon accounting now

India's CCTS (Carbon Credit Trading Scheme) under Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022: Draft rules notified; market launch expected 2026–2027 — the UK's statutory carbon budget precedent will be cited in regulatory impact assessments; Indian companies in energy-intensive sectors should engage BEE on PAT-to-CCTS transition planning

SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard: Indian companies with UK institutional shareholders or UK supply chain customers will face accelerating pressure to submit SBTi targets — CFOs should assess SBTi submission timelines against the UK's 2040 milestone as a reference point for near-term target ambition
sections.climate_angle: The UK's Seventh Carbon Budget is structurally significant for India's transition finance architecture because it confirms that the world's most advanced statutory carbon budget regimes are now moving past power sector decarbonisation into the hard-to-abate sectors — industry, buildings, transport, and agriculture — that represent the core of India's own emissions challenge and export economy. The CCC's sequencing analysis is a roadmap that will be mirrored, with a lag, in India's own policy evolution under the National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC) and India's updated NDC (targeting a 45% emissions intensity reduction by 2030 against 2005 levels and 50% non-fossil electricity capacity). For Indian transition finance, the UK budget reinforces three capital flow trends that CFOs and ESG investors must price. First, UK and European institutional capital will increasingly apply a carbon budget lens to emerging market allocations — Indian companies without credible Scope 1, 2, and 3 reduction pathways aligned to a 1.5°C or well-below-2°C trajectory will face higher cost of capital from UK asset managers operating under Paris-aligned investment mandates. Second, the UK's emphasis on electrification as the primary mechanism — consistent with ISSB IFRS S2's requirement to disclose climate-related transition risks and opportunities — creates a direct commercial opportunity for Indian renewable energy companies, EV manufacturers, and battery storage players seeking UK partnership or investment. Third, the agriculture and land use decarbonisation focus in the Seventh Budget's later phase directly intersects with TNFD's nature-related financial disclosure framework, which is gaining traction with UK institutional investors — Indian agri-business companies with UK exposure will need to demonstrate land use and biodiversity risk management capabilities that go well beyond current BRSR reporting. The BRSR's Principal 6 (environment) disclosures on water, land, and biodiversity are a starting point, but are not yet calibrated to TNFD's LEAP approach or to the granularity UK supply chain due diligence will eventually require.
what_to_do: Sustainability/ESG teams at Indian exporters with UK revenue exposure (>5% of turnover): initiate a Scope 3 Category 11 (use of sold products) and Category 1 (purchased goods/services) mapping exercise within 90 days — UK buyers will begin including carbon data requirements in RFPs from 2027 onwards

CFOs at BSE 500 companies with UK institutional shareholders: schedule a climate disclosure readiness review against BRSR Core's current reasonable assurance requirements and map gaps to ISSB IFRS S2 — proactively brief UK investors on your Scope 1/2 trajectory before your next AGM season

Board Risk Committees: add UK CBAM watch-item to climate transition risk register — commission a scenario analysis on the financial impact of a UK carbon border levy on your top 3 export product lines, using EU CBAM pricing as a reference scenario

CSOs at energy-intensive manufacturers (steel, cement, chemicals, aluminium, textiles): engage BEE now on PAT-to-CCTS transition planning and assess whether your current PAT cycle performance supports an SBTi submission aligned to a well-below-2°C pathway — use the UK's 87% by 2040 milestone as an external benchmark in board discussions on target ambition

Procurement and supply chain heads at Indian companies with UK subsidiary operations: audit your UK subsidiary's exposure to Seventh Budget sectoral decarbonisation obligations — particularly in buildings, transport fleet, and industrial energy — and model the capex implications for FY2027–2032 planning cycles

Investor Relations teams: proactively disclose in your next sustainability report or BRSR that you are monitoring the UK Seventh Carbon Budget's supply chain implications — this signals climate risk governance maturity to UK and EU ESG-screened funds and differentiates you in a tightening capital allocation environment

Company Secretaries and Audit Committee Chairs: ensure that the UK Seventh Carbon Budget and anticipated UK SDS obligations are reflected in your climate-related material risk disclosures under BRSR's Leadership Indicators and, where applicable, under any ISSB-aligned disclosures you are piloting ahead of mandatory adoption
sections.our_take: The UK's Seventh Carbon Budget is a 14-year statutory commitment that will reshape global supply chain carbon economics more profoundly than any single corporate ESG framework update. What boards and investors must watch is not the 2040 headline — it is the 2026–2030 delivery plan that will follow Parliamentary approval. That plan will specify sectoral decarbonisation trajectories, building regulation upgrades, industrial energy transition subsidies, and, critically, the UK's approach to carbon leakage prevention — which is where India's export economy faces its most material near-term exposure. A UK CBAM covering steel, aluminium, cement, and chemicals is no longer a question of if but when, and the Seventh Budget's ambition makes a 2029–2030 UK CBAM introduction politically and technically credible. For India's own climate policy trajectory, I expect the UK's statutory carbon budget model to feature prominently in MoEFCC and CCTS design debates over the next 24 months. India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme will face domestic industry pressure to remain 'flexible,' but the international evidence — 54% UK emissions reduction since 2008, delivered through statutory budget discipline — will be a powerful counter-argument for rigour. SEBI's BRSR trajectory is already moving in this direction: reasonable assurance on Scope 1/2, voluntary Scope 3, and a clear runway toward mandatory value chain disclosures. The UK budget accelerates the timeline by which Indian listed companies will find their BRSR disclosures being stress-tested by UK and European institutional investors against externally validated, Paris-aligned benchmarks. Indian CFOs who treat BRSR as a compliance checkbox rather than a capital markets communication tool are systematically underestimating the repricing risk they carry into the next investment cycle. The boards that invest in transition planning now — before UK procurement standards, UK SDS obligations, and a potential UK CBAM crystallise into contractual and regulatory hard stops — will have a material cost of capital and market access advantage over those that wait for regulatory compulsion.

posts.title: SLR's Acquisition of Anchor Environmental Signals a Global Arms Race in Natural Capital Advisory — Here's What Indian Boards Must Read Into It
posts.summary: SLR Consulting's acquisition of Cape Town-based Anchor Environmental Consultants — reported by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/slr-acquires-natural-capital-advisory-firm-anchor-environmental-consultants/) — is not merely an M&A footnote. It is a leading indicator that global ESG consulting infra

sections.summary: SLR Consulting's acquisition of Cape Town-based Anchor Environmental Consultants — reported by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/slr-acquires-natural-capital-advisory-firm-anchor-environmental-consultants/) — is not merely an M&A footnote. It is a leading indicator that global ESG consulting infrastructure is rapidly consolidating around natural capital, marine biodiversity, and ecosystem services valuation capabilities, driven by the hardening of TNFD disclosure expectations and the emergence of nature-as-risk in institutional investment mandates. For Indian CFOs and CSOs at companies with coastal, port, offshore energy, mining, or aquaculture exposure, this signals that natural capital accounting and biodiversity impact assessment are transitioning from voluntary best practice to audit-grade disclosure requirements — and that the specialist advisory capacity to meet those requirements is being locked up by global consulting platforms. Indian companies that delay building internal capability or securing credible external advisory relationships risk being materially under-resourced precisely when regulatory and investor scrutiny peaks.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today on 2 June 2026 confirms that SLR has acquired Anchor Environmental Consultants, a 45-person South Africa and Namibia-based specialist firm with deep expertise in marine taxonomy, ecosystem services assessment, natural capital accounting, and ecological-social-economic (ESE) impact evaluation. Anchor's client roster spans governments, international finance institutions (IFIs), mining majors, offshore energy developers, port operators, and aquaculture companies — an almost precise map of the sectors that face the sharpest near-term scrutiny under the Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) framework adopted in September 2023 and now being embedded into regulatory architecture globally. The deal follows SLR's acquisitions of climate-analytics platforms Planetrics and ClimSystems, sustainability advisory firm SB&CO, and ESG data provider WAP Sustainability — a deliberate platform-building strategy combining physical climate risk modelling, ESG data infrastructure, corporate sustainability advisory, and now nature and marine science. The strategic logic is unmistakable: SLR is assembling the full-stack capability stack that large-cap companies and project finance lenders will need to satisfy TNFD-aligned disclosures, biodiversity net gain assessments, and natural capital valuation for financial reporting. For India, the implications are concrete and urgent. SEBI's BRSR Core framework — mandatory for the top 150 listed companies by market capitalisation for FY2023-24, expanding to the top 250 for FY2024-25 onwards — already requires disclosure under Principle 6 on biodiversity, ecosystems, and environmental impacts. BRSR Core's Leadership Indicators ask companies about proximity of operations to biodiversity-sensitive areas, impacts on ecosystem services, and biodiversity conservation measures. These disclosures are currently qualitative and self-reported, but SEBI has signalled progressive assurance requirements. Simultaneously, MoEFCC's EIA Notification 2006 and its Draft EIA Notification 2020 (still under deliberation), the Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) Notification 2019, and the Wetlands (Conservation and Management) Rules 2017 collectively create a regulatory perimeter around natural capital impacts for Indian project developers that is already legally enforceable — but systematically under-disclosed in capital market reporting. The gap between project-level environmental compliance and investor-grade natural capital disclosure is the precise gap that firms like Anchor-SLR are positioning to fill globally, and that gap exists in India at significant scale.
who_is_affected: Port and maritime infrastructure developers: Adani Ports, JSW Infrastructure, and state port trusts with coastal and estuarine project pipelines facing CRZ and EIA compliance plus emerging TNFD disclosure pressure

Offshore energy developers: Companies pursuing offshore wind (Gujarat, Tamil Nadu coast), offshore gas (ONGC, Reliance), and green hydrogen/ammonia export projects requiring marine ESIA and natural capital baseline assessments

Mining and minerals companies: Coal India, NMDC, Vedanta, Hindalco — operating near forest, wetland, and coastal biodiversity-sensitive zones — facing both MoEFCC Forest Conservation Act compliance and TNFD-linked investor disclosure expectations

Aquaculture and fisheries sector: Mid-to-large aquaculture exporters (shrimp processing, marine products) supplying EU and US markets where buyers and lenders are beginning to require TNFD-aligned supply chain disclosures

Infrastructure and industrial conglomerates: BSE 500 companies with operations in coastal, riverine, or forested geographies for whom BRSR Core Principle 6 disclosures require ecosystem services impact assessment

CFOs and CSOs at top-150 listed companies: Responsible for BRSR Core assurance, now facing the prospect that biodiversity disclosures will require third-party verification aligned to TNFD LEAP methodology

ESG-focused institutional investors and DFIs: IFC, ADB, NaBFID, and domestic ESG funds with project finance or equity exposure to coastal or resource-extraction sectors where natural capital risk is a material but unquantified liability

Audit committee members and statutory auditors: As SEBI moves toward reasonable assurance on BRSR Core metrics, auditors will need to assess the quality of biodiversity and ecosystem service disclosures — currently an area with almost no Indian audit firm capability
key_obligations: BRSR Core — Principle 6 biodiversity disclosures: Mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24 filings; expanding to top 250 from FY2024-25; limited assurance required on Core KPIs — companies must disclose operations near biodiversity-sensitive areas and impacts on ecosystem services

TNFD v1.0 Framework (September 2023): While not yet mandatorily integrated into SEBI rules, TNFD adoption is being tracked by global institutional investors; companies with foreign institutional investor (FII) shareholding above 15-20% face informal disclosure expectations from FY2025-26 onwards

MoEFCC EIA Notification 2006 / Draft EIA 2020: Any project requiring environmental clearance in CRZ, forest, or ecologically sensitive zone must conduct biodiversity impact assessment — but this data is rarely translated into investor-grade natural capital accounting

CRZ Notification 2019: Coastal development projects must demonstrate no net loss to mangrove, coral, and coastal wetland ecosystems — a natural capital obligation that will increasingly be referenced in ESG due diligence by lenders and equity investors

Wetlands (Conservation and Management) Rules 2017: Operations near notified wetlands require state wetland authority clearance — material for ESG risk disclosure but routinely absent from BRSR or annual report disclosures

SEBI's ESG Disclosure Consultation (anticipated FY2026-27): SEBI is expected to consult on aligning BRSR more explicitly with ISSB IFRS S1/S2 and TNFD; companies should anticipate that nature-related financial disclosures will be formally incorporated within a 24-36 month horizon

International project finance lenders (IFC Performance Standards, Equator Principles IV): Any project seeking DFI or Equator Principles-aligned financing must already meet IFC PS6 on biodiversity — this is contractually binding today, not aspirational
sections.climate_angle: The SLR-Anchor deal crystallises a critical but underappreciated dimension of India's climate transition: nature and climate are inseparable risk categories, and the infrastructure to manage both is now being consolidated at speed in global consulting markets. India's NDC 2.0 commitments include creating an additional carbon sink of 2.5-3 billion tonnes of CO₂ equivalent through forests and tree cover — a nature-based solution target that requires precisely the kind of ecosystem services accounting and natural capital valuation that Anchor specialises in. Yet India's domestic capacity in this space — particularly for marine, coastal, and freshwater ecosystems — remains thin and fragmented. For companies pursuing green hydrogen and ammonia export projects along India's western and southern coastlines (a pipeline now exceeding 50 GW in announced capacity), ESIA work for coastal and offshore infrastructure is a non-negotiable regulatory and lender requirement. The overlap with SLR-Anchor's exact project experience — including prior collaboration on green hydrogen/ammonia ESIA in Southern Africa — is direct. Indian project developers partnering with SLR or equivalent global platforms will need to budget for natural capital baseline assessments as a standard project development cost, not a discretionary add-on. More broadly, as ISSB IFRS S2 climate disclosures (which SEBI is expected to use as the basis for future BRSR evolution) explicitly require physical risk assessments that incorporate ecosystem dependencies — water, coastal protection, soil stability — the boundary between climate disclosure and nature disclosure is dissolving. Indian companies that treat these as separate compliance workstreams are building a disclosure architecture that will require costly reconstruction within three to five years. The BRSR-to-TNFD integration is not a question of if, but when, and the companies that begin TNFD LEAP assessments now — identifying their locate, evaluate, assess, and prepare obligations — will be positioned to lead, not scramble.
what_to_do: Sustainability / ESG team (immediate, within 60 days): Conduct a rapid screen of all operating sites and project pipelines against MoEFCC's list of ecologically sensitive zones, CRZ categories, notified wetlands, and proximity to Protected Area networks — map this against current BRSR Principle 6 disclosures to identify gaps before the next annual report cycle

CFO / treasury team (within 90 days): Assess all project finance facilities, green bonds, and sustainability-linked loans for IFC Performance Standard 6 and Equator Principles IV covenants — identify where natural capital and biodiversity obligations are contractually binding today and whether current monitoring data satisfies those covenants

CSO / Head of Sustainability (within 6 months): Commission a TNFD LEAP pilot assessment for the company's top two or three highest-impact business units — this creates the internal data architecture needed for future BRSR Core assurance on biodiversity metrics and demonstrates proactive governance to ESG-focused institutional investors

Board Risk Committee (next quarterly meeting): Add nature-related financial risk as a standing agenda item, distinct from but linked to climate risk — request management to report on ecosystem service dependencies (water, coastal protection, pollination, soil) that are material to business continuity, referencing TNFD and SEBI BRSR Principle 6 as the governance framework

Procurement / Supply Chain team (within 6 months): For companies in aquaculture, forestry products, mining, or coastal manufacturing — begin mapping Tier 1 supplier exposure to biodiversity-sensitive geographies, as EU CSRD's ESRS E4 (biodiversity and ecosystems) will apply to EU-market supply chains and will flow upstream to Indian exporters via contractual due diligence requirements by FY2027-28

Investor Relations team (before next AGM): Proactively brief institutional investors — particularly FIIs and ESG-mandated domestic funds — on the company's approach to natural capital risk, referencing TNFD and BRSR Principle 6, to pre-empt shareholder questions and signal governance maturity ahead of SEBI's expected tightening of biodiversity disclosure standards
sections.our_take: The SLR-Anchor acquisition is a small transaction by global M&A standards, but it is a highly legible signal about where ESG consulting infrastructure and regulatory pressure are converging. The global consulting market is quietly but decisively reorganising itself around nature and biodiversity capability — because the regulatory demand signal is now strong enough to justify platform-scale investment. TNFD has achieved in 18 months what TCFD took five years to accomplish: it is being embedded into financial regulation, lender covenants, and investment mandates at a pace that has surprised even its architects. In India, the regulatory hook is already in place — BRSR Principle 6, CRZ, EIA, Wetlands Rules, Forest Conservation Act — but the translation from project-level environmental compliance to investor-grade natural capital disclosure has not happened. That translation is now being forced by three simultaneous pressures: SEBI's progressive assurance requirements on BRSR Core, DFI and green finance lender covenants referencing IFC PS6 and Equator Principles IV, and EU CSRD's extraterritorial reach into Indian export supply chains via ESRS E4. Indian boards should expect that by FY2028, natural capital disclosures will be subject to the same level of third-party assurance scrutiny as Scope 1 and 2 emissions data today — and the companies that have not begun building the internal data systems and external advisory relationships to support that assurance will face the same credibility crisis that unprepared companies faced when BRSR Core GHG disclosures were first required. The competitive advantage in the next phase of ESG maturity in India will not go to companies that comply fastest with minimum disclosure requirements — it will go to companies that convert natural capital data into a genuine strategic asset: lower cost of capital from nature-positive financing instruments, stronger export market access as EU and UK biodiversity due diligence regulations mature, and reduced regulatory risk from proactive MoEFCC and state environmental authority engagement. The SLR-Anchor deal is a prompt for Indian boards to ask a simple question: do we know what our nature-related financial risks are? For most BSE 500 companies, the honest answer is no — and that is exactly the gap that will define the next frontier of ESG accountability in India.

posts.title: CPCB Tightens the Screws: What the Notice to E-Waste Recyclers Means for EPR Compliance
posts.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued a notice directing registered e-waste recyclers to strictly comply with the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, signaling heightened scrutiny of the EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) credit ecosystem. The move is a clear warning shot: recycler

sections.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued a notice directing registered e-waste recyclers to strictly comply with the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, signaling heightened scrutiny of the EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) credit ecosystem. The move is a clear warning shot: recyclers generating EPR certificates without verifiable processing capacity or audit trails face deregistration, and producers buying those certificates face downstream compliance risk.
sections.what_changed: Under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022, EPR obligations are fulfilled through a digital portal where producers, importers, brand owners (PIBOs) purchase EPR certificates generated by CPCB-registered recyclers. Concerns have emerged about the integrity of these certificates — specifically, whether some recyclers are issuing certificates beyond their physical processing capacity or without genuine collection and dismantling activity. The CPCB notice reiterates that recyclers must operate strictly within their registered capacity, maintain robust documentation of inbound e-waste and outbound recovered materials, and ensure that EPR certificates correspond to actual quantities processed. Non-compliance can trigger suspension, cancellation of registration, and recovery of environmental compensation.
who_is_affected: CPCB-registered e-waste recyclers and dismantlers across India
Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) — including consumer electronics, IT hardware, telecom, white goods, and medical devices
Refurbishers and bulk consumers of EEE participating in the EPR portal
Aggregators and collection agencies in the e-waste value chain
Auditors, ESG consultants, and verification agencies supporting EPR filings
key_obligations: Recyclers must process e-waste strictly within their CPCB-approved annual capacity — no over-issuance of EPR certificates
Maintain complete chain-of-custody records: inbound manifests, weighbridge slips, dismantling logs, downstream material flow
File quarterly and annual returns on the EPR portal accurately and on time
PIBOs must verify the authenticity of EPR certificates purchased — including the recycler's registered capacity and audit status
Cooperate fully with CPCB and SPCB inspections; deficiencies must be rectified within timelines specified in show-cause notices
Environmental compensation will be levied for shortfalls in EPR obligation fulfilment as per the schedule under the 2022 Rules
sections.climate_angle: E-waste is not just a hazardous waste issue — it is a circular economy and climate issue. Recovering critical minerals (copper, gold, rare earths, lithium, cobalt) from end-of-life electronics reduces virgin mining emissions and strengthens supply chain resilience as India scales electronics manufacturing under PLI schemes. Globally, frameworks like ISSB's IFRS S2, the EU's CSRD/ESRS E5 (Resource Use and Circular Economy), and the EU Battery Regulation are pushing companies to disclose product-level circularity metrics. Indian PIBOs selling into export markets — or supplying global brands — will increasingly be asked to demonstrate not just EPR compliance on paper, but verifiable, audited material recovery. CPCB's crackdown on certificate integrity is a precursor to the kind of assurance-grade EPR data that international buyers and investors will demand.
what_to_do: Recyclers: Conduct an internal audit reconciling registered capacity, actual throughput, and EPR certificates issued for the past two fiscal years; fix discrepancies before CPCB does
PIBOs: Diligence your EPR certificate suppliers — request capacity certificates, audit reports, and physical site visits before bulk procurement
Build a digital traceability system linking your EEE sales data, end-of-life collection, and certificate procurement to withstand regulatory and ESG audits
Update internal ESG and BRSR disclosures to reflect actual e-waste recovered and any contingent liabilities from EPR shortfalls
Engage with PROs (Producer Responsibility Organisations) and recyclers on long-term contracts tied to physical recovery, not just paper compliance
sections.our_take: This notice should be read as the opening move in a broader cleanup of India's EPR ecosystem — we expect similar scrutiny to extend to plastic, battery, and tyre waste EPR regimes over the next 12-18 months. Producers that have treated EPR as a low-cost compliance line item, purchasing the cheapest available certificates, are now exposed to both regulatory clawback and ESG-reporting risk. The winners will be companies that integrate EPR into their broader circular economy strategy: investing in reverse logistics, partnering with credible recyclers, and disclosing recovered material metrics in line with global standards. Paper compliance is ending; physical, auditable circularity is the new baseline.

posts.title: CPCB's New EPR Certificate Framework for E-Waste: What Producers Need to Know About Metal-Based Targets
posts.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has released a framework that fundamentally changes how Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) certificates are generated under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 — moving from a unit/weight-based system to one anchored in actual metal recovery (Gold, Cop

sections.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has released a framework that fundamentally changes how Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) certificates are generated under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 — moving from a unit/weight-based system to one anchored in actual metal recovery (Gold, Copper, Aluminium, and Iron). Producers must now meet differentiated targets per metal, with Gold obligations phased in over five years (starting at just 20% in FY 2023-24) while Non-Ferrous and Ferrous obligations apply at 100% from year one. This shift directly links EPR compliance to India's critical minerals security and circular economy ambitions, and demands an urgent rethink of producer recycler contracts.
sections.what_changed: Until now, EPR obligations under e-waste rules were largely expressed as a percentage of the weight of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) placed on the market. The new CPCB framework converts that obligation into specific quantities of recovered key metals — Gold (Au), Copper (Cu), Aluminium (Al), and Iron/Steel (Fe) — based on standardized average material composition tables (Annexure I) published item-wise across 45+ EEE categories. Because India's verified gold recovery capacity from e-waste (across 11 CPCB-audited recyclers) is currently far below aggregate producer obligations, CPCB has introduced a glide path: gold EPR obligations start at 20% in FY 2023-24, rising to 30%, 45%, 60%, 80%, and finally 100% by FY 2028-29. Non-ferrous and ferrous obligations, however, kick in at 100% immediately. Producers can also trade off over- or under-performance between gold and non-ferrous targets within defined rules, and Rare Earth Elements (REE) recovery will be separately incentivized in the future.
who_is_affected: Producers (manufacturers, importers, and brand owners) of all EEE categories listed in Schedule I of the E-Waste Rules 2022 — IT and telecom equipment (ITEW1–ITEW27), consumer electronics and white goods (CEEW1–CEEW17), and lighting products
Electronics OEMs and importers of laptops, mobile phones, tablets, televisions, refrigerators, washing machines, air conditioners, and solar PV modules
Telecom infrastructure companies — particularly those deploying Base Transceiver Stations (BTS), which carry the highest gold composition (0.0035%) among listed items
Registered e-waste recyclers, who must now demonstrate verified metal recovery capacity to issue EPR certificates
Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) and EPR aggregators managing compliance portfolios
Solar PV module producers (CEEW14), now formally drawn into metal-based recovery targets for Iron and Aluminium
key_obligations: FY 2023-24: Meet 20% of gold EPR obligation; 100% of Copper, Aluminium, and Iron obligations
FY 2024-25: Gold obligation rises to 30%
FY 2025-26 and FY 2026-27: Gold obligation at 45% and 60% respectively
FY 2027-28: Gold obligation rises to 80%
FY 2028-29: Full 100% gold EPR obligation kicks in — closing the five-year capacity gap
Calculate EPR liability per EEE category using CPCB's Annexure I composition tables (e.g., 0.0015% Au in cellular phones; 11% Cu and 0.0035% Au in BTS)
Procure EPR certificates from CPCB-verified recyclers against verified metal recovery — not equipment weight
Track surplus/deficit performance per EEE item; either carry-forward gold liabilities to next FY or offset against non-ferrous purchases in the same FY
Maintain auditable records of certificate purchases by metal category to support CPCB filings on the EPR portal
sections.climate_angle: This framework is far more than a waste management adjustment — it is India's most concrete operationalization of the circular economy for critical minerals. Gold, copper, and aluminium recovered from e-waste directly displace virgin mining, with copper recycling alone consuming up to 85% less energy than primary production. For producers, this means EPR compliance is now a direct lever on Scope 3 emissions and supply chain resilience. The framework also aligns India's regime with global directions: the EU's Critical Raw Materials Act, the ISSB's IFRS S2 requirement to disclose climate-related risks in value chains, and CSRD's expectations around resource use and circular economy (ESRS E5). Companies reporting under BRSR Core will find these metal-recovery metrics flowing directly into Principle 2 and Principle 6 disclosures. The explicit signal that Rare Earth Elements will be incentivized next is a clear pointer that producers of EVs, wind turbines, and advanced electronics should start mapping REE recovery pathways now.
what_to_do: Recompute your EPR liability per EEE category using Annexure I composition factors — your existing weight-based estimates are now obsolete. Build a metal-by-metal obligation matrix for FY 2023-24 through FY 2028-29.

Audit your recycler contracts: confirm that your downstream partners are among the CPCB-verified recyclers with demonstrated gold recovery capacity, and renegotiate certificate pricing now before market tightness pushes premiums up.

Decide your offset strategy: for each EEE SKU, model whether it is more cost-effective to carry forward gold deficits or to over-procure non-ferrous certificates in the current year — this is a treasury and procurement decision, not just a sustainability one.
Set up internal MIS to track surplus/deficit per EEE item per metal — CPCB's offset mechanism requires SKU-level traceability and cannot be managed at portfolio aggregate level.
Begin scenario planning for Rare Earth Element recovery, especially if you manufacture or import devices with magnets, batteries, or specialty optics — this is the next regulatory frontier.
sections.our_take: CPCB's metal-based EPR framework is a quietly transformative move. By tying compliance to actual recovered metals rather than equipment weight, India is forcing the e-waste value chain to invest in real metallurgical capacity — not paper-trail compliance. The phased gold trajectory is a pragmatic acknowledgment that domestic recycling infrastructure cannot yet absorb full obligations, but the message to producers is unambiguous: capacity must scale, and certificate prices will reflect scarcity. Expect a sharp run-up in gold and BTS-linked certificate costs over the next 18 months, consolidation among formal recyclers, and growing scrutiny from auditors mapping these obligations into BRSR Core and ISSB-aligned disclosures. Producers who treat this as a procurement-only exercise will be blindsided; those who integrate it into product design (design-for-disassembly, recovered-content targets) and supplier engagement will turn compliance cost into competitive advantage. The REE signal at the end of the notice is the one to watch — that is where the next compliance wave, and the next material-risk disclosure obligation, will land.

posts.title: CPCB Notice: Interim Arrangement for E-Waste EPR Credit Generation and Transfer by Recyclers
posts.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an interim arrangement governing how registered E-Waste Recyclers generate and transfer EPR end-product credits (certificates) on the EPR Portal. This is a transitional measure under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, designed to keep the EPR

sections.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an interim arrangement governing how registered E-Waste Recyclers generate and transfer EPR end-product credits (certificates) on the EPR Portal. This is a transitional measure under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, designed to keep the EPR credit market functional while the digital traceability framework is being stabilized. Producers, refurbishers, and recyclers should align their internal documentation and reconciliation processes immediately to avoid disruption in EPR target fulfilment.
sections.what_changed: Under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations are met through a credit-based mechanism. Registered recyclers process end-of-life electronics and generate EPR certificates on the CPCB's EPR Portal, which producers then purchase to discharge their annual recycling targets. Because portal-based generation and transfer of these certificates has faced operational and verification challenges, CPCB has notified an interim arrangement. In practice, this means recyclers may generate and transfer EPR end-product credits under a defined manual or semi-automated workflow — with prescribed documentation, quantity verification, and audit trails — until the full digital functionality is operationalised. Transactions executed under this interim window will remain valid for EPR compliance, provided recyclers and producers maintain the records CPCB specifies.
who_is_affected: Registered E-Waste Recyclers operating under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022
Producers (manufacturers, importers, and brand owners) of EEE listed in Schedule-I with EPR obligations
Refurbishers handling used electronic equipment under registered EPR pathways
Bulk consumers and large IT/ITeS enterprises disposing of e-waste through registered recyclers
ESG and compliance teams in electronics, appliances, telecom, and consumer durables sectors
key_obligations: Recyclers must generate EPR end-product credits strictly in line with the interim procedure specified by CPCB on the EPR Portal
Credits should be transferred only against verified quantities of processed e-waste, supported by inward manifests, processing records, and end-product evidence
Maintain a complete audit trail — weighbridge slips, processing logs, downstream sale invoices for recovered materials — for all interim-period transactions
Producers purchasing credits must conduct due diligence on the recycler's registration status and verify the credit basis before recording it against EPR targets
All interim transactions remain subject to retrospective verification by CPCB; any inflated or unsubstantiated credits may be cancelled and trigger enforcement action
Comply with annual EPR filing timelines on the EPR Portal; the interim arrangement does not extend statutory reporting deadlines
sections.climate_angle: E-waste sits at the intersection of circular economy regulation and Scope 3 emissions management. Globally, frameworks like the ISSB's IFRS S2 and the EU's CSRD require companies to disclose resource-use intensity, end-of-life treatment of products, and value-chain emissions — all of which depend on credible recycling data. India's EPR credit mechanism is the primary evidence base producers will rely on for these disclosures. A weak or unverifiable credit trail creates two compounding risks: regulatory exposure under the E-Waste Rules and disclosure risk under emerging climate and sustainability reporting standards (including BRSR Core assurance). For multinationals, the interim arrangement is also a supply-chain integrity issue — buyers in the EU and Japan increasingly request documentary proof of responsible recycling for imported electronics, and CPCB-issued credits are becoming the de facto Indian benchmark.
what_to_do: Recyclers: Map every step of your credit-generation workflow against CPCB's interim procedure and close documentation gaps within the next 30 days
Producers: Reconcile EPR credits already purchased in FY24–25 with underlying recycler records; flag any credits without verifiable processing evidence
Set up a vendor due-diligence checklist for recycler partners — registration status, processing capacity, downstream material flow, and CPCB inspection history
Integrate EPR credit data into your BRSR and Scope 3 disclosure workflows so the same evidence supports both regulatory and investor reporting
Brief your board's risk or ESG committee on potential retrospective cancellation risk and quantify financial exposure if credits are invalidated
sections.our_take: This interim notice is a signal, not just a workaround. CPCB is making clear that the EPR credit market will continue functioning — but on its terms, with tighter traceability and a real possibility of retrospective scrutiny. We expect the next phase to bring full digital generation of certificates with embedded verification, more aggressive audits of recyclers, and possibly a price floor or trading band similar to the plastic waste EPR regime. Companies treating EPR as a procurement exercise — buying the cheapest available credits — will be most exposed. Those that build recycler partnerships with operational transparency and link EPR data into their broader climate disclosures will be best positioned, both for compliance and for the assurance requirements coming with BRSR Core and ISSB-aligned reporting.

posts.title: CPCB Interim Arrangement for Tyre Waste EPR Credits: What Recyclers and Producers Must Know
posts.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an interim arrangement governing how registered tyre waste recyclers generate and transfer Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) end product credits — known as EPR certificates — on the centralised online portal. The move is designed to bridge o

sections.summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an interim arrangement governing how registered tyre waste recyclers generate and transfer Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) end product credits — known as EPR certificates — on the centralised online portal. The move is designed to bridge operational gaps in the EPR regime for waste tyres notified under the Hazardous and Other Wastes Rules, 2022, and unblock credit transactions between recyclers, producers, and importers of new tyres.
sections.what_changed: Under India's EPR framework for waste tyres, producers (manufacturers and importers of new tyres) must discharge their EPR obligations by purchasing certificates from registered recyclers who process end-of-life tyres into approved end products such as reclaimed rubber, crumb rubber, char, and recovered oil. The CPCB's EPR portal is the single source of truth for issuing and trading these certificates. Because the portal's full automated functionality is still being stabilised, CPCB has put in place an interim arrangement that allows recyclers to generate EPR end product credits manually-validated or through a simplified workflow, and to transfer those credits to obligated producers. This avoids a compliance freeze while the system is upgraded, and ensures that recyclers continue to receive revenue for processed volumes and producers can fulfil their annual obligations without penalty.
who_is_affected: Registered tyre waste recyclers on the CPCB EPR portal who convert waste tyres into approved end products
Producers of new tyres — including domestic manufacturers across all tyre categories (two-wheeler, passenger, truck/bus, off-the-road, etc.)
Importers of new tyres and of tyres fitted on imported vehicles, who carry an equivalent EPR obligation
Retreaders registered under the EPR system
Auto OEMs and fleet operators indirectly exposed through supply chain compliance and procurement clauses
SPCBs/PCCs who monitor recyclers and producers within their jurisdiction
key_obligations: Recyclers must generate EPR end product credits only against verified quantities of waste tyres processed into approved end products, with supporting documentation (weighbridge slips, processing logs, end product dispatch records)
Credits must be generated and transferred on the CPCB EPR portal following the interim workflow prescribed in the notice — offline or parallel trades outside the portal will not be recognised
Producers must purchase and accept the transfer of certificates against their annual EPR target for the relevant compliance year
Recyclers should maintain a full audit trail (purchase of waste tyres, processing, end product sale, credit issuance) ready for CPCB inspection or third-party verification
Any shortfall by producers at year-end will attract environmental compensation as per the Hazardous Waste Rules and CPCB's EC guidelines
Companies should monitor the CPCB portal and subsequent CPCB notices for the date on which the interim arrangement will be replaced by the fully automated system
sections.climate_angle: Waste tyre EPR sits at the intersection of circular economy policy and climate risk. End-of-life tyres are a high-volume, hazardous waste stream — and uncontrolled disposal (open burning, dumping, informal pyrolysis) creates significant Scope 3 emissions, black carbon, and local air-quality liabilities for the entire automotive value chain. A functioning EPR credit market incentivises material recovery, displaces virgin rubber and carbon black, and reduces lifecycle GHG intensity of tyres — a metric that global frameworks like ISSB (IFRS S2), CSRD/ESRS E5 (Resource Use and Circular Economy), and CDP increasingly require companies to disclose. Indian tyre producers and auto OEMs supplying European or listed multinational customers should expect EPR compliance evidence, recycler traceability, and recycled content data to be requested as part of supplier ESG assessments and CBAM-adjacent due diligence.
what_to_do: Reconcile your CPCB EPR portal account immediately — confirm registration status, target quantities, and any pending credit transactions stuck in the system
For recyclers: tighten internal records linking waste tyre intake to end product output, so credits generated under the interim workflow are fully defensible in an audit
For producers: lock in credit purchase contracts early in the compliance year with reputable, traceable recyclers rather than chasing volumes at year-end when prices spike
Integrate EPR data flows into your ESG and BRSR reporting — quantity of waste managed, certificates procured, and recycled content should feed Principle 2 and Principle 6 disclosures
Set up a quarterly compliance review with your EHS, procurement, and finance teams to track EPR obligation status against the CPCB portal and flag any policy updates
sections.our_take: The interim arrangement is a pragmatic stopgap, but it signals a deeper truth: India's EPR ecosystem is still maturing, and operational glitches will keep surfacing across plastics, e-waste, batteries, and tyres until portal infrastructure, recycler verification, and credit pricing transparency catch up. Companies that treat EPR as a year-end procurement exercise will face volatile pricing, supply shortages, and reputational exposure. The smarter play is to build long-term partnerships with verified recyclers, embed EPR data into mainstream sustainability disclosures, and prepare for the next phase — where regulators are likely to introduce credit price floors, stricter recycler audits, and tighter linkages between EPR performance and BRSR or CBAM-style reporting. Get the plumbing right now; the scrutiny is only going to intensify.

posts.title: Food Systems Under the ESG Lens: What India's Agri-Business Boards Must Act On Before BRSR Core and TNFD Disclosures Converge
posts.summary: Food and agriculture sits at the exact intersection of nature risk, climate transition, and supply chain disclosure — three regulatory currents converging simultaneously on Indian listed companies. GreenBiz's sustained coverage of this sector (trellis.net/topic/food-agriculture/) reflects a global e

sections.summary: Food and agriculture sits at the exact intersection of nature risk, climate transition, and supply chain disclosure — three regulatory currents converging simultaneously on Indian listed companies. GreenBiz's sustained coverage of this sector (trellis.net/topic/food-agriculture/) reflects a global editorial consensus that agriculture is no longer a peripheral ESG theme but a core financial materiality issue, driven by TNFD v1.0 adoption, SBTi's FLAG (Forests, Land and Agriculture) guidance, and the EU's incoming supply chain due diligence requirements under CSDDD. For Indian CFOs and CSOs at FMCG, agri-processing, dairy, sugar, and food-export companies, the regulatory and capital market exposure is now direct, deadline-bound, and board-level. The single most important insight: Indian food and agri-businesses that have treated nature and land-use as a non-financial narrative will face a hard reckoning as SEBI's BRSR Core mandatory disclosures for top-150 listed companies (FY2024-25 onward) explicitly require biodiversity impact disclosure, and global buyers and institutional investors are now operationalising TNFD-aligned due diligence in their own procurement and portfolio risk frameworks.
sections.what_changed: Reporting from GreenBiz (trellis.net/topic/food-agriculture/) tracks a decisive shift in how food and agriculture companies are being evaluated — not just on carbon, but on water stress, soil degradation, deforestation linkages, and biodiversity loss. This is no longer voluntary thought leadership territory. Three structural changes are now in force globally that directly implicate Indian agri-business: First, the TNFD v1.0 framework (released September 2023) provides a full disclosure architecture for nature-related financial risks, and over 400 companies globally have already committed to TNFD-aligned reporting. Indian companies with international institutional shareholders or cross-listed instruments are already in scope via investor expectation, even before domestic regulation mandates it. Second, SBTi's FLAG guidance requires companies in land-intensive sectors — including food processing, sugar, palm oil, dairy, and aquaculture — to set separate science-based targets for land-use emissions, which are distinct from and additional to Scope 1/2/3 energy targets. Companies pursuing SBTi validation in India without a FLAG component will face target rejection or incomplete status. Third, the EU Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD) and the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR), which came into force in June 2023, now require any company placing cattle, soy, palm oil, coffee, cocoa, wood, or rubber on the EU market to demonstrate these commodities are not sourced from deforested land post-December 2020. Indian exporters of coffee (Karnataka, Kerala), spices, rice, and processed foods face direct export eligibility risk if supply chain traceability to farm level cannot be demonstrated. On the India domestic side, SEBI's BRSR Core framework — mandatory for the top 150 listed entities from FY2024-25 — includes Key Performance Indicators on water consumption intensity, biodiversity impact areas, and Scope 3 disclosure. For food and agriculture companies, these KPIs are operationally the most complex to populate, requiring data from contract farmers, commodity aggregators, and rural supply chains that have historically operated outside any ESG reporting architecture. The convergence of these frameworks is not coincidental — it reflects the scientific consensus from the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF, December 2022), which set a binding target to protect 30% of land and oceans by 2030 (30x30), and to require businesses to assess and disclose biodiversity dependencies and impacts by 2030. India is a signatory. MoEFCC has committed to aligning national biodiversity targets with the GBF, which means domestic regulatory follow-through on biodiversity disclosure — including for agri-businesses — is a matter of when, not if.
who_is_affected: FMCG and food processing companies listed on BSE/NSE — especially those in the top 1000 by market capitalisation who will face progressive BRSR Core requirements by FY2026-27
Sugar mills, rice processors, edible oil refiners (palm, soy, sunflower), and dairy cooperatives with direct land-use and water-intensive supply chains
Agri-commodity exporters with EU market exposure — coffee, spices, basmati rice, processed foods — who face EUDR traceability compliance from December 2024 onward
CFOs at listed agri-businesses who must now integrate land-use and water risk into financial materiality assessments and annual report disclosures
Chief Sustainability Officers responsible for BRSR Core KPI population, SBTi FLAG target-setting, and TNFD-aligned nature risk disclosure
Procurement and supply chain heads who must build farm-level traceability systems to satisfy both EU market access requirements and Scope 3 Category 1 (purchased goods) GHG accounting

ESG-focused institutional investors — domestic mutual funds under SEBI's stewardship code obligations, FPIs with SFDR Article 8/9 mandates — who must assess Principal Adverse Impacts on biodiversity in their Indian portfolio companies
Green and sustainability-linked lenders who are beginning to include TNFD alignment and deforestation-free sourcing as covenant conditions in structured finance for agri-sector borrowers
key_obligations: SEBI BRSR Core — Mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2024-25 (reports due mid-2025): includes KPIs on water consumption, biodiversity-sensitive area operations, and Scope 3 disclosure; food and agri-sector companies must begin data collection from FY2024 supply chains immediately
SEBI BRSR Core — Expanding to top 250 companies from FY2025-26 and top 500 from FY2026-27: companies outside the top 150 must begin gap assessments and data infrastructure build now, not reactively

EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) — Large company compliance deadline: December 30, 2024 (delayed from June 2024 by EU Commission; Indian exporters of in-scope commodities must have due diligence systems operational by this date)

SBTi FLAG Guidance — Any Indian company submitting or renewing SBTi targets in land-intensive sectors must include FLAG-compliant land-use emission targets; SBTi has indicated non-compliant submissions in covered sectors will not receive validation from 2025 onward

TNFD v1.0 — No mandatory Indian deadline yet, but companies committing to TNFD-aligned disclosure are expected to begin reporting in the first cycle within 2 years of commitment; investor pressure is accelerating this timeline for BSE 500 companies with significant foreign institutional ownership

Kunming-Montreal GBF 30x30 — India's national biodiversity strategy and action plan (NBSAP) update (due to CBD by end-2024) will translate into domestic policy; agri-businesses operating in biodiversity-sensitive landscapes (Western Ghats, Northeast, river deltas) should anticipate enhanced environmental impact assessment requirements from MoEFCC and state PCBs

CSDDD (EU Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive) — Indian suppliers to large EU companies will face contractual due diligence requirements from their EU buyers from 2026-2027; supplier ESG data provision obligations will flow down value chains to Indian agri-processors and exporters
sections.climate_angle: Agriculture accounts for approximately 14% of India's total GHG emissions per official inventory data, with rice cultivation (methane from flooded paddies), enteric fermentation in livestock, and synthetic fertiliser use (nitrous oxide) as the dominant sources. These are Scope 1 emissions for farming entities and Scope 3 Category 1 emissions for every food company that purchases these commodities. The ISSB's IFRS S2 standard — which SEBI is expected to align BRSR with progressively — requires disclosure of all material climate-related risks and opportunities, and for food companies, agricultural supply chain emissions represent by far the largest share of their full carbon footprint. A company like an Indian FMCG giant sourcing wheat, rice, sugar, and dairy cannot present a credible net zero pathway without a land-use emissions reduction strategy — yet most Indian food companies' published climate targets address only their manufacturing Scope 1 and 2 emissions, leaving the vast majority of their climate impact unaddressed and therefore undisclosed. This is the gap that SBTi FLAG, TNFD, and BRSR Core are simultaneously closing. The transition pathway for Indian food companies must now include: regenerative agriculture programmes with contract farmers, methane-reducing rice cultivation practices (alternate wetting and drying), fertiliser efficiency improvements aligned with BEE's Perform Achieve Trade scheme for fertiliser sector units, and supply chain deforestation elimination — all of which must be measurable, third-party verifiable, and disclosed under the frameworks named above. Companies that build this capability will be positioned for preferential access to green and transition finance; those that do not will face stranded cost risks as carbon pricing under India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) matures and as EU carbon border adjustment mechanisms potentially extend to processed agricultural goods in later phases.
what_to_do: CFO / Finance team — Within 90 days: commission a financial materiality assessment of nature and land-use risks using TNFD's LEAP (Locate, Evaluate, Assess, Prepare) methodology, focusing on water dependency, soil health, and deforestation exposure in top-5 commodity supply chains; this feeds directly into BRSR Core KPI population and IFRS S2-aligned risk disclosure

CSO / Sustainability team — Immediately: conduct a SBTi FLAG eligibility screening; if your company operates in covered sectors (food, beverage, tobacco, agriculture), initiate FLAG target-setting process in parallel with any existing SBTi commitment to avoid submission rejection in 2025

Supply chain / Procurement — Before December 2024: for any commodity exported to or sourced from supply chains serving the EU, implement a farm-level geolocation and traceability system sufficient to meet EUDR due diligence requirements; engage commodity aggregators and FPOs (Farmer Producer Organisations) on data collection protocols now

Board Risk Committee — At next quarterly meeting: add nature and land-use risk to the enterprise risk register with explicit linkage to TNFD v1.0 risk categories; request management to present a TNFD readiness assessment and timeline to first disclosure

Investor Relations — Within 60 days: proactively brief top FPI holders and domestic institutional investors on your company's BRSR Core biodiversity KPI status, TNFD roadmap, and EUDR compliance posture; investors are already asking these questions in AGM season and in CDP response reviews

Legal / Compliance — Audit all existing supplier contracts for agri-commodity procurement to assess CSDDD and EUDR due diligence gap; begin inserting ESG data-sharing clauses and deforestation-free sourcing warranties into new or renewed supplier agreements
sections.our_take: The food and agriculture sector is about to experience what the energy sector experienced between 2018 and 2022: a rapid, non-linear escalation from voluntary ESG aspiration to mandatory, auditable, capital-market-consequential disclosure. The trajectory is clear. Within 36 months, Indian listed food companies will face a tripartite disclosure obligation — BRSR Core for domestic capital market compliance, TNFD-aligned nature risk disclosure for foreign institutional capital access, and EUDR/CSDDD-driven supply chain transparency for export market eligibility. These are not separate programmes; they require the same underlying data infrastructure — farm-level traceability, supplier GHG data, water and land-use metrics — and companies that treat them as three separate compliance projects will waste capital and fall behind. The smarter boards will recognise this as a single integrated data and governance challenge and invest accordingly. We expect SEBI to formally reference TNFD in its next BRSR revision cycle (likely FY2025-26 consultation), and MoEFCC to propose mandatory biodiversity impact disclosure for EIA-category B projects in ecologically sensitive zones within the same window, effectively closing the policy gap that currently allows agri-businesses to operate in biodiversity-critical landscapes without systematic nature risk reporting. The capital market consequence is already visible: ESG-screened FPI capital and sustainability-linked loans are beginning to price nature risk alongside carbon risk. Indian food companies with opaque supply chains, no SBTi FLAG targets, and BRSR biodiversity KPIs populated with zeros or 'not applicable' will face investor engagement escalation, proxy advisor flags at AGMs, and ultimately, a cost-of-capital differential versus peers who have built this capability. The window to build proactively — rather than reactively — is 12 to 18 months. Boards should act as if the regulation is already in force, because for your foreign investors and export buyers, it already is.

posts.title: Candour as Capital: Why Honest Sustainability Reporting Drives Shareholder Value — and What Indian CFOs Must Learn Before BRSR Core Enforcement Arrives
posts.summary: New research published by GreenBiz (trellis.net) confirms what sophisticated ESG investors have long suspected: companies that disclose sustainability shortcomings with specificity and accountability generate measurable share price outperformance, while glossy, kitchen-sink sustainability reports ac

sections.summary: New research published by GreenBiz (trellis.net) confirms what sophisticated ESG investors have long suspected: companies that disclose sustainability shortcomings with specificity and accountability generate measurable share price outperformance, while glossy, kitchen-sink sustainability reports actively erode institutional investor confidence. For Indian listed companies navigating mandatory BRSR Core disclosures and incoming third-party assurance requirements from SEBI, this is not a communications lesson — it is a board-level risk and capital market strategy imperative. The era of defensive, hedged, and aspirationally worded ESG reporting is closing fast, and companies still producing it face a compounding penalty: regulatory non-compliance exposure, investor trust deficits, and exclusion from ESG-indexed capital flows simultaneously.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by GreenBiz (https://trellis.net/article/how-candor-sustainability-reporting-benefits-companies-stakeholders/) authored by Jeff Senne and Steve Rochlin on May 29, 2026, presents findings from Project ROI research demonstrating that institutional investors — including a major SRI fund CEO with billions in AUM — explicitly reject 'glossy' sustainability reports. The research identifies three companies — Williams Companies, Plains All American Pipeline, and Microsoft — that disclosed missed targets candidly, explained root causes, and outlined corrective plans.
